create_cell_signal_em_model [-help] [-cell cell_name ] -dir directory_name [-def filename ] [-def_options { list_of_options }] [-include_def] [-lef list_of_LEF_files ] [-log_dir directory_name ] [-overwrite | -reuse_existing ] [-power_net_voltages voltage ] [-sdc list_of_SDC_files ] [-spef list_of_SPEF_files ] [-spef_options { list_of_options }] [-tech_file filename ] [-tech_file_map mapping_file ] [-use_verilog] [-verilog list_of_Verilog_files ]
Specifies to create the cell boundary model for the Top Scope Signal EM analysis flow. After the boundary model is generated, it is automatically loaded to the higher-level module.
You can specify the create_cell_signal_em_model command before or after running the set_top_module command. The details of the two use models are given below:

- If the create_cell_signal_em_model command is specified before set_top_module , the command will generate the boundary model for a sub-cell and load it. For this use model, the -cell , -dir , -spef , and -verilog parameters must be specified.
- If the create_cell_signal_em_model command is specified after set_top_module , the command will generate the model for the top cell for use in a subsequent top-level run. In this case, the command is issued in a block-level run, and the SPEF should be loaded prior to the create_cell_signal_em_model command. For this use model, the -dir parameter must be specified. Since the design is already loaded, the relevant information is obtained from the current session.

data:
Outputs a brief description that includes the type and default information for each create_cell_signal_em_model parameter. 
 For a detailed description of the command and all of its parameters, use the man command man create_cell_signal_em_model .

Specifies the name of the cell that is being modeled. The -cell parameter takes a single cell name. If the -cell parameter is used with the command after specifying the set_top_module command, the cell name must be the top cell name.

Specifies to read the list of DEF files during the boundary model generation session so that the physical information is present when generating the model. When this parameter is specified, the Tech LEF file must be specified with the -lef parameter. 
 Note : The -def and -include_def options must be used together. These parameters are specified if you want the boundary model to include a DEF that corresponds to the boundary model logic. When the boundary model is loaded into the top-level session, this DEF will override any user-supplied DEF that was specified to the read_def command for the model block. If you do not specify a DEF for the boundary model block, then the boundary model DEF will be added to the list of DEFs loaded by read_def .

Specifies additional options that are to be used with the read_def command run in the boundary model generation session. The options specified with the -def_options parameter are appended to the read_def command. The -def_options parameter must be specified with the -def parameter. 
 For example, if you want to preserve the wire segments in the user-supplied DEF that was specified with the read_def command for the model block, use the following command: create_cell_signal_em_model … -def files -def_options {-preserve_shape}
Reads the list of specified DEF files during the boundary model generation session so that the boundary model physical information is available.

Specifies to generate the boundary DEF file for the netlist in the model. When specified, the boundary model directory will contain a subset of the input DEF for just the parts of the netlist that are included in the model netlist. 
 Note : The -def and -include_def options must be used together. These parameters are specified if you want the boundary model to include a subset DEF that corresponds to the boundary model logic. When the boundary model is loaded into the top-level session, the subset DEF will override any user-supplied DEF which was specified to the read_def command for the model block. If you do not specify a subset DEF for the boundary model block, then the boundary model DEF will be added to the list of DEFs loaded by read_def .
Specifies the list of LEF files to be loaded. A technology LEF with the layer definition is required if layer information is to be preserved in the SPEF generated for the model.

Specifies the directory where the log files for the boundary model generation run will be saved. If the directory does not exist, a new directory will be created. This directory contains the voltus_sigem.log , voltus_sigem.logv , and voltus_sigem.cmd files.
Allows overwrite of the existing directory.

Specifies the voltage values for the power nets. The format of the -power_net_voltages argument is a list of net names and voltage values with the @ character separating the power net name from the voltage. Example: {VDD1@1.0 VDD2@0.9}
Specifies to load an existing boundary model directory.
Specifies the list of SDC files to be loaded. If SDC files are specified, then the fanin to the clock definition pins will be included in the generated model.
Specifies the list of SPEF files to be loaded.
Specifies the extraction technology file to be used with the RC corner. The technology file is required if layer information is to be preserved in the SPEF generated for the model.

Specifies the mapping file that includes the mapping between the SPEF layers to the LEF layers. The mapping file is required if layer information is to be preserved in the SPEF generated for the model. 
 The format of the mapping file is: 
 # <LEF/DEF Layer Name> #<SPEF Layer Name> M1 METAL_1 \ VIA1 VIA_1 \ M2 METAL_2 \

Specifies to generate boundary models that use Verilog for the model netlist. When this parameter is not specified, the models are generated using a non-readable binary database. The -use_verilog parameter can be specified if you want to debug the models and prefer a readable format for the model netlist.
Specifies the list of Verilog files to be loaded.

lines:
- The following is the command for creating a boundary model and loading it into the current session: create_cell_signal_em_model -dir bm_dir -cell cella -verilog cella.v -spef cella.spef.gz -overwrite -lef tech.lef allcells.lef -tech_file 65lp_typical.tch -tech_file_map leflayermap
- The following is the command for creating a boundary model after running set_top_module in a block-level session: create_cell_signal_em_model -dir bm_dir -overwrite T
- The following command can be used prior to init_design (or set_top_module ) to import physical design data: create_cell_signal_em_model -dir bm_dir -overwrite -verilog block.v -spef block.spef -def block.def -include_def Note : It is not possible to import physical design data after init_design (or set_top_module ) as the data has already been loaded. A warning will be issued but will not prevent boundary model generation.
- The following command generates a boundary model, and saves the log files produced by the boundary model generation sub-process into a different directory: create_cell_signal_em_model -dir bm_dir -overwrite -verilog block.v -spef block.spef -log_dir ./bm_logs
- "Signal ElectroMigration" in the Voltus User Guide
create_top_scope [-help] [-cell_list list ] [-fanout_limit value ] [-ignore_nets netname ] [-include_pins pin_names ] [-include_side_loads] [-inst_list list ] [-summary] [-thread_count value ]

Specifies to to perform abstraction of the top-level hierarchical instances for analysis of only the top-level logic, resulting in faster turnaround time. The command allows you to perform abstraction of all the hierarchical instances in the top-level, or specify the top-level hierarchical instances that should be abstracted. When a hierarchical instance is abstracted, all the internal logic is ignored while retaining the interface paths.
The create_top_scope command must be specified before the read_sdc and set_top_module commands.

data:
Outputs a brief description that includes the type and default information for each create_top_scope parameter. 
 For a detailed description of the command and all of its parameters, use the man command man create_top_scope .
Specifies to include the interface paths of only the specified cells.
Specifies to unmark the boundary nets with fanout greater than the given limit. The default limit is 1000.
Skips unmarking the fanin/fanout of the specified boundary nets.
Specifies to include all side receivers in the scope.
Specifies to include the interface paths of only the specified instances.

Specifies a list of user-defined pins which should be included that may not be on the boundary paths in the Top Scope Signal EM Analysis flow. The top scope will include the full fanin cone to all the specified pins as part of the analysis.
 This parameter allows you to handle scenarios where there are some clock constraints defined on pins in sub-blocks which are not part of the boundary logic. In such a case, the constraint would fail and display an error message, and any constraints for clocks generated from the failing clock would fail as well. With this parameter, you can include all the root pins for the clock constraints as part of the top scope, thus resulting in no errors.
Specifies to print statistics about the included instances or nets.
Specifies the number of threads to be used.

lines: - The following command specifies the top-level hierarchical instances that should be abstracted: create_top_scope –summary –inst_list {InstanceName1, InstanceName2..}
Displays the following information about a set_signal_em_analysis_mode parameter in the system log file and in the system console:
- Whether the current value was set by user
If you do not specify a parameter, the software displays information for all of the set_signal_em_analysis_mode parameters.

data: Outputs a brief description that includes type and default information for each get_signal_em_analysis_mode parameter. For a detailed description of the command and all of its parameters, use the man command: man set_signal_em_analysis_mode .

Displays information for the specified parameters. You can specify one or more parameters. 
 See set_signal_em_analysis_mode for description of the parameters you can specify.

Displays the current settings for the specified parameters in Tcl list format only. 
 If you specify -quiet without any parameters, the software displays the current settings of all set_signal_em_analysis_mode parameters in the Tcl list format.

lines: The following command displays the current setting of the -use_db_freq parameter: get_signal_em_analysis_mode -use_db_freq The software displays the following information:
Reports the following design rule violations:
- Nets that exceed the maximum capacitance constraints in the timing library and timing constraints file.
- Nets that exceed the transition constraints in the timing constraints file.
- Pins that exceed the maximum fanout constraints in the timing library and timing constraints file.

data:
Specifies whether the violations are reported by driver, net or pin. 
 Default: Violations are reported by net.
Specifies the file that contains the hierarchical net names that are excluded from the capacitance violation report.
Reports only maximum capacitance violations, or only minimum capacitance violations. Default: -max
Specifies the name of the file to which the results are written.
Specifies the file that contains the hierarchical net names for the report. Only these net names are considered for inclusion in the capacitance violation report.

Specifies whether to report transition, capacitance, or fanout constraints violations. 
 Default: Reports all design rule violations.

lines: The following command reports the design rule violations in the report file maxcap.report:

report_freq_violation [-help] [-detailed] [-em_type {avg peak rms all}] [-excInstFile excInstFileName ] [-outfile fileName ] [-reportFormat integer ] [-selInstFile selInstFileName ] [-slew {min | max}] [-sort {name | freq | minDiff | maxDiff | minFreq | maxFreq}] [-tableScale float ] [-view viewName ]
Runs electromigration (EM) analysis based on the frequency table defined in the Liberty library, and generates a frequency violation report.

The report_freq_violation command uses default activity (1.0 or 100% activity on data network and 200% on clock network) or user-defined activity (TCF/VCD) to compute effective frequency on each instance pin. It then determines frequency violation at the output pin from the electromigration templates defined in Liberty, as shown in the following figure:
The frequency limit on the output pin is derived based on the slew on the related input pin and output load.
The activity for the design can be specified as follows:

data: Generates a detailed report containing information for all instances, including those without violations.

Specifies to report frequency violations for the rms/peak/avg current waveform in the frequency violation report. When this parameter is specified, the software reads the em_max_toggle_rate table in the Liberty library for the specified EM current type (rms/peak/avg). 
 You can specify all to perform all three types of analyses.
Specifies a file that lists the names of instances to exclude from the frequency violation report.

Outputs a brief description that includes type and default information for each report_freq_violaltion parameter. 
 For a detailed description of the command and all of its parameters, use the man command: man report_freq_violation .
Specifies the report file (default: freqViolations.rpt ) for the violation data.
Specifies a format of the violation report. The report details vary according to the selected report format. You can specify report formats 0, 1, or 2. Default : 0
Specifies a file that lists the names of instances to include in the frequency violation report.
Specifies the minimum or maximum slew value for the report. Default : min
-sort { name | freq | minDiff | maxDiff | minFreq | maxFreq }
Sorts the instance-based frequency data by name , freq , minDiff , maxDiff , minFreq , and maxFreq . By default, the violation report is not sorted.
Name of a pin or arc.
Frequency value of a pin or arc.
Minimum frequency value of a pin or arc as defined in the frequency table.
Maximum frequency value of a pin or arc as defined in the frequency table.
Difference between freq and minFreq of a pin or arc.
Difference between freq and minFreq of a pin or arc.

Specifies the scale factor for EM table value. 
 Default: 1.0
Specifies the view for which to generate the frequency violation report. Use this parameter when working with multi-mode multi-corner (MMMC) designs.

lines: The following command reports instances that violate frequency constraints and lists them in the freqViol.rpt report file:

The instance file is a text file that contains a list of instances. Each instance name appears in one line in the file. The comments begin with " # ". Leading or ending spaces in instance names are ignored.
The report formats are described below.
The following command generates a report providing details of minFreq , Freq , Tran , Cap , Remark , and Arc/Pin and directs the output to report file summaryViol.rpt :
minFreq Freq Tran Cap Remark Arc/Pin 5.985 1.009 0.0356 0.003103 tt0/data/r3/add_27/U1_1_6/Y
When reportFreqViolation -reportFormat is set to 1 , the report provides details as shown in the following example:
Status Remark freq minFreq maxFreq Arc/Pin [P] * 1.009 5.985 5.995 tt0/data/r3/add_27/U1_1_6/Y
A detailed report is generated when reportFreqViolation -reportFormat is set to 2 , as shown in the following example:
Status Remark freq minFreq maxFreq minTran maxTran minCap max_cap Arc/Pin [P] * 1.009 5.985 5.995 3.560e-02 3.790e-02 3.103e-03 3.103e-03 tt0/data/r3/add_27/U1_1_6/Y

data: Arc or pin status - F (fail), V (violation), P (pass), or ? (unknown).
Indicates whether the pin is a clock.
Working frequency of the arc or pin.
Minimum frequency value taken from the frequency table.
Maximum frequency value taken from the frequency table.
Name of the arc or pin.

lines: set_max_cap_per_freq -cap { cap1 cap2 …} [-force] -freq { freq1 freq2 …} [-lib libName ] -pins { cell pin }
Adds a maximum capacitance per frequency table to the existing max_capacitance statement in the timing library.

Within-cell, DC electromigration limits vary with varying frequency of transition. The software manages DC electromigration using a maximum capacitance limit based on switching frequency. This is driven by maxCapPerFreq data in the liberty. The violations can be reported using the report_design_rule command in Voltus. These constraints can be set for cell pins manually using this command.
Timing analysis uses this table to detect violations, and optDesign in Innovus can be used to repair violations.

data:
Specifies a list of capacitance values that must be greater than or equal to 0. You must specify the same number of -freq and -cap values. 
 The software derives the maximum capacitance from a linear interpolation (or extrapolation) for a given frequency value using two frequency values in the table. For interpolation, the software uses the closest value above the frequency, and the closest value below the frequency. For extrapolation, the software uses the two closest values to the frequency. 
 Type: Float, specified in units of pF

Computes the maximum capacitance from the interpolated -freq and -cap values only, and ignores any previously defined max_capacitance values for these pins in the library. 
 Default: Computes the maximum capacitance using the worst case (minimum value) between the library max_capacitance values and the interpolated -cap values.

Specifies a list of at least two frequency values that must be greater than or equal to 0. You must specify the same number of -freq and -cap values, and the frequency values must be specified in ascending order. 
 Type: Float, specified in units of Hz

Specifies the library name inside liberty. For example, library ( slowlvt ) inside slow.lib. The library name should be specified as slowlvt . 
 Modifies only the matching cell-pins in the specified library. 
 Default: Modifies the matching cell-pins in all libraries

Adds the new capacitance constraints to the specified set of cells and pins. You can specify output or inout pins. The asterisk ( * ) wildcard character can be used in a cell or pin name. If a table associated with the pin already exists, it is replaced with the new table. 
 For analysis and optimization, the software uses the most restrictive (the lowest capacitance) value from either the table or the .lib max_capacitance . If you also specify the -force parameter, the max_capacitance value is ignored. The final value can be further modified using .sdc set_max_capacitance statements.

lines: Assume all output pins of a library have a max_capacitance of 3.5 pF from the library file.

- The following command sets the frequency dependent max_capacitance to 3.0 pF at a frequency of 100 Mhz, and to 1.5 pF at a frequency of 200 Mhz, for all output or inout pins for all cells in the library named fast : set_max_cap_per_freq -lib fast -freq {100e6 200e6} -cap {3.0 1.5} -pins {* *} As a result, for a net at 300 Mhz, an and2 cell output pin has an extrapolated max_capacitance value of 0 pF (which means the cell cannot be used at that frequency). An extrapolation to 50 Mhz results in a max_capacitance value of 3.75 pF. However, the 3.75 pF value is greater than the existing .lib max_capacitance limit of 3.5 pF; therefore timing analysis uses the 3.5 pF value.

- The following command applies the same constraints to all libraries with cell names matching dff* that have a pin named Qbar : set_max_cap_per_freq -force -freq {100e6 200e6} -cap {3.0 1.5} -pins {dff* Qbar} The original .lib max_capacitance limit is ignored because the -force parameter is specified; therefore extrapolation to 50 Mhz results in a final max_capacitance constraint value of 3.75 pF.
set_max_tran_per_freq [-force] -freq { freq1 freq2 ...} [-lib libName ] -pins { cell pin } -tran { tran1 tran2 ...}
Adds a maximum transition per frequency table to the existing max_transition statement in the timing library.

Within-cell, DC electromigration limits vary with varying frequency of transition. The software manages DC electromigration using a maximum transition limit based on switching frequency. The violations can be reported using the report_design_rule command in Voltus. These constraints can be set for cell pins manually using this command.
Timing analysis uses this table to detect violations, and optDesign in Innovus can be used to repair violations.
Use this command any time after loading the timing library files (that is, after importing or restoring the design).

data:
Computes the maximum transition from the interpolated -freq and -tran values only and ignores any previously defined max_transition values for these pins in the library. 
 Default: Uses the worst case (minimum value) between the .lib max_transition values and the interpolated -tran values.

Specifies a list of at least two frequency values that must be greater than or equal to 0. You must specify the same number of -freq and -tran values, and the frequency values must be specified in ascending order. 
 Type: Float, specified in units of Hz

Specifies the library name inside liberty. For example, library ( slowlvt ) inside slow.lib. The library name should be specified as slowlvt . 
 Modifies only the matching cell-pins in the specified library. 
 Default: Modifies the matching cell-pins in all libraries.

Adds the new transition constraints to the specified set of cells and pins. You can specify input, output, or inout pins. The asterisk ( * ) wildcard character can be used in a cell or pin name. If a table associated with the pin already exists, it is replaced with the new table. 
 For analysis and optimization, the software uses the most restrictive (the lowest transition) value from either the table or the .lib max_transition . If you also specify the -force parameter, the max_transition value is ignored. The final value can be further modified using .sdc set_max_transition statements.

Specifies a list of transition values that must be greater than or equal to 0. You must specify the same number of -freq and -tran values. 
 The software derives the maximum transition limit from a linear interpolation (or extrapolation) for a given frequency value using two frequency values in the table. For interpolation, the software uses the closest value above the frequency, and the closest value below the frequency. For extrapolation, the software uses the two closest values to the frequency. 
 Type: Float, specified in units of ns

lines: Assume all output pins of a library have a max_transition value of 0.9 ns from the library file.

- The following command sets the frequency dependent max_transition to 1.0 ns at a frequency of 100 Mhz, and to 0.7 ns at a frequency of 200 Mhz, for pins matching in* of cells matching * (that is, all cells) in the library named slow : set_max_tran_per_freq -lib slow -freq {100e6 200e6} -tran {1.0 0.7} -pins {* in*} As a result, at 300 Mhz, an and2 cell input pin named in1 has an extrapolated max_transition value of 0.4ns. An extrapolation to 50 Mhz results in a max_transition value of 1.15 ns. However, the 1.15 ns value is greater than the existing .lib max_transition value of 0.9 ns; therefore timing analysis uses the 0.9 value.

- The following command applies the same constraints to all libraries with cell names matching dff* that have a pin named clk : set_max_tran_per_freq -force -freq {100e6 200e6} -tran {1.0 0.7} -pins {dff* clk} The original .lib max_transition value is ignored because the -force parameter is specified; therefore an extrapolation to 50 Mhz results in a final max_transition limit of 1.15 ns.

set_signal_em_analysis_mode [-help] [-additional_individual_violation_report {true | false}] [-avgRecovery em_recover ] [-check_thermal_aware_em {true | false}] [-current_scale_factor {{avg value } {rms value } {peak value }}] [-current_scale_table current_scale_table_file ] [-default_freq_for_unconstrained_nets freq_in_Hz ] [-delta_T value ] [–delta_T_layer_list {{< LEF_layer_name > < delta_T_in_C >}+}] [-effort_level {low | medium | high}] [-em_cdf_percentage value ] [-em_limit_scale_factor {{avg value } {rms value } {peak value }}] [-em_limit_scale_table em_limit_scale_table_file ] [-em_res_width {drawn silicon}] [-em_temperature value ] [-em_temperature_layer_list {{< LEF_layer_name > < em_temperature_in_C >}+}] [-em_threshold < value >] [-enable_seb {true | false}] [–env_temperature temperature ] [-error value ] [-extraction_tech_file filename ] [-forceHoldView] [-handle_pin_obs_via] [-ict_em_models file ] [-Ipeak_Td_method {effective_width_from_integration effective_half_peak_width max_equivalent_dc_peak sum_half_peak_width}] [-ircx_models {RC.ircx EMIR.ircx}|{RC.ircx}] [-lifetime value ] [-method {rms peak avg}] [-minPeakDutyRatio value ] [-minPeakFreq value ] [-net netNames | -selected ] [-net_file list_file ] [-net_freq_file filename ] [-read_detail_delta_temperature_file filename ] [-report filename [-detailed]] [-report_db] [-reset] [-seb_lifetime value ] [-seb_table table_filename ] [-seb_temperature temperature ] [-set_current_file filename ] [-simulation_net list_of_nets ] [-simulation_net_file filename ] [-skip_category_mode {0 | 1 | 2}] [-skip_net_file list_file ] [-skip_net net_name_list ] [-toggle value ] [-top_scope_ignore_block_internal_nets_on_boundary_path] [-useQrcTech] [-use_rms_delta_t {true | false}] [-view viewName ]

Sets global parameters for signal electromigration. Use this command to specify the required verify_AC_limit mode settings before running any commands that perform signal electromigration. The parameters specified with the set_signal_em_analysis_mode command are then used automatically whenever signal electromigration takes place.
Use the get_signal_em_analysis_mode command to return the current settings for the set_signal_em_analysis_mode command.
Note: Use the -method parameter to specify the waveform calculation method to be used ( rms , peak , or avg ).

data:
Outputs a brief description that includes type and default information for each set_signal_em_analysis_mode parameter. 
 For a detailed description of the command and all of its parameters, use the man command: man set_signal_em_analysis_mode

Specifies to generate separate violation reports for each current waveform calculation method ( rms , peak , or avg ). If this parameter is not specified, a single violation report file is generated using the -method {RMS AVG peak} parameter. This parameter gives you the flexibility of generating either a single collated report or individual reports for each analysis method.
 Example : 
 set_signal_em_analysis_mode \ -method {rms peak avg} \ -report context1.rpt \ ... -additional_individual_violation_report true 
 This command will generate 3 different reports, namely context1.rpt_avg , context1.rpt_peak , and context1.rpt_rms . If there are no EM violations for a method, the tool will not generate the corresponding separate report. In this example, if there are violations for avg and rms but none for peak , then it will generate only two reports, context1.rpt_avg and context1.rpt_rms. 
 Default : false

Specifies the recovery factor used for calculating I avg current density with recovery. By default, the recovery factor (per layer) is read from the QRC techfile. If you specify -avgRecovery em_recover , it overrides the QRC tech em_recover factor for all layers used in I avg limits. The minimum -avgRecovery value is -1 and the maximum is 1 . 
 Note: If you do not specify -avgRecovery and the em_recover factor is not specified in the QRC techfile as well, the tool gives an error that I avg cannot be computed.

Specifies to use the thermal map for EM checks. The thermal map file is specified using the -read_thermal_map parameter. The Rail Analysis engine uses the temperature specified in the thermal map file for EM analysis. EM limit has a strong temperature dependency. For a chip that has substantial temperature variation across the chip, the Rail Analysis engine can use the temperature specified in the thermal map file to perform accurate EM analysis. 
 Default : false

Scales the signal EM current by the respective factor for avg, rms, or peak current specified by {avg value } {rms value } {peak value } . 
 The value specified for this parameter must be > 0 and <= 10.

Specifies the name of the scale table file used for layer-based scaling of the signal EM current. The scale table contains the scale factor for avg, rms, or peak current for each layer. 
 This parameter is mutually exclusive with -current_scale_factor . The -current_scale_table parameter takes precedence over -current_scale_factor . 
 The format of the table is given below: 
 layer avg rms peak 
 layer1 scalefactor1 scalefactor2 scalefactor3 layer2 scalefactor1 scalefactor2 scalefactor3 
 Here, 
 layer name is the LEF layer name that could be a metal/via layer. avg, rms, peak correspond to the respective scale factor for avg, rms, or peak current. The scale factor value must be > 0 and <= 10.

Specifies the frequency for EM calculation when a net has no defined frequency or has a defined frequency of 0 Hz in the design. 
 Default : 1MHz

Specifies the maximum temperature rise permitted in units of Celsius. This value is normally used in the QRC tech file for the RMS current limit equation. For example: 
 em_jmax_ac_rms EQU sqrt(12.66 * delta_T * (w - 0.003)^2 * (w - 0.003 + 0.264) / (w - 0.003 + 0.0443)) 
 Default : 5
Specifies the delta temperature for specific LEF layers during signal EM RMS current limit analysis.
Generates a detailed report containing information for all signal nets, including those without violations.

Specifies the method used for current calculation of nets in the Signal ElectroMigration analysis flow. The two methods are: non-simulation mode - use the capacitance ratio to distribute the current down the stream from the driver to the receiver. simulation mode - simulates resistance and capacitance to get the true current on each segment. This mode is more accurate but requires a longer run time. 
 This parameter has the following three arguments: 
 low : enables the non-simulation mode for all nets where the current distribution is based on the capacitance ratio. medium : enables the simulation mode for the nets with physical loops, and the non-simulation mode for the non-loop nets. high : enables the simulation mode for all nets. 
 The detailed report ( set_signal_em_analysis_mode -report < filename > -detailed ) shows “ N/A ” in the Cap column for nets in the simulation mode. 
 The default value for -effort_level is low . The medium and high levels require a VTS-AA license. 
 Note : For the simulation mode with effort level medium or high : 
 If you use extract_rc , run set_extract_rc_mode -qrcCmdType partial -qrcCmdFile qrc.user.cmd , where qrc.user.cmd must include the following CCL commands: extraction_setup -analysis em -cluster_vias_in_em true output_db -type spef -subtype "EXTENDED" 
 Refer to the example below: output_db \ -type spef -busbit_delimiter "[]" -escape_special_character false \ -hierarchy_delimiter "/" -output_incomplete_nets true \ -output_unrouted_nets true -pin_delimiter ":" -subtype "EXTENDED" \ -disable_subnodes false -add_explicit_vias true \ -include_parasitic_res_width true -include_parasitic_res_width_drawn true 
 
 If you run standalone Quantus to generate the required SPEF file in the extended format separately, then instead of extract_rc , use the following command for signal EM analysis: read_spef -starN -extended spef_file

Calculates the Cumulative Distribution Factor (CDF) derating factor based on the EM rules specified in the ICT EM file, given by the -ict_em_models parameter. This parameter is used to calculate the fail rate and reliability of a design. The value of the CDF percentage should be between 0 and 100. The default value is 1e-9 . 
 The use model of this parameter is: 
 set_signal_em_analysis_mode \ -method {rms peak avg} \ ... -em_cdf_percentage 5 
 When the CDF related rule exits in the ICT EM file, the software uses the default value 1e-09 for -em_cdf_percentage if it is not specified.

Scales the calculated signal EM limits by the respective factor for avg, rms or peak current limit specified by {avg value } {rms value } {peak value } . The software compares the current through the net against the scaled EM limit to report violations in signal EM. 
 The value specified for this parameter must be > 0 and <= 10.

Specifies the name of the scale table file used for layer-based scaling of the signal EM limit. The scale table contains the scale factor for avg, rms, or peak current for each layer. This parameter is mutually exclusive with -em_limit_scale_factor . The -em_limit_scale_table parameter takes precedence over -em_limit_scale_factor . 
 The format of the table is given below: 
 layer avg rms peak 
 layer1 scalefactor1 scalefactor2 scalefactor3 layer2 scalefactor1 scalefactor2 scalefactor3 
 Here, 
 layer name is the LEF layer name that could be a metal/via layer. avg, rms, peak correspond to the respective scale factor for avg, rms, or peak current. The scale factor value must be > 0 and <= 10.

Specifies width used to get EM limit. The legal values are: 
 drawn silicon 
 Default : drawn
Specifies the temperature expected for the device and is used to look up the temperature scaling factor. By default, no scaling is done. The value is a float, in units of degrees Celsius.
Sets a different temperature for specific LEF layers during signal EM AVG current limit analysis.
Specifies to report violations in signal EM above the threshold value. The value is the ratio of signal current (avg/peak/rms) to the respective EM limit. Default is 1.0 .

Specifies to enable the Statistical ElectroMigration Budgeting (SEB) analysis. 
 Default : false

Specifies the ambient temperature used to calculate S DC for SEB analysis. 
 Default : 25

Controls the number of errors to be included in the signal EM violation report file . If you do not specify this parameter, the top 10000 signal nets with violation will be included in the violation report. 
 This parameter can be used to control the size of the violation report file. 
 Default: 10000

Specifies an extraction technology file for the Signal ElectroMigration analysis flow. If this parameter is not specified, then by default the extraction technology file will be taken from the analysis view (current RC corner).
 
 This parameter must be used when the technology file is not specified through the analysis view ( create_rc_corner - qx_tech_file ). If you specify both create_rc_corner - qx_tech_file and set_signal_em_analysis_mode -extraction_tech_file , then the technology file specified with the -extraction_tech_file parameter will take precedence. In addition, if a technology file is not specified using either of these two command parameters, the software displays a warning message for the missing technology file, and will not read the EM models specified through the -ict_em_models parameter.
Forces set_signal_em_analysis_mode to use the hold view for current calculation even during setup fixing.

Specifies to consider the vias defined in the OBS (obstructions/blockages) section of LEF in the Signal ElectroMigration flow. By default, the software does not consider via in obstruction. 
 This parameter does not honor the via obstructions with " SPACING 0 ".

Specifies the ICT-EM file which only contains the EM rules and excludes the RC extraction data in ICT. The software will use the EM rules in the specified ICT-EM file to calculate EM limits for signal net EM analysis even if there are embedded EM rules in the Quantus QRC tech file.

Specifies the Td calculation method for Ipeak check. The legal values are depicted below. 
 
 effective_width_from_integration 
 
 
 
 effective_half_peak_width 
 
 
 
 
 
 max_equivalent_dc_peak 
 
 
 
 
 
 sum_half_peak_width 
 
 
 
 Default : effective_width_from_integration

Specifies an encrypted or unencrypted RC/EM technology (.ircx) file. The foundry-specific IRCX file, which contains the process information for extraction and EM modeling, can be used when the ICT-EM file is not available for electromigration analysis. 
 You can either specify two IRCX files, one file containing the RC process data ( RC.ircx ) and the other file containing EM rules ( EM.ircx ), or a single IRCX file that includes both the RC and EM sections ( RC.ircx ). A single IRCX file is used for the mature nodes, and separate RC and EM IRCX files are used for the advanced process nodes.

Specifies the total hours of operation expected for the device, and is used to look up the lifetime scaling factor. value is used to find the scaling factor by interpolating from a piece-wise-linear table in the LEF (see ACCURRENTDENSITY HOURSPWL table) or the QRC tech file ( em_jmax_ac model with jmax_lifetime table). If there is no scaling factor table, no scaling is done. value is a float, in units of hours.

Specifies the current waveform calculation method. You can specify one or more of the following options: 
 avg : Checks I avg limits and reports any average current limit violations in the design. peak : Checks I peak limits and reports any peak current limit violations in the design. rms : Checks I rms limits and reports any RMS current limit violations in the design. 
 Default : rms 
 Notes 
 If you specify peak or avg but AAE is not enabled, the tool displays an error message. 
 When you run set_signal_em_analysis_mode -method rms, the command uses AC limit rules in LEF even if the EM qrcTechfile is provided. Use set_signal_em_analysis_mode -method rms -useQrcTech to check rms EM limits from qrcTechfile.

Sets the minimum duty ratio value for I peak calculation. If the calculated duty ratio of a net is lower than the specified value, the specified value will be used to calculate I peak . The minimum value for duty_ratio is 0.0 and the maximum is 1.0 . 
 Default: 0.05 
 The following determines the priority and precedence of the -minPeakDutyRatio parameter: 
 When there are explicit duty ratio (r) dependencies on EM rules, the user-specified minPeakDutyRatio value will overwrite the software-calculated duty ratio (r), if the calculated duty ratio is smaller than the user-specified minPeakDutyRatio value. The minPeakDutyRatio value will then be applied to the EM rule selection to find the corresponding EM rule with duty ratio (r) dependency for EM limit calculation. When there are no explicit duty ratio (r) dependencies on EM rules, the user-specified minPeakDutyRatio value will overwrite the tool-calculated duty ratio (r), if the calculated duty ratio is smaller than the user-specified minPeakDutyRatio value, and the software will calculate the EM limit based on the user-specified minPeakDutyRatio . If you do not specify a value to minPeakDutyRatio , the default value of 0.05 will be applied.

Ignores I peak current limit calculation for signal nets that have an effective frequency below the specified value (in Hertz). 
 Default : 1e+06 Hz (1 MHz)

Specifies whether to check named or selected nets. If you name only one net, you do not need the braces around the net name. If you specify more than one net, you must separate the net names with a space. For example, set_signal_em_analysis_mode -nets {clk0 clk1} . 
 The -selected parameter analyzes the net specified with the selectNet { netName } | [command] command, and/or by selecting a net in the main GUI window. The following are examples of this parameter: 
 Example 1 : selectNet {netA} set_signal_em_analysis_mode -selected 
 Example 2 : selectNet [get_ccopt_clock_tree_nets] set_signal_em_analysis_mode -selected 
 Default: If you do not specify -nets or -selected , set_signal_em_analysis_mode verifies all nets.

Specifies the net from file to check the signalEM analysis. The format is one net name per line, with comments using # as the first character. For example: 
 #list of nets to skip net_a net_b net_c

Specifies a file containing the user-specified frequency for a list of nets. This parameter allows you to overwrite the frequency for specific nets if the frequency set by -toggle needs to be modified. 
 The format of the file is: <net name> <freq in Hz> 
 Example : IN_1 1000000000 n_I2_1 2700000000 OUT_1 1200000000
Specifies to read the detailed delta temperature file generated by the analyze_self_heat command (self-heat analysis).

Specifies the report file for the violation data. The report file includes name of the net, I rms , interpolated ACCURRENTDENSITY limit (I limit ), layer, width, X and Y location, rise and fall time, effective frequency for the net (F eff ), Cnet and Vdd for each net.

Specifies to write the signal EM data into a database file ( filename.db ) after performing signal EM analysis. This file contains the information required for loading and displaying signal EM data in GUI.
 filename is the name specified using the -report parameter.
Resets all the specified parameters back to the default values.

Specifies the life time of product in hours. 
 Default : 43800 hours (that is, 5 years)

Specifies the file name of the Failures in Time (FIT) table. This file is provided by the foundry. The file has the following columns: 
 Layer MTF Sigma Activate energy Current density exponent

Specifies the temperature used to update the median time to failure (MTF) with respect to the ambient temperature. 
 If this temperature is specified, it would be used globally for all the resistors. If this temperature is not specified, the seb_temperature may take the following possible values depending on the specific circumstances: If detailed delta temperature file is not used for SEB, the seb_temperature always takes the same value as that used for EM analysis. If detailed delta temperature file is used for SEB, the seb_temperature is calculated by the following equation: seb_temperature = env_temperature + delta_T Where delta_T is the maximum temperature rise, including joule heat and coupling heat from FEOL, in the resistor’s bounding box with zero extension.

Specifies the current of a macro's output pins for EM calculation, in case the macro does not have the respective timing library. When the macro output pin current is set by the current file, the value calculated (if any) from AAE is overwritten. 
 The format of the current file is: 
 {CELL cell_name |INSTANCE instance_name } PIN pin_name RMS rms_current PEAK peak _current AVG avg _current Td <value> NetFreq <value> 
 The unit of Td is Nanosecond and NetFreq is GHz. 
 Following are few examples of the current file format: 
 CELL cell_macro_A PIN Q1 RMS 0.01 PEAK 0.1 AVG 0.01 CELL cell_macro_B PIN Q1 RMS 0.01 PEAK 0.1 AVG 0.01 INSTANCE inst_macro_1 PIN Q2 RMS 0.01 PEAK 0.1 AVG 0.01 INSTANCE inst_macro_2 PIN Q2 RMS 0.01 PEAK 0.1 AVG 0.01
Forces the specified nets to use the simulation mode.
Forces the nets in the specified file to use the simulation mode.

Specifies to skip nets that belong to categories mapping to the specified mode. This parameter allows you to specify three modes, namely 0, 1 (default), and 2. 
 The following table lists the common categories for all modes, and the categories specific to each mode: 
 
 For example, if you set set_signal_em_analysis_mode -skip_category_mode to 1, the report file lists all categories listed in the Common Categories and 1 Skip Category Mode columns. 
 Following is a snippet of the report file for mode 1 :

Specifies the net to be skipped in the signalEM analysis. 
 Refer to the example below: {net_a net_b net_c}

Specifies the net file to be skipped in the signalEM analysis. 
 The format is one net name per line, with comments using # as the first char. For example: #list of nets to skip net_a net_b net_c

Specifies the switching factor for signal nets. A value of 1.0 means that if a flip-flop is clocked by a 20 Mhz clock, it changes state on 100% of the clock cycles. Therefore, the signal has 20 million transitions, which is half the number of transitions of the input clock. As a result, the final switching frequency of the signal net is 10 Mhz. 
 Default: 1.0 Value Range: 0.0 to 1.0

Ignores the internal nets from the first instance to the first flop on the boundary path for the signal EM checking. The following figure represents that when this parameter is used, the internal nets (highlighted in the blue rectangles) are ignored. However, all the nets highlighted through the red lines are reported in the TOP scope ( create_top_scope ) flow:

Forces I rms checks to use the QRC tech file instead of the technology LEF file. If either I peak or I avg is also checked, all checks will use the QRC tech file, including I rms . This parameter is enabled by default. 
 Note: The -method avg and -method peak analysis will only support AC current density rules from QRC tech file. If either avg or peak is also specified, then all checks will use the QRC tech file equations, including RMS.

When this parameter is set to true , the delta_T used to calculate seb_temperature would be based on the RMS delta T (joule heat only). 
 If this parameter is set to false , the delta_T used to calculate seb_temperature would be based on the sum of self-heat delta T and RMS delta T. 
 Default : false
Specifies the name of the view.

lines:
- The following command specifies to calculate current waveform using the rms, avg, and peak methods, and save violations in a report named sigem.rpt set_signal_em_analysis_mode \ -method {rms avg peak} \ -detailed true \ -report sigem.rpt \ -avgRecovery 0 \ -useQrcTech true \ -ict_em_models /../ictfile.ict \ -em_threshold 0 \ 
 verify_AC_limit
- The following is an example of the -extraction_tech_file parameter : set_signal_em_analysis_mode -method {avg rms peak} -detailed \ -ict_em_models ../../EMdata.ict \ -extraction_tech_file qrc_file -em_temperature 105 \ -avgRecovery 0.5 -report AC_signalEM.tcf.rpt -error 100000000
- The following command specifies different temperature values to the layers M1, M2, M3, VIA1, VIA2, and VIA3 for average current limit analysis: set_signal_em_analysis_mode ... 
 -em_temperature 110 \ #temperature for the remaining layers -em_temperature_layer_list {M1 125 M2 115 M3 105 VIA1 115 VIA2 105 VIA3 120} #layer-based temperature 
 The log file will display the following info message: Layer
 specific EM temperature will be used for AVG check for layer M1(125C), 
M2(115C), M3(105C), VIA1(115C), VIA2(105C), VIA3(120C).
Checks for the following types of AC current violations on signal nets:
- Root-mean-square (RMS) current limit violations ( I rms violations)
- Peak current limit violations ( I peak violations)
In addition, verify_AC_limit can be used for average current limit ( I avg ) analysis.

AC current limit violations are sometimes also referred as wire self-heat violations. DRC manuals have these current limits to avoid over-heating a signal-net wire with too much AC current. To prevent wire self-heating or AC signal electromigration, signal interconnects should be analyzed for their AC current carrying capacity and measured against the AC current limits specified by foundry.

data:
Outputs a brief description that includes type and default information for each verify_AC_limit parameter. 
 For a detailed description of the command and all of its parameters, use the man command: man verify_AC_limit

lines: analyze_self_heat [-help] [-alpha_parameters {{<layer_name> < α overlapping > < α connecting >}+}] [-beta_parameters {C1 C2 C3}] [-cell_thermal_resistance_file filename ] [-detail_delta_temperature_file filename ] [-detail_delta_temperature_region { x1 y1 x2 y2 }] [-domain rail_domain_name ] [-instance_delta_temperature_file filename ] [-instance_power_file filename ] [-output_directory dir_name ] [-report_conn_pin_wire filename ] [-scale_rms_limit value ] [-tile_delta_temperature_file filename ] [-tiles { n_tile_in_x m_tile_in_y }]

Specifies the number of tiles, layer-based α coefficients, β coefficients, and list of cells with cell thermal resistances. This command will use the static power data to compute delta T FEOL for all instances in the design. This command:

- computes the RMS currents on signal wires
- computes the RMS currents on power network wires
- generates Detail Delta Temperature File (ddt), Instance Delta Temperature File (idt), and Tile Delta Temperature File (tdt)
[INFO] Self-heat analysis ( analyze_self_heat ) should be performed for the entire design, that is, you must use domain analysis with all PG nets ( -domain ALL ).

data:
Outputs a brief description that includes the type and default information for each analyze_self_heat parameter. 
 For a detailed description of the command and all of its parameters, use the man command man analyze_self_heat .
Specifies the alpha parameters for all layers. It is the metal layer effect.
Specifies the thermal coupling effect coefficient Value.

Specifies the name of the cell's equivalent thermal resistance file. The specified file contains the list of cells and their corresponding thermal resistance value. The unit of thermal resistance is in °C/W (temperature rise per unit rate of heat dissipation). 
 The format of the file is: <cell_name> <thermal_resistance_in_C/W> 
 To get instance deltaT, use half of the instance power multiplied by the cell thermal resistance.
Specifies the name of the generated BEOL detailed delta-temperature file.
Specifies the region coordinates for generating detailed delta temperature file.
Specifies the domain name of the rail.
Specifies the name of the generated FEOL instance-based delta-temperature file.
Specifies a file for instances and their corresponding static power. It is a two column format of < instance_name > and < total_power >.

Specifies the directory name for storing the self-heat analysis results. When you specify this parameter, the following result files are saved in the specified directory: 
 Rail analysis output directory Detail delta temperature file Instance delta temperature file Tile delta temperature file 
 If -output_directory is not specified, by default the self-heat analysis results are stored in the current working directory.
Reports results on wires connecting to cell pins.
Specifies the scale factor for the root-mean-square (rms) limit relaxation factor during Electromigration current limit analysis.
Specifies the name of the generated FEOL/BEOL tile-based delta-temperature file.
Specifies the number of tiles in the x and y directions in the tile-based delta temperature file.

lines: - The following command is an example of analyze_self_heat command: analyze_self_heat -domain PD1 -alpha_parameters {{M1 0.65 0.8} {M2 0.55 0.8} {M3 0.47 0.8} {M4 0.39 0.8} {M5 0.33 0.8} {M6 0.28 0.8} {M7 0.28 0.8} {M8 0.28 0.8} {M9 0.28 0.8} {M10 0.28 0.8} {M11 0.28 0.8}} -beta_parameters {0.0022 -0.0028 0.8424} -cell_thermal_resistance_file res.trf -instance_power_file static_db/static_power.rpt -tiles {10 10} -scale_rms_limit 1.32 -instance_delta_temperature_file sh_itm.txt -tile_delta_temperature_file sh_ttm.txt -detail_delta_temperature_file power_detail.rpt -report_conn_pin_wire self_heat.rpt
- " Self-Heating Effect Analysis " in the Voltus User Guide

analyze_joule_heat [-help] [-detail_delta_temperature_file filename ] [-detail_delta_temperature_region { x1 y1 x2 y2 }] [-domain rail_domain_name ] [-net pg_net_name ] [-output_directory dir_name ] [-report_conn_pin_wire filename ] [-scale_rms_limit value ] [-tile_delta_temperature_file filename ] [-tiles { n_tile_in_x m_tile_in_y }]

Specifies to perform joule heat analysis. This command will use the dynamic RMS current to compute delta-T RMS on the power and signal wires for all segments in the design. The analyze_joule_heat command is similar to the analyze_self_heat command, except that the analyze_self_heat command computes both delta-T FEOL (FEOL self heat) and delta-T RMS (BEOL self heat) whereas the analyze_joule_heat command computes only delta-T RMS.

[INFO] Joule heat analysis ( analyze_joule_heat ) should be performed for the entire design, that is, you must use domain analysis with all PG nets ( -domain ALL ). The -net < pgnet > parameter should be used only for preliminary checking.

data:
Outputs a brief description that includes the type and default information for each analyze_joule_heat parameter. 
 For a detailed description of the command and all of its parameters, use the man command man analyze_joule_heat .
Specifies the name of the generated BEOL detailed delta-temperature file.
Specifies the region coordinates for generating detailed delta temperature file.
Specifies the domain name of the rail.
Specifies a net for joule heat analysis.

Specifies the directory name for storing the joule heat analysis results. When you specify this parameter, the following result files are saved in the specified directory: 
 Rail analysis output directory Detail delta temperature file 
 If -output_directory is not specified, by default the joule heat analysis results are stored in the current working directory.
Reports results on wires connecting to cell pins.
Specifies the scale factor for the root-mean-square (rms) limit relaxation factor during Electromigration current limit analysis.
Specifies the name of the generated BEOL tile-based delta-temperature file.
Specifies the number of tiles in the x and y directions in the tile-based delta temperature file.

lines: - The following command is an example of analyze_joule_heat command: analyze_joule_heat -domain ALL -tile_delta_temperature_file joule_ttm.txt -tiles {10 10} -detail_delta_temperature_file joule_heat_detail.rpt -report_conn_pin_wire joule_heat.rpt
- " Self-Heating Effect Analysis " in the Voltus User Guide
Displays one of the following types of information based on the specified argument:
- Syntax of a command or global variable.
- Summary of the message text based on the specified message ID.
- List of commands based on keywords.
- List of commands based on search string.
To display the syntax of a command or a variable, use the following command:
The command syntax output is written in the log file and displayed in the Voltus console.
To display a summary of the message text for a particular message ID, use the following command:
Shows the summary of message text for the message ID specified.
To perform a keyword search, use the following syntax:
Shows a list of commands with the specified keyword.
To perform a partial string search, use the following syntax:
Shows all commands containing the specified string. The search results vary depending on whether you use wildcards (*) in the beginning, end, or middle of the string.

data: Displays help for the specified command name. If you do not specify a command name, all officially supported Voltus commands are displayed.
Searches for the specified keyword and displays all commands associated with that keyword.
Specifies command pattern, message id or keyword.

Instructs the Voltus software to display all commands that contain the specified series of characters. The search results vary depending on whether you use wildcards (*) in the beginning, end, or middle of the string.

lines: You can use this command after starting the Voltus software.
- The following command prints the command syntax for the set_power_analysis_mode command: help set_power_analysis_mode
- The following command shows the message summary for the ENCSYC-139 message ID: help ENCSYC-139
- The following command displays all commands that include "analysis" as a keyword: help -k analysis
- The following commands show how wildcards can be used in string searches: Use wildcards at the beginning of the string to get all commands that end with the specified string: voltus 18> help *power

Multiple commands found: report_power set_power Use wildcards in the middle of the string when you are not sure what comes in the middle of the string: voltus 22> help set*mode Multiple commands found: set_analysis_mode set_delay_cal_mode set_eco_mode set_ilm_mode set_import_mode set_mode set_noise_run_mode set_power_analysis_mode set_pg_library_mode set_rail_analysis_mode Use wildcards at the end of the string to get all commands that begin with the specified string: voltus 23> help add* Multiple commands found: add_inst add_net add_receiver_stage_spice add_repeater add_thermal_layer add_thermal_package_model add_to_collection

Use wildcards at the beginning and end of the string to get a list of all the commands that contain the string: voltus 24> help *rail* Multiple commands found: analyze_rail set_advanced_rail_options set_dynamic_rail_simulation set_rail_analysis_domain set_rail_analysis_mode
Displays information for a command from the Voltus Text Command Reference . If no manual page is located, man prints an error message.
The Voltus Text Command Reference online help directories are located in the following directory:

You can access Voltus Text Command Reference and other Voltus documentation by clicking on Help menu option in the main Voltus window. You can also access documentation by typing the following UNIX command at a UNIX prompt:

Both actions open the cdnshelp: Library window. From this window you can open or search for topics in the Voltus Text Command Reference , the Voltus User Guide , and other relevant Cadence documentation.

data: Indicates the name of the text command for which the manual information is required.

lines: - The following command returns the manual description of the command set_rail_analysis_mode : man set_rail_analysis_mode
set_analysis_view [-drv string ] -setup { defaultSetupView setupView2 ...} -hold { defaultHoldView holdView2 ...} [-update_timing]

Defines the analysis views to use for setup and hold analysis and optimization. These "active" views represent the different design variations that will be analyzed. Active views can be changed throughout the flow to utilize different subsets of views. Voltus/Tempus can handle the views concurrently or sequentially, depending on their individual capabilities. Libraries and data are loaded into the system, as required to support the selected set of active views.
You must define at least one setup and one hold analysis view.

Note: The order in which you specify views with the -setup and -hold parameters is important. By default, the first views defined in the -setup and -hold lists are the default views. If you use the set_analysis_view command to change the default views, the existing timing, delay calculation, and RC data is reset.

data: Allows you to set DRV views.
Sets the active views for hold analysis.
Sets the active views for setup analysis.

Reloads the configuration described by the MMMC view and mode objects. When the timing global timing_defer_mmmc_object_updates is set to true , this parameter is required to update the MMMC timing environment. 
 The -update_timing parameter may be specified without any -setup or -hold arguments. In this case, the data associated with the current set of setup and/or hold views will be loaded. If no active setup or hold views exist, then using this parameter alone may display an error. 
 The -update_timing parameter may also be used in conjunction with the -setup and -hold options. In this case, the specified setup/hold view configuration will be loaded with the updated MMMC object information.

lines: - The following command sets missionWCCOM and mission2WCCOM as the active views for setup analysis, and missionBCCOM and testBCCOM as the active views for hold analysis: set_analysis_view -setup {missionWCCOM mission2WCCOM} \ -hold {missionBCCOM testBCCOM}

voltus [-abort_on_error] [-batch] [-cds_lib_file in_file ] [-common_ui] [-cpus integer ] [-disable_user_startup] [-execute list_of_Tcl_commands ] [-files in_file_list ] [-help] [-lic_multi_cpu lic_list ] [-lic_options lic_list ] [-lic_startup lic_list ] [-lic_startup_options lic_list ] [-lic_startup_extra lic_list ] [-log prefix ] [-no_logv] [-no_gui] [-overwrite] [-version] [-wait minutes ]
Launches a Voltus session and starts one of the following products:

- Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution L
- Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution XL
For an overview of the products and product licensing, see Product and Licensing Information .

To start a Voltus session, type the voltus command with the appropriate parameters on the UNIX/Linux command line. If you type a command without parameters, the software starts in the GUI mode and creates a log file and a command file. The system attempts to check out the license with the most functionality, then the license with the next most functionality, and so on.

data: Prints out the command usage. For a detailed description of the command and all of its parameters, use the man command: man voltus
Exits if an error is detected at any point. If any Tcl command returns TCL_ERROR to the top level, or any Error (XXX): … message occurs, the process will exit.
Runs without interactive prompt. After all the options, including -execute and -file , are processed, the job exits.

Loads the specified file instead of the default cds.lib file in the current directory to obtain the names and locations of the OpenAccess libraries. The cds.lib file is used to list the OpenAccess library names, and their physical locations.
Launches the Common UI interface. This changes the Tcl commands to the Common UI interface described in the Voltus Common UI Text Command Reference Manual manual.

Specifies the number of CPUs to be used for multi-threading on current host. This calls the appropriate Tcl command to set the local CPU count. This happens before any -execute or -file commands are processed. This may require extra multi-CPU licenses to be checked out (see -lic_multi_cpu ).

Reads only the initialization file in the installation hierarchy. Does not read the initialization files in the home or current directory. This option suppresses the default loading from the home and current directory, which can be used to guarantee there are no local over-rides to the various project settings. 
 For more information, see "Setting Preferences" in the Getting Started chapter of the Voltus User Guide for init files that are loaded by default.
Specifies the Tcl commands to be executed as a quoted string at startup.
Specifies one or more Tcl files to be sourced at startup.

Controls which licenses are to be used for higher multi-CPU usage in priority order from left to right. The allowed license names are: 
 
 vtsmp : Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution MP 
 
 vtsxl : Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution XL

Specifies the order that extra option licenses should be checked-out if needed. In some cases more than one option license can be used to enable a given feature. You can control which options are allowed to be checked-out, and in what priority (from left to right) when required during the run. The allowed license names are: 
 vtsaa : Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution Advanced Analysis GXL Option vtsesd : Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution - ESD (Electrostatic Discharge Analysis) Option vtsxm : Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution - XM (Extreme Modeling) Option tpsxl : Tempus Timing Signoff Solution XL tpsl : Tempus Timing Signoff Solution L tpstso : Tempus Timing Signoff Solution TSO tpspi : Tempus Timing Signoff Solution - PI (Power Integrity) Option invs_ms : Innovus Mixed Signal Option 
 Option licenses can be checked-out dynamically when a specific Tcl command requires it. This may cause the Tcl script to stall and wait for a license, or to exit if the wait period expires. 
 If you specify more than one product option, begin and end the list with double quotation marks or braces. 
 For example, -lic_options " vtsaa vtsesd " will only allow these two options to be checked-out dynamically, and in the given priority order. 
 Note: For information, see Product and Licensing Information .

Requests one of these licenses at startup in priority order from left to right. The supported licenses are: 
 vtsxl : Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution XL 
 vtsl : Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution L 
 vdsp : Virtuoso Digital Signoff Power Solution 
 For example, -lic_startup "vtsxl vtsl" will try to check-out vtsxl first and then vtsl . The check-outs will be attempted repeatedly until it succeeds or the specified wait ( -wait ) period expires.

Specifies additional option licenses needed at startup. If not provided, they will be checked out when needed during the run. The allowed license names are: 
 vtsaa : Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution Advanced Analysis GXL Option vtsesd : Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution - ESD (Electrostatic Discharge Analysis) Option vtsxm : Voltus IC Power Integrity Solution - XM (Extreme Modeling) Option tpsxl : Tempus Timing Signoff Solution XL tpsl : Tempus Timing Signoff Solution L tpstso : Tempus Timing Signoff Solution TSO tpspi : Tempus Timing Signoff Solution - PI (Power Integrity) Option invs_ms : Innovus Mixed Signal Option 
 For example, -lic_startup_options "vtsaa, vtsesd " will try to check-out both vtsaa and vtsesd when Voltus starts up. The check-outs will be attempted repeatedly until it succeeds or the -wait period expires.

Checks out the specified Innovus license to enable saving of physical changes. By default, Voltus cannot save physical changes to the database, but if you check out the correct extra Innovus license at startup, you can save the physical changes. The supported licenses are: 
 invs - Innovus Implementation System invsb - Innovus Implementation System Basic vdixl - Virtuoso® Digital Implementation XL 
 Note : The vdixl license can only be used with the Voltus base license vdsp .

Specifies a name for the log file. By default, the software saves log files in the run directory and increments them, for example, voltus .log , voltus.log1 , voltus .log2 , voltus .log3 , and so on. 
 Default: voltus.log 
 Specifies the prefix for the .log , .cmd , and .logv files. If three arguments are given, such as -log "a b c" , then the three names are used exactly without any extension added. So, -log "mylog mycmd mylogv" will result in mylog mycmd mylogv files. 
 The default for prefix is voltus . So if -log is not specified, voltus.log , voltus .cmd , and voltus .logv files will be created. If you specify -log test , the tool creates test.log , test.cmd and test.logv files. 
 The .log file contains the normal logging output, the .cmd file contains the Tcl commands that were executed, and the .logv file contains a super-set of the .log output plus "verbose" debugging output that is needed only for detailed debugging. 
 If the .log file already exists, an integer is added to the end of all the files to make them unique. This means if -log test is specified, the tool will create test.log test.cmd test.logv for the first run, test.log1 test.cmd1 test.logv1 for the next run, and so on. Only the existence of .log is checked, the existence of the .cmd or .logv files is not checked. The -overwrite option can be used to disable this behavior and overwrite an existing .log file. 
 If the prefix has a period in it, the last extension is stripped off for the .cmd and .logv usage. For example, -log out.log will result in out.log out.cmd out.logv files, and -log out.a.log will result in out.a.log out.a.cmd out.a.logv files. 
 You can disable any given file by using /dev/null . For example, -log "my.log /dev/null /dev/null" will only create my.log . 
 Default : voltus.log 
 Note : If you provide the log file name under a directory path and the directory path does not exist, Voltus will create the directory path and save the log file under the directory.
Disables GUI and Tk support. GUI will not be available later in the session.
Specifies that verbose log file will not be created.
Overwrites an existing .log file (and associated .cmd and .logv files) instead of adding an integer to make the files unique.
Specifies the number of minutes the system waits for a license to become available before exiting. Default : 0 (no wait time) Value Range : 0 to 10,000

lines: If install_dir is the location of your Voltus installation, you should set up your run-time environment like this:

- Add the install_dir /bin directory to your path. The bin directory has links to all the public executables in the install hierarchy.
- If you want the legacy Voltus man pages to be available from the Unix man command, you can add install_dir /share/voltus/man to your MANPATH envar.
- If you want the Tcl man pages to be available from the Unix man command, you can add install_dir /share/tcltools/man to your MANPATH envar.
For example, you might add this to your startup shell script:

Note : When Voltus launches, it automatically adds either the legacy or the Common UI man pages (depending on the -common_ui option) and the Tcl man pages to the beginning of the current MANPATH inside Voltus. Therefore, from within Voltus, the man command will see both sets of man pages before any other man pages.
Each Voltus session creates its own temporary directory to store temporary files at the beginning of the run.
By default, tmp_dir is created in /tmp . If the Unix envar TMPDIR is set, then the tmp_dir directory is created inside $TMPDIR .
The name of the tmp_dir directory will look like:
Where _xxxxxx is a string added to make the directory unique. For example:
The temporary directory is automatically removed on exit or if the run terminated with a catchable signal (e.g. SIGSEGV ).
