Returns predefined metrics directly after the associated command is run without any need for log parsing. Metrics can be written to a log or printed as TCL output. The metrics are automatically saved/restored with design data ( designName .metric ). Metrics can accumulate across multiple sessions and keep being appended to the .metric file.

By default, the get_metric command returns all metrics last computed from the commands generating the metrics. Currently, get_metric supports the following command: report_power ,analyze_rail , verify_AC_limit , and report_freq_violation commands. The get_metric command is applicable only for static power analysis.

data:
Prints a brief description that includes type and default information for each get_metric parameter. 
 For a detailed description of the command and all of its parameters, use the man command: man get_metric .

Specifies list of patterns to match snapshot branches. 
 This will limit all output to metrics stored in snapshots whose branch matches the specified branch. This requires any inheritance to be re-run to exclude inherited values from non-matching snapshots.
Specifies not to return any inherited value.
Name of id (if read_metric was used). Default is current database.
Name of label to get metrics from (wildcards supported); no label reports current metrics.

Returns the value of specified metric names. 
 Note: When specifying metric names, you can use wildcards.
Returns metrics from the pending (not yet created) snapshot.
Returns metrics as raw value (without normalization to defined units).

lines: - The following command returns metrics for the analyze_rail command: get_metric -pending The final output is as follows: rail.averagedemandcurrent.net:VSS 0 rail.averagesupplycurrent.net:VSS 0 rail.gridcap.net:VSS 0 rail.intrinsiccap.net:VSS 0 rail.ir.dynamic.avg.net:VSS 0.0119396 rail.ir.dynamic.max.net:VSS 0.0442943 rail.ir.dynamic.min.net:VSS 0 rail.ir.dynamic.violations.net:VSS 0 rail.loadingcap.net:VSS 0 rail.peakdemandcurrent.net:VSS 0 rail.peaksupplycurrent.net:VSS 0 rail.referencevoltage.net:VSS 0 rail.rj.avg.net:VSS NA rail.rj.max.net:VSS NA rail.rj.min.net:VSS NA rail.rj.violations.net:VSS 0 rail.thresholdvoltage.net:VSS 0.05 rail.totalcap.net:VSS 0 rail.worstircycle.net:VSS {3.550 nS}

Saves your design as a standalone testcase that includes both the design and the library files. All database references to files in the generated testcase are self-contained, such that the database can be loaded into Voltus on a different network.
You can use this command after loading a design.

Note: Exit Voltus after using the command. This is to avoid a possibility where save_design may create a configuration file with a timing constraints file pointing to the testcase directory. Given that the testcase directory is considered temporary, and may be gzipped or even deleted, a pointer to its data is not desirable. Exiting Voltus and re-loading a previously saved database will avoid this possibility.

data:
Specifies the name of the testcase to be created. The path to the saved testcase location is: 
 directoryName/testcaseName 
 If a previously generated testcase exists at the specified location, the older testcase will be renamed before the new testcase is saved. 
 Default : testcase

Specifies the directory in which to save the testcase. Both, absolute and relative directory paths, may be used. 
 Default : Current working directory
Specifies to copy foundation flow files.

Specifies that RC extraction data should be saved. 
 Default : RC parasitics files are not saved. 
 Note: This option works with these limitations: 
 The -rc parameter does not have save RC extraction data if the design is not extracted or if you extract the data using the default extraction engine. The previously saved RC data can only be restored on systems that share the same platform and architecture. For example, if you saved the RC data on a Linux platform you can not restore it on a IBM AIX platform. Similarly, RC data saved on a 64-bit system cannot be restored on a 32-bit system.

Specifies that a single script should be generated containing both shell executable and Voltus TCL commands. 
 Default : Two script files are generated. A shell executable file and a Voltus TCL script.

Saves only the libraries listed in the init_oa_tech_lib , init_oa_ref_lib , and init_oa_search_lib open access designs, which are referenced through the on-demand processing. On specifying this parameter, a new cds.lib file is created with only the specified libraries. When this parameter is not used, all the open access libraries are saved in the original cds.lib file. 
 Note: This parameter works only when the init_design_netlisttype is OA , init_oa_ref_lib is not null, and init_lef_file is null.
Skips saving of timing graph by default. This saves time by preventing the timing update to occur in case of designs where no graph is generated in the session.

Specifies that existing files should be overwritten. 
 Default : Existing testcase files are renamed before new testcase is saved.

Specifies that the generated self contained design should be stored in a gzip compressed tar file. The generated test case directory and files are deleted after gzip compression is complete. 
 Default : If you do not specify this parameter, the generated test case is not gzipped.

lines: - The following command creates a self contained design in the current working directory under the testcase directory. save_testcase
- The following command creates a gzip compressed tar file /tmp/testcase.tar.gz containing a self contained testcase: save_testcase -dir /tmp -gzip

set_delay_cal_mode [-help] [-accuracy_level level ] [-accurate_receiver_out_load {true | false}] [-advanced_pincap_mode {true | false}] [-combine_mmmc {none | early_late}] [-early_irdrop_data_type {best_average | worst | best | average | worst_average}] [-enable_high_fanout {true | false}] [-enable_quiet_receivers_for_hold {true | false}] [-equivalent_waveform_model {none | no_propagation | propagation}] [-equivalent_waveform_model_for_timing_check {true|false}] [-ewm_type {moments | simulation}] [-honorSlewPropConstraint {true | false}] [-irdrop_data_type {best_average | worst | best | average | worst_average}] [-irDrop_window_based {none | late | early | both}] [-late_irdrop_data_type {best_average | worst | best | average | worst_average}] [-library_interpolation_mode {linear | non_linear}] [-reportOutBound {true | false}] [-reset] [-SIAware {true | false}] [-slewOutBoundLimitHigh value ] [-slewOutBoundLimitLow value ] [-socv_accuracy_mode {low | medium | high | ultra} ] [-socv_lvf_mode {moments | early_late}] [-socv_machine_learning_level {0|1}] [-socv_use_lvf_tables {all | delay | slew | constraint}]
Sets the global parameters or delay calculation.
By default, the software uses linear interpolation for timing check delay calculation.

data:
Outputs a brief description that includes type and default information for each set_delay_cal_mode parameter. 
 For a detailed description of the command and all of its parameters, use the man set_delay_cal_mode command.

Sets the accuracy level for delay calculations. 
 Default : 0, Min : 0, Max : 4

When set to true , Voltus uses distributed interconnect model as receiver output load to compute accurate miller effect. 
 In this mode, Voltus uses a reduced interconnect model for receivers instead of lumped capacitance as the receiver load. This improves computation of the back miller effect and the delay accuracy. 
 When set to false , Voltus uses lumped capacitance as the receiver’s load. 
 Default : false

Simulates the current stage and receiver stages together, such that the back miller effect is modeled dynamically. 
 When set to true , the receivers are no longer modeled as simple non-linear current source models. This results in increasing the delay calculation accuracy significantly as the modeling back miller effect improves. 
 When set to false , the receiver current sources are computed using ECSM/CCS capacitance tables with the estimated input slew. 
 Default : false

Specifies whether delay calculation runs are to be combined for delay calculation simulations. This parameter provides user control for improving the runtime of AAE for all analysis types by combining runs for early and late simulations into a single simulation. The views with common delay corners may also be combined into a single delay calculation simulation for added runtime improvement. 
 When none is specified, the software does not combine any delay calculation runs. When early_late is specified, the software combines early and late simulations of a single corner into one delay calculation simulation. 
 Default: early_late

Allows you to set the early IR drop EIV values for different EIV voltages. 
 Default : worst

When this parameter is set to true , the default net delay will be annotated on high fanout nets. A net will be treated as a high fanout net when the fanout is more than the specified limit. The default value of this command is 1000 fanouts. The default net delay is also specified using this command. The default net delay is 1ns . 
 The cell delay will use the lumped capacitance that is specified using the delaycal_default_net_load global variable for the table lookup. The default net load is 0.5pF . The driver slew will use the default value of 0ps and there will be no slew degradation along the interconnect. 
 The default value of this parameter is false .

When set to true , Voltus forces some receivers as static (“quiet”). To compute the worst-case delays for early analysis, the software assumes that the receivers are not switching. This yields more conservative early delays. 
 When set to false , the software assumes receivers may switch. 
 Default : false

Controls the equivalent waveform model to be used. You can specify one of the following options: 
 none : When this option is specified, the waveform shape beyond slew measurement points does not impact delays. For example, set_delay_cal_mode -equivalent_waveform_model none This mode is equivalent to setting the following options in the previous releases: set_delay_cal_mode -equivalent_waveform_model_type none set_delay_cal_mode -equivalent_waveform_model_propagation false no_propagation : In this mode, the impact of receiver waveform shape on the next stage delay is estimated and adjusted the net delay of the current stage. For example, set_delay_cal_mode -equivalent_waveform_model no_propagation set_delay_cal_mode -ewm_type moments This mode is equivalent to setting the following options in the previous releases: set_delay_cal_mode -equivalent_waveform_model_type ecsm set_delay_cal_mode -equivalent_waveform_model_propagation false propagation : When specified, the impact of input waveform shape is considered during delay calculation and the waveforms are propagated across each stage of the entire path. For example, set_delay_cal_mode -equivalent_waveform_model propagation set_delay_cal_mode -ewm_type moments 
 This mode is equivalent to setting the following options in the previous releases: set_delay_cal_mode -equivalent_waveform_model_type ecsm set_delay_cal_mode -equivalent_waveform_model_propagation true

Models the waveform distortion effect for timing check value look ups at both the data and clock pins. 
 The model uses simulations to convert the distorted waveforms into equivalent waveforms (pre-driver waveforms in the libraries) by matching the pin CCB output waveforms. This feature requires the CCSN library. 
 Default : false

Specifies the equivalent waveform model (EWM) to be used: 
 The options are described below: 
 moments : Enables moment-based equivalent waveform model. simulation : Enables simulation-based equivalent waveform model. The simulation-based EWM is required for predicting delay in noisy waveforms. The simulation option requires noise models (ECSMN/CCSN). If noise models are not available, then the software defaults to moments-based EWM. 
 It is recommended to use simulation-based EWM as it is more accurate than moment-based EWM. 
 Default: moments

Determines whether to propagate the slew from the disabled timing arcs to the output pin. When this parameter is set to false , the slew from the disabled timing arc affects the delay of the next stage. 
 When set to true, the timing constraints defined using commands, such as set_disable_timing and set_case_analysis impact the slew propagation, and the slew from the disabled timing arcs is not propagated. 
 Default : true

Allows you to set the early/late IR drop EIV values for different EIV voltages. 
 Default : worst

Searches window-based EIV values from the EIV database. 
 Default : both

Allows you to set the late IR drop EIV values for different EIV voltages. 
 Default : worst

Controls library scaling mode - linear or non_linear - for the interpolation flow. 
 Default : linear

Generates a report that contains a list of index values (input transition) in the delay tables that are beyond the index range. The index values detected by this parameter can cause inaccurate delay values to be calculated. The report generated by this parameter can be used for debugging. 
 When this parameter is set to true , extrapolation information will be dumped into the following two files: 
 <design-name>.dc.outbound.slew.early.<view-name>.txt <design-name>.dc.outbound.slew.late.<view-name>.txt 
 The software writes a warning message in the log files noting that there are input transition values that are out of the index range of the timing table. The warning message also provides the file name for viewing the detail report. 
 When set to true , the –reportOutBound parameter will generate reports that can be controlled using set_outbound_report command. 
 Default : false

Resets the parameters to their default values. The -reset parameter must be specified before the parameters for which you want to reset the values. If you specify the -reset parameter by itself, the software resets all the set_delay_cal_mode parameters to their default values.

Enables SIAware delay calculation that also includes cross-talk induced delays. 
 When set to true , this parameter enables signal integrity (SI) analysis. 
 When set to false , this parameter disables SI analysis. 
 Default : false

Limits the maximum slew used in the analysis to the specified value. 
 Default : 1.3 
 Min : 1.000000 
 Max : 340282346638528859811704183484516925440.000000

Limits the minimum slew used in the analysis to the specified value. 
 Default :0.5 
 Min : 0.001000 
 Max : 1.000000)

Allows the ability to control levels of accuracy. 
 This parameter specifies level-based accuracy effort for delays and slews. You can use one of the following options: 
 low : Ignores correlation between slews and delays. This is the default setting. medium : Models correlation between immediate slew and delay. high : Considers skewness of non-gaussian distribution into account for computing quantiles. The skewness is local to one stage only. ultra : Considers skewness of non-gaussian distribution, and also propagates skewness to the next stage in PBA analysis.

Controls interpretation of LVF data. 
 Default : moments

Enables the SOCV machine learning mode. 
 For accurate SOCV analysis, the software creates several statistical models, which are not available with standard LVF library, such as correlation between cells delay and output slew, conversion of moments from late/early sigma tables, and effects of waveform distortion on sigma and mean shift. When the -socv_machine_learning_level parameter is set to 1, the software enables several machine learning algorithms to generate such models internally. 
 Default : 0

Controls the type of variations that are considered during analysis. 
 Default : all

lines:
- The following command enables waveform propagation along the path for equivalent waveform model: set_delay_cal_mode -equivalent_waveform_model propagation
- The following command specifies that the software should not combine any delay calculation runs: set_delay_cal_mode -combine_mmmc none

- If you have set the value of the -SIAware parameter to true , use the following command to reset the value of the parameter to the default value, which is false, to disable SI analysis during delay calculation: set_delay_cal_mode -reset -SIAware Now when you retrieve the value of the parameter using the get_delay_cal_mode command, the software returns the following: get_delay_cal_mode -SIAware 
 -SIAware false # enums={true false}, default=false false

set_message [-help] [-id list_of_msgIDs ] [-severity {warn error info} | -limit <number> | -no_limit | -suppress | -unsuppress | -on_error {msg_only exit stop_script} | - stop_script_limit limitNumber ]

Specifies to change the message severity levels, INFO, WARNING or ERROR , at the beginning of the message. This command allows you to control the messages that are displayed for a particular design. For example, if there are some warnings that are critical for the design, you can change the message severity to ERROR . Similarly, if there are messages that you want to ignore, you can change the message severity to INFO .

data:
Outputs a brief description that includes type and default information for each set_message parameter. 
 For a detailed description of the command and all of its parameters, use the man command: man set_message .
Specifies the list of message IDs that require a change in the message severity level. If you do not specify a message ID, the change in severity level is applied to all message IDs.
Sets the message limit for the selected messages. The limit must be greater than zero.
Removes any existing limit on the selected messages. Suppressed messages are not unsuppressed.

Specifies an extra action for error messages (not for warn or info messages). If no message is specified, the action applies to all error messages. 
 msg_only : Prints error message only, and the command proceeds as it normally would. This is the default. exit : Exits immediately and returns to Linux. stop_script : Waits for the current Tcl command to finish, then stops executing any Tcl script, and returns to the interactive Tcl prompt. 
 Note, if a message severity is changed from error to either warn or info, it will no longer trigger this extra action.
Specifies the new severity level for the message ID being modified.

Sets the message limit for a public command. If you set -stop_script_limit for a command as 10 and message for the command is triggered for the 10th time, the tool will wait for the current Tcl command to finish, then stop executing any Tcl script, and return to the interactive Tcl prompt. The default value of -stop_script_limit is -1 , which is unlimited.

lines:
- The following command changes the message ENCLF-81 from ERROR to WARN : set_message -id ENCLF-81 -severity warn
- The following command returns all messages to their original severity: set_message -severity reset
- The following command resets the specified message to its original severity: set_message -id ENCLF-18 -severity reset

Limits error and warning messages to a specified number per unique ID. Any occurrences beyond the limit are suppressed. The default limit is 20 per message ID. The default limit is applicable only for ERROR* and WARNING* messages; there is no default limit for verify*, check*, and INFO* messages.

data:
Outputs a brief description that includes type and default information for each set_message_limit parameter. 
 For a detailed description of the command and all of its parameters, use the man command: man set_message_limit .
Specifies the maximum number of messages to be displayed for each unique ID. The value of numberToLImit should be greater than zero.
Specifies prefix-based message limit for specific warnings.

lines: - The following command sets a limit of 10 messages per unique ID for all error and warning messages: set_message_limit 10

- The following command sets a limit of 2 for the TCLCMD-917 error message. set_message_limit 2 TCLCMD-917 After 2 occurrences of TCLCMD-917 , which is the same as the limit set, the system announces that the limit has been reached. [DEV]voltus 4> set_propagated_clock CLK1 **ERROR: (TCLCMD-917): Cannot find 'ports, pins, or clocks' that match 'CLK1' [DEV]voltus 5> set_propagated_clock CLK1 **ERROR: (TCLCMD-917): Cannot find 'ports, pins, or clocks' that match 'CLK1' [DEV]voltus 6> set_propagated_clock CLK1 Message <TCLCMD-917> has exceeded the message display limit of '2'. setMessageLimit/set_message_limit sets the limit. unsetMessageLimit/unset_message_limit can be used to reset this.
Reads a file and executes the commands in the file (scripts).

data:
Enables verbose source. If -echo is specified, the tool will ignore it and continue with the source instead of giving an error. 
 Note: This is optional.
Specifies the name of the file to source.

Forces source to be normal Tcl source command. 
 Note: This is optional.

Forces source to be in verbose mode. 
 Note: This is optional.

lines: Copies the Voltus Foundation flows templates into the directory you specify, or to the current directory if you do not specify one with the -directory parameter.

data:
Outputs a brief description that includes type and default information for each write_flow_template parameter. 
 For a detailed description of the command and all of its parameters, use the man command: man write_flow_template .
Specifies the directory in which to save the templates.

lines: - The following command copies the Voltus foundation flows templates into the voltus FF directory: write_flow_template -directory voltusFF
This chapter describes the general global variables that can be set to change the default behavior in Voltus. You can set general global variables as follows:
This chapter describes the general global variables that can be set to change the default behavior in Voltus. You can set general global variables as follows:

Creates a dummy .lib file and a user-defined noise model (UDN) for cells that are not present in the loaded .lib or cdB. The dummy .lib file is used for timing analysis, and the dummy UDN file is used for signal integrity analysis. To generate the dummy .lib and UDN, set this variable to 1 .
To set this variable, use the set_var command.

Infers the rise or fall of NLDM pin capacitance range from the ECSM capacitance range while calculating the total loading capacitance during switching power computation. If the global is set to true and the capacitance range is not defined in the library, the rise/fall capacitance range is inferred from the ECSM receiver capacitance models. The capacitance range values are derived either from the pin-level ECSM cap models or the timing-level ECSM cap models.
When this global is set to false , the rise/fall capacitance range values are not inferred, and the library values are honored.
To set this global variable, use the set_global command.

Specifies to use the Composite Current Source (CCS) unified power library format suitable for power optimization, power analysis, and rail analysis. It also allows you to capture a supply current waveform for each power or ground pin.
When this global is set to 1 , the CCS Power construct from the Liberty library is loaded and used in power analysis.
To set this global variable, use the set_global command.
When set to true , enc_tcl_error_info prints the error information if there is an issue in the Tcl script.

Excludes all references to the named cells in the specified file, from the design database

lines: Used to resolve conflicts between names in the design database and names used in the library. One usage is to force design cell names to correspond to library names - this is a more common problem in GDS2 designs where uppercase cell names do not match lowercase library names. Another usage is to change a design cell that is not intended to be mapped to a library view.
Specifies how to cluster or re-cluster via arrays. Since the clustering approach reduces the RC elements by clustering vias into one node, it can boost the IR drop analysis runtime in dynamic mode.

data: Specifies the name of the via layer.

Specifies that all via geometries on the specified via layer within 
 distance 
 , in microns, of other via geometries will be clustered together into a multi-cut via.

Specifies that if the resulting cluster exceeds the 
 max_dimension 
 in either number of rows or columns of vias it will be split in half repeatedly until the maximum number of rows or columns, per cluster, is less than or equal to the 
 max_dimension 
 .

lines: This example specifies that all via1 geometries within 1 micron distance from each other will be clustered. The maximum cluster size in either x or y direction is 4 vias.
The density_check_rectangle command checks wire density in a specified area of the chip.
Note: This command must be used with the variable density_check_method , set to rectangle .

 Y

The extraction_type command controls the type of extraction performed on the nets and selects the nets on which to perform the extraction. By default , Fire & Ice QX extracts all regular nets as a decoupled RC network.

data:
Extracts all nets listed in the 
 filename 
 file. 
 Note: f you select this option, you should first turn off the default value by setting the all option to none , as in the following example, unless you want to extract in two different modes, such as coupled and decoupled:

Selects all nets whose names contain the pattern specified by 
 pattern 
 . 
 pattern 
 uses the DEF regular expression syntax, which follows:
Matches any sequence of 0 or more characters. For example, *net* matches 123netxyz , netvdd , and 10ce463pnet .

Matches any single character. 
 For example, bus% matches bus1 , bus2 , bus3 , and so forth. Similarly, clock%test matches clock7test , clock8test , and clock9test , but not clock10test .

Matches the whole sequence of characters up to and including the next period ("."). If there is no "." in the string, it matches to the end of the string. 
 For example, --bus3 matches bus3 in the following expression: block1.cell2.bus3
Specifies the type of RC extraction. The type of RC extraction can be one of the following:
Extracts the decoupled resistance and capacitance network. This option is the default .
Performs no extraction on the defined set of nets.
Specifies the type of capacitance extracted. The type of capacitance extraction can be one of the following:

lines: Note: There is no limit to the number of extraction_type commands that you can define in a single command file, so you can use them repeatedly to fully describe the nets to extract and completely control the extraction. Later specifications override earlier ones if they select the same nets. Note: By default , zx extracts all regular nets as a decoupled RC network. Reduction of the output is controlled by the reduce_output variable. Note: zx detects power and ground nets from the DEF file by their +USE POWER and +USE GROUND attributes. It excludes these nets from the extraction unless you explicitly request that they be extracted by using their name or a matching pattern with the extraction_type command.

The layer command maps the layer names in the LEF technology file to the layer names in the IceCaps technology file or the GDSII file used to import metal fill. You only need to use this command if the layer names specified in the IceCaps technology file or the GDSII file are different from those used in the LEF file.

data: Specifies the name of the layer in the LEF file.
Maps between a layer in the LEF file and a layer in the IceCaps technology file or the RCgen technology file.
Maps between a layer in the LEF file and the GDSII file.
Specifies the layer number of the GDSII layer.

The layer bias command sets a bias value, in microns, by which to undersize all wires on a defined layer. It must be in the range of -1 to 1. This value is applied to each edge of the wire, decreasing the width by two times the bias value. The biasing changes only the width of the wire, not the length. You can select a different bias value for resistance extraction and capacitance extraction.

data: Specifies the name of the layer containing the wires to be biased.
Specifies the amount, in microns, by which to bias the wires. A positive value undersizes the width of the wire.
Applies the bias value only during resistance extraction.
Applies the bias value only during capacitance extraction.
Applies the bias value during both resistance and capacitance extraction.

This command associates a 
 layer_name 
 with the integer layer value 
 gds_layer_num 
 found in the GDS2 data. This layer name comes from the extraction technology file.

All layers in GDS2 format, to be used for extraction, must be defined with the layer gds command. Multiple commands may use the same 
 layer_name 
 in order to combine data in GDS2 with different layer or datatype fields into a single extraction layer.

The GDS2 format defines legal values for both layers and datatypes as 0 to 255 inclusive. Any combination of layer gds statements may be used with these values for layer and datatype. Omission of any datatype information implies that all datatypes for that layer are used. Single values and ranges for datatypes may also be specified for each layer gds statement.

data: Specifies the name of the layer
Specifies an optional single gds2 datatype number, or the first datatype number for when a range is specified
Specifies a gds2 datatype range, specified as a third number.
Keyword used to include GDS2 text objects (all texttypes) as port definitions for the layer

Keyword used to transfer text from GDS2 text objects (all texttypes) as a net name annotation to the geometry enclosing the text location. There is no difference between the 
 port 
 and 
 net 
 keywords - they are duplicated in order to get consistent terminology with the label net command.
Keyword indicating that all GDS2 text on the given layer is to be read (including all texttypes) and interpreted as labels
Keyword indicating that geometries with the given layer/datatype values are to be considered to be explicit fill data
Keyword indicating that geometries with the given layer/datatype values are to be considered as explicit slotting data

The layer map command can also be used to associate DEF layer names to technology layer names when they do not match.

 layer_name

The promote_ports command is used to control the use of port geometries during extraction.

data: The default value. Also, this is the default if no promote_ports command is found. This means that all port geometry will be subtracted from interconnect geometry and will not be used to generate parasitic values. This option will ensure that no cell parasitic is included in the interconnect model, but it could cause errors with respect to feedthru resistance or coupling capacitance to ports.

The all option merges all of the port geometries into the interconnect. This provides more accurate feedthru resistance and coupling capacitance, but could duplicate some parasitic effects included in the library model.

The cell 
 cell_name 
 pin 
 pin_name 
 specification is used to selectively include the port geometry of a specific pin from all instances of a cell.

The inst 
 inst_name 
 pin 
 pin_name 
 specification is used to selectively include the port geometry of a specific pin from a single instance.
Specifies the specific cell or instance pin.

lines: The first example below merges all port geometries into the interconnect: The second example promotes the port geometry of pin FT1, of cell R64. And the third example promotes pin Z, of instance IOA.
The density_check_method variable selects the method that zx uses to check the wire density of the chip.

data: Checks the wire density in the area defined by the diearea keyword in the DEF file. It checks the area on all layers. It checks the area by dividing it into a tile size defined by the technology file, usually 50 to 100 microns on a side. This option is recommended for full-chip checking.

Checks the wire density in the area defined by the layout database. This area is defined by a leftmost, bottommost point to a rightmost, topmost point. It checks the area on all layers. It checks the area by dividing it into a tile size defined by the technology file, usually 50 to 100 microns on a side. This option is recommended for block-level checking. The default is database .
Checks the wire density in the area defined by the extent of routing on each area of a layer. This option is recommended for validating layout accuracy only.
Checks the wire density in a rectangular region defined by the command density_check_rectangle .
Uses the default method, which is database .

lines: The following example checks the wire density in an area defined by the diearea keyword in the DEF file:
The dump_options variable saves the current zx variable settings in the log file (see log_file ).

data: Saves the variable settings to a log file.
Does not save the settings to a log file. The default is false

lines: The following example saves the current variable settings to the log file:
The enable_erosion_effects variable determines whether to include the effects of erosion on resistance, capacitance, or both in the extraction results.

data: Extracts only resistance effects due to erosion.
Extracts only capacitance effects due to erosion.
Extracts resistance and capacitance effects due to erosion. The default is rc .
Does not extract resistance or capacitance effects due to erosion.

lines: In the following example, zx includes the effects of erosion on capacitance in the extraction results:

The enable_metal_fill_effects variable determines whether zx includes in the extraction results the effects of the metal fill between wires on a layer that was deposited according to the specifications in the DEF file.
Note: zx issues a warning if the DEF file does not contain specifications for metal fill.

data: When you set this variable to true , zx includes the effects of metal fill between the wires. The default is true .

If you set the variable enable_metal_fill_effects to true , you must use the variable metal_fill_type to specify the type of metal fill to use, either grounded, which has a fixed electrical charge, or floating, which has no fixed electrical charge. Typically, grounded metal has a higher capacitance.
When you set this variable to false , zx does not include the effects of metal fill between the wires.

lines: In this example, zx does not include the effects of metal fill between the wires in the extraction results:
The enable_poly_routing variable enables zx to extract parasitic data for the layer type POLYCIDE.

data: When you set this variable to true , zx extracts parasitic data for the layer type POLYCIDE.
When you set this variable to false , zx will not extract parasitic data for the layer type POLYCIDE. The default is false.

lines: The following example specifies that zx should extract parasitic data for the layer type POLYCIDE.
The enable_r_function_width enables zx to establish resistivity values based on wire width, versus establishing resistivity based on a fixed value.

data: When you set this variable to true , the tool establishes resistivity values based on wire width.
When you set this variable to false , the tool does not establish resistivity values based on wire width.

lines: The following example specifies that zx establishes the resistivity values based on wire width.

The enable_toplevel_diff_extract variable extracts ports defined on the diffusion layer in power-grid view library. This variable is used during body bias analysis to extract connectivity in the diffusion layer..

data: When you set this variable to true , the tool extracts ports defined on the diffusion layer in power-grid view library.
When you set this variable to false , the tool does not extract the ports defined on the diffusion layer.

lines: The following example specifies that zx extracts ports defined on the diffusion layer in power-grid view library.
The exclude_blockagewire variable determines whether zx ignores, for parasitic extraction, any wires in the DEF input file which have SHAPE BLOCKAGE in the SpecialNets section.

data: When you set this variable to true , zx ignores, for parasitic extraction, any wires in the DEF input file which have a SHAPE BLOCKAGE statement. The default is true .
When you set this variable to false , zx does not ignore wires that have a SHAPE BLOCKAGE statement.

lines: The following example specifies that zx should not ignore wires that have a SHAPE BLOCAGE statement specified in the DEF input file.:
The gds_unit variable specifies what conversion factor zx is to use to convert GDS database units - used for geometric dimensions, coordinates, etc. - to microns.

data: Specifies the conversion factor zx is to use to convert GDS database units to 1 micron. It is an integer value within the range of 1 through 1000000 (inclusive). The default is 1000.

lines: The following example converts 10,000 GDS database units to 1 micron:

The ignore_shorts variable enables zx to either flag or ignore detected shorted nets, as in previous releases. The default setting is true for DEF input, and f alse for other input. Setting it to true will make zx behave the same as in Version 3.x.

data: When set to true, if shorts are detected, they will be ignored and zx continues with some degree of inaccuracy.
When set to false, shorts will be flagged and zx will continue but with those nets shorted.

lines: The following example specifies that zx is to ignore any detected shorts:
The layout_scale variable scales the dimensions in the layout by the scale factor that you specify. Using this option enables zx to correctly extract designs that have been sized by a scale factor.

data: Specifies the scale factor by which zx scales the layout. This factor can range from 0.01 to 5. The default is 1.0, indicating that no scaling is performed.

lines: This example scales the layout of the design by 25 percent:
The load_optimized_views variable specifies that zx load the optimized version of the power-grid views from the cell library. The load_optimized_views variable is used in power-grid analysis flow.

Optimized means that small notches in a VDD port geometry are basically smoothed out. In effect, the extraction for power-grid analysis would typically run faster because it has to deal with less geometry. The side effects would some time cause disconnects or opens typically in tie-hi and/or tie-low types of connection.The default in power-grid analysis flow is true. If optimized view does not exist in the cell library, QX will load the non-optimized one.
Note: The optimization, which is done in LibGen, is only performed for cells which are smaller than the setting of the stdCellSizeLimit LibGen variable.

data: When you set this variable to true , zx loads the optimized version of the power-grid views from the cell library. The default is true in a power-grid analysis flow. If an optimized view does not exist in the cell library, zx will load the non-optimized one.
When you set this variable to false , zx does not load the optimized views

data:
number 
 must be an integer in the range of 1 through 2,147,483,647, inclusive. The default is 100.

lines: The following example sets the maximum number of error messages at 250:
The max_resistor_length variable specifies the maximum resistor length, in microns. To maintain accuracy, zx may need to fracture large resistances into multiple smaller resistances.

data:
number 
 can be from 10 to 800 microns, inclusive. The default is 50 microns. Smaller values increase the accuracy of the capacitance extraction, decrease capacity, and increase run times.

The message_detail_level variable sets the maximum number of warning messages that zx issues during a session.

data:
number 
 must be an integer from 1 through 2147483647, inclusive. The default is 10.

lines: The following example sets the maximum number of warning messages at 75:

The metal_fill_type variable specifies the type of metal fill to use, either grounded, which has a fixed potential, or floating, which has a fixed electrical charge. Typically, grounded metal has a higher capacitance.

Note: The variable enable_metal_fill_effects must be set to true , its default, for this variable, metal_fill_type to have any effect. If enable_metal_fill_effects is set to false , zx ignores metal fill if it is present in the DEF file or GDSII file.
[WARNING] Do not set this variable to grounded only to obtain higher capacitance for downstream tools.

data: When you set this variable to grounded , zx models the metal fill as grounded.
When you set this variable to floating , the tool models the metal fill as floating. The default is floating .

The temperature of the analysis point stored in the oaOpPoint will be set if it is defined either in the command file or in the technology file
oa_analysis_point can be used for dumping multiple parasitic network generated from different zx runs in the same OA database.

 Note: zx will create the specified directory if it does not already exist.

The output_drawn_geometry variable determines whether drawn resistor widths and lengths should output (not scaled).

data:
A value of true causes the drawn resistor lengths and widths to be output (not scaled). 
 The default is false.

lines: This example specifies that resistor widths and lengths are output as drawn.

lines: This example sets gnu as the name of the output files:

The output_incomplete_net s variable determines whether zx includes the parasitics of any open nets (for example partially routed or incomplete networks) in the parasitic databases for the analysis tool.

data: When you set this variable to true , zx includes open nets in the parasitic databases
When you set this variable to false , the tool does not include open nets. The default is false .

lines: Note: When incomplete nets are not written out (output_incomplete_nets false), coupling to those nets will be decoupled.
The following example includes open nets in the parasitic databases:
The output_oa variable controls whether or not zx writes the parasitics back to the OpenAccess (OA) database which was read from as input.
Note: In order for zx to process an OA database, it is required that the design input only come from an OA database.
Note: If an OA output is specified, but the OA input is hierarchical, a flattened SPEF file containing the extraction data will be created instead.

data: When set to true, zx will write the parasitics to the OA database. This is the default if OA input was specified.
When set to false, zx does not write the parasitics to the OA database.

lines: The following example specifies that zx is to write the parasitics back into the OA database.

The r emove_dangling_resistors variable determines whether zx removes floating metals--that is, metals that have no outputs or inputs--from the RC extraction. The purpose of this variable is to get rid of the resistors that are only connected on one end. It only has an impact on resistance and not capacitance.

Note: This variable was previously named 
 remove_floating_metals 
 .
Note: Setting this variable to false will generate version 3.3.2 comparable networks.

data: Removes floating metals from the RC extraction. The default is true.
Retains floating metals from the RC extraction.

lines: This example removes floating metals from the RC extraction:
The tech_file variable specifies the name of the technology file to be used by zx. This file is generated by RCgen, either by you or by the foundry.
Note: LibGen can also place a copy of this technology file in the cell library directory. zx will then use that file if the tech_file variable is not set in the zx command file.

data: Name of the technology file. There is no default file name.

The top_level_names_only variable is used if you want to preserve only the top level net names of a hierarchical design, and ignore any lower level names. This is only applicable to GDSII data.

data: Set this variable to true to preserve only the top level net names.
This preserves the lower level net names also.

lines: The following example preserves only the top level net names:
The use_def_over_library variable determines if ZX will use the DEFdata instead of .cl.

data: Set this variable to true to specify to use DEF data instead of .cl. The default is true .
Set this variable to false to specify to use .cl data.

lines: The following example specifies that DEF data should be used instead of .cl:

The use_layer_bias_in_cmd_and_process_bias_in_tech variable determines whether zx applies the layer biasing in the technology file, or in both the command file and the technology file to the wires in the design.

data: When you set this variable to true , zx applies the layer biasing in the command file and the technology file to the wires.
When you set it to false , zx applies the layer biasing in the technology file only. The default is false .

The use_manhattan_ports variable determines if zx will promote and convert the non-manh macro port geometry to manhattan geometry.

data: When you set this variable to true , zx will promote and convert the non-manh macro port geometry to manhattan geometry. It will give a better correlation to the pure manhattan top level design without any non-manh routing.
When you set it to false , zx will not promote and convert the non-manh macro port geometry to manhattan geometry . The default is false .

The wee_type variable determines whether to include the effects of wire-edge enlargement on resistance, capacitance, or both in the extraction results.

data: Extracts only resistance effects due to wire-edge enlargement.
Extracts only capacitance effects due to wire-edge enlargement.
Extracts resistance and capacitance effects due to wire-edge enlargement. The default is rc .
Does not extract resistance or capacitance effects due to wire-edge enlargement.

One usage is to force design cell names to correspond to library names - this is a more common problem in GDS2 designs where uppercase cell names do not match lowercase library names. Another usage is to change a design cell that is not intended to be mapped to a library view.
Specifies how to cluster or re-cluster via arrays. Since the clustering approach reduces the RC elements by clustering vias into one node, it can boost the IR drop analysis runtime in dynamic mode.

data: Specifies the name of the via layer.

Specifies that all via geometries on the specified via layer within 
 distance 
 , in microns, of other via geometries will be clustered together into a multi-cut via.

Specifies that if the resulting cluster exceeds the 
 max_dimension 
 in either number of rows or columns of vias it will be split in half repeatedly until the maximum number of rows or columns, per cluster, is less than or equal to the 
 max_dimension 
 .

The extraction_type command controls the type of extraction performed on the nets and selects the nets on which to perform the extraction. By default , Fire & Ice QX extracts all regular nets as a decoupled RC network.

data:
Extracts all nets listed in the 
 filename 
 file. 
 Note: f you select this option, you should first turn off the default value by setting the all option to none , as in the following example, unless you want to extract in two different modes, such as coupled and decoupled:

Selects all nets whose names contain the pattern specified by 
 pattern 
 . 
 pattern 
 uses the DEF regular expression syntax, which follows:
Matches any sequence of 0 or more characters. For example, *net* matches 123netxyz , netvdd , and 10ce463pnet .

Matches any single character. 
 For example, bus% matches bus1 , bus2 , bus3 , and so forth. Similarly, clock%test matches clock7test , clock8test , and clock9test , but not clock10test .

Matches the whole sequence of characters up to and including the next period ("."). If there is no "." in the string, it matches to the end of the string. 
 For example, --bus3 matches bus3 in the following expression: block1.cell2.bus3
Specifies the type of RC extraction. The type of RC extraction can be one of the following:
Extracts the decoupled resistance and capacitance network. This option is the default .
Performs no extraction on the defined set of nets.
Specifies the type of capacitance extracted. The type of capacitance extraction can be one of the following:

lines: Note: There is no limit to the number of extraction_type commands that you can define in a single command file, so you can use them repeatedly to fully describe the nets to extract and completely control the extraction. Later specifications override earlier ones if they select the same nets. Note: By default , zx extracts all regular nets as a decoupled RC network. Reduction of the output is controlled by the reduce_output variable. Note: zx detects power and ground nets from the DEF file by their +USE POWER and +USE GROUND attributes. It excludes these nets from the extraction unless you explicitly request that they be extracted by using their name or a matching pattern with the extraction_type command.

The layer command maps the layer names in the LEF technology file to the layer names in the IceCaps technology file or the GDSII file used to import metal fill. You only need to use this command if the layer names specified in the IceCaps technology file or the GDSII file are different from those used in the LEF file.

data: Specifies the name of the layer in the LEF file.
Maps between a layer in the LEF file and a layer in the IceCaps technology file or the RCgen technology file.
Maps between a layer in the LEF file and the GDSII file.
Specifies the layer number of the GDSII layer.

The layer bias command sets a bias value, in microns, by which to undersize all wires on a defined layer. It must be in the range of -1 to 1. This value is applied to each edge of the wire, decreasing the width by two times the bias value. The biasing changes only the width of the wire, not the length. You can select a different bias value for resistance extraction and capacitance extraction.

data: Specifies the name of the layer containing the wires to be biased.
Specifies the amount, in microns, by which to bias the wires. A positive value undersizes the width of the wire.
Applies the bias value only during resistance extraction.
Applies the bias value only during capacitance extraction.
Applies the bias value during both resistance and capacitance extraction.

lines: The following example decreases each edge of the wires in metal5 by one tenth of a micron during resistance extraction, and by two tenths of a micron during capacitance extraction:

This command associates a 
 layer_name 
 with the integer layer value 
 gds_layer_num 
 found in the GDS2 data. This layer name comes from the extraction technology file.

All layers in GDS2 format, to be used for extraction, must be defined with the layer gds command. Multiple commands may use the same 
 layer_name 
 in order to combine data in GDS2 with different layer or datatype fields into a single extraction layer.

The GDS2 format defines legal values for both layers and datatypes as 0 to 255 inclusive. Any combination of layer gds statements may be used with these values for layer and datatype. Omission of any datatype information implies that all datatypes for that layer are used. Single values and ranges for datatypes may also be specified for each layer gds statement.

data: Specifies the name of the layer
Specifies an optional single gds2 datatype number, or the first datatype number for when a range is specified
Specifies a gds2 datatype range, specified as a third number.
Keyword used to include GDS2 text objects (all texttypes) as port definitions for the layer

Keyword used to transfer text from GDS2 text objects (all texttypes) as a net name annotation to the geometry enclosing the text location. There is no difference between the 
 port 
 and 
 net 
 keywords - they are duplicated in order to get consistent terminology with the label net command.
Keyword indicating that all GDS2 text on the given layer is to be read (including all texttypes) and interpreted as labels
Keyword indicating that geometries with the given layer/datatype values are to be considered to be explicit fill data
Keyword indicating that geometries with the given layer/datatype values are to be considered as explicit slotting data

lines: The extraction technology file contains information for a layer called MET1. The information for this layer is read from the GDS2 file on integer layer 8. The corresponding command file would contain the following:

This single statement would be sufficient to get data from this layer in the GDS2 to the extractor on the MET1 layer. The data used for this layer includes all polygons and paths (of all pathtypes). If the technology file was changed to use METAL1 as the layer name instead of MET1, then in the command file you could either change the layer gds command or add an additional layer map command:
The layer map command can also be used to associate DEF layer names to technology layer names when they do not match.

Any number of layer gds commands may be used. Multiple statements with the same 
 layer_name 
 have the effect of combining data.
This example combines data from GDS2 layers 8 and 14 into a single extraction layer named MET1.
To specify a range of datatypes for a particular layer, you enter the beginning and ending datatype numbers.
This command associates GDS layer number 8 with the layer name MET1, for all data types numbered 2 to 7.

The layer map command is used to map a 
 layer_name 
 to a layer defined in the technology file. Layers with names that match technology layer names are mapped implicitly. Design layers may be excluded from extraction by mapping the 
 layer_name 
 to a name not defined in the technology file.

data: Name of the layer defined in the technology file

lines: Note: This example will discard data on METAL_8 from extraction.

The promote_ports command is used to control the use of port geometries during extraction.

data: The default value. Also, this is the default if no promote_ports command is found. This means that all port geometry will be subtracted from interconnect geometry and will not be used to generate parasitic values. This option will ensure that no cell parasitic is included in the interconnect model, but it could cause errors with respect to feedthru resistance or coupling capacitance to ports.

The all option merges all of the port geometries into the interconnect. This provides more accurate feedthru resistance and coupling capacitance, but could duplicate some parasitic effects included in the library model.

The cell 
 cell_name 
 pin 
 pin_name 
 specification is used to selectively include the port geometry of a specific pin from all instances of a cell.

The inst 
 inst_name 
 pin 
 pin_name 
 specification is used to selectively include the port geometry of a specific pin from a single instance.
Specifies the specific cell or instance pin.

lines: The first example below merges all port geometries into the interconnect: The second example promotes the port geometry of pin FT1, of cell R64. And the third example promotes pin Z, of instance IOA.
The density_check_method variable selects the method that zx uses to check the wire density of the chip.

data: Checks the wire density in the area defined by the diearea keyword in the DEF file. It checks the area on all layers. It checks the area by dividing it into a tile size defined by the technology file, usually 50 to 100 microns on a side. This option is recommended for full-chip checking.

Checks the wire density in the area defined by the layout database. This area is defined by a leftmost, bottommost point to a rightmost, topmost point. It checks the area on all layers. It checks the area by dividing it into a tile size defined by the technology file, usually 50 to 100 microns on a side. This option is recommended for block-level checking. The default is database .
Checks the wire density in the area defined by the extent of routing on each area of a layer. This option is recommended for validating layout accuracy only.
Checks the wire density in a rectangular region defined by the command density_check_rectangle .
Uses the default method, which is database .

lines: The following example checks the wire density in an area defined by the diearea keyword in the DEF file:
The dump_options variable saves the current zx variable settings in the log file (see log_file ).

data: Saves the variable settings to a log file.
Does not save the settings to a log file. The default is false

The enable_erosion_effects variable determines whether to include the effects of erosion on resistance, capacitance, or both in the extraction results.

data: Extracts only resistance effects due to erosion.
Extracts only capacitance effects due to erosion.
Extracts resistance and capacitance effects due to erosion. The default is rc .
Does not extract resistance or capacitance effects due to erosion.

lines: In the following example, zx includes the effects of erosion on capacitance in the extraction results:

The enable_metal_fill_effects variable determines whether zx includes in the extraction results the effects of the metal fill between wires on a layer that was deposited according to the specifications in the DEF file.
Note: zx issues a warning if the DEF file does not contain specifications for metal fill.

data: When you set this variable to true , zx includes the effects of metal fill between the wires. The default is true .

If you set the variable enable_metal_fill_effects to true , you must use the variable metal_fill_type to specify the type of metal fill to use, either grounded, which has a fixed electrical charge, or floating, which has no fixed electrical charge. Typically, grounded metal has a higher capacitance.
When you set this variable to false , zx does not include the effects of metal fill between the wires.

lines: In this example, zx does not include the effects of metal fill between the wires in the extraction results:
The enable_poly_routing variable enables zx to extract parasitic data for the layer type POLYCIDE.

data: When you set this variable to true , zx extracts parasitic data for the layer type POLYCIDE.
When you set this variable to false , zx will not extract parasitic data for the layer type POLYCIDE. The default is false.

When you set this variable to false , the tool does not establish resistivity values based on wire width.

When you set this variable to false , the tool does not extract the ports defined on the diffusion layer.

When you set this variable to false , zx does not ignore wires that have a SHAPE BLOCKAGE statement.

The gds_unit variable specifies what conversion factor zx is to use to convert GDS database units - used for geometric dimensions, coordinates, etc. - to microns.

The ignore_shorts variable enables zx to either flag or ignore detected shorted nets, as in previous releases. The default setting is true for DEF input, and f alse for other input. Setting it to true will make zx behave the same as in Version 3.x.

data: When set to true, if shorts are detected, they will be ignored and zx continues with some degree of inaccuracy.
When set to false, shorts will be flagged and zx will continue but with those nets shorted.

The layout_scale variable scales the dimensions in the layout by the scale factor that you specify. Using this option enables zx to correctly extract designs that have been sized by a scale factor.

data: Specifies the scale factor by which zx scales the layout. This factor can range from 0.01 to 5. The default is 1.0, indicating that no scaling is performed.

lines: This example scales the layout of the design by 25 percent:
The load_optimized_views variable specifies that zx load the optimized version of the power-grid views from the cell library. The load_optimized_views variable is used in power-grid analysis flow.

Optimized means that small notches in a VDD port geometry are basically smoothed out. In effect, the extraction for power-grid analysis would typically run faster because it has to deal with less geometry. The side effects would some time cause disconnects or opens typically in tie-hi and/or tie-low types of connection.The default in power-grid analysis flow is true. If optimized view does not exist in the cell library, QX will load the non-optimized one.
Note: The optimization, which is done in LibGen, is only performed for cells which are smaller than the setting of the stdCellSizeLimit LibGen variable.

data: When you set this variable to true , zx loads the optimized version of the power-grid views from the cell library. The default is true in a power-grid analysis flow. If an optimized view does not exist in the cell library, zx will load the non-optimized one.
When you set this variable to false , zx does not load the optimized views

The output_oa variable controls whether or not zx writes the parasitics back to the OpenAccess (OA) database which was read from as input.
Note: In order for zx to process an OA database, it is required that the design input only come from an OA database.
Note: If an OA output is specified, but the OA input is hierarchical, a flattened SPEF file containing the extraction data will be created instead.

data: When set to true, zx will write the parasitics to the OA database. This is the default if OA input was specified.
When set to false, zx does not write the parasitics to the OA database.

lines: The following example specifies that zx is to write the parasitics back into the OA database.

The r emove_dangling_resistors variable determines whether zx removes floating metals--that is, metals that have no outputs or inputs--from the RC extraction. The purpose of this variable is to get rid of the resistors that are only connected on one end. It only has an impact on resistance and not capacitance.

Note: This variable was previously named 
 remove_floating_metals 
 .
Note: Setting this variable to false will generate version 3.3.2 comparable networks.

data: Removes floating metals from the RC extraction. The default is true.
Retains floating metals from the RC extraction.

lines: This example removes floating metals from the RC extraction:
The tech_file variable specifies the name of the technology file to be used by zx. This file is generated by RCgen, either by you or by the foundry.
Note: LibGen can also place a copy of this technology file in the cell library directory. zx will then use that file if the tech_file variable is not set in the zx command file.

data: Name of the technology file. There is no default file name.

lines: The following line indicates that chip731.tch is the name of the technology file to be submitted to the tool:
The top_level_names_only variable is used if you want to preserve only the top level net names of a hierarchical design, and ignore any lower level names. This is only applicable to GDSII data.

data: Set this variable to true to preserve only the top level net names.
This preserves the lower level net names also.

lines: The following example preserves only the top level net names:
The use_def_over_library variable determines if ZX will use the DEFdata instead of .cl.

data: Set this variable to true to specify to use DEF data instead of .cl. The default is true .
Set this variable to false to specify to use .cl data.

lines: The following example specifies that DEF data should be used instead of .cl:

The use_layer_bias_in_cmd_and_process_bias_in_tech variable determines whether zx applies the layer biasing in the technology file, or in both the command file and the technology file to the wires in the design.

data: When you set this variable to true , zx applies the layer biasing in the command file and the technology file to the wires.
When you set it to false , zx applies the layer biasing in the technology file only. The default is false .

lines: The following example applies layer biasing in the technology file to the wires in the design:
The use_manhattan_ports variable determines if zx will promote and convert the non-manh macro port geometry to manhattan geometry.

data: When you set this variable to true , zx will promote and convert the non-manh macro port geometry to manhattan geometry. It will give a better correlation to the pure manhattan top level design without any non-manh routing.
When you set it to false , zx will not promote and convert the non-manh macro port geometry to manhattan geometry . The default is false .

lines: The following example specifies that zx will promote and convert the non-manh macro port geometry to manhattan geometry:
The wee_type variable determines whether to include the effects of wire-edge enlargement on resistance, capacitance, or both in the extraction results.

data: Extracts only resistance effects due to wire-edge enlargement.
Extracts only capacitance effects due to wire-edge enlargement.
Extracts resistance and capacitance effects due to wire-edge enlargement. The default is rc .
Does not extract resistance or capacitance effects due to wire-edge enlargement.

lines: In the following example, zx extracts only resistance effects due to wire-edge enlargement:

This section lists the advanced Library Generation commands and variables that can be included in Voltus using the set_advanced_pg_library_mode - libgen_command_file command parameter. They are listed in alphabetical order.

This command specifies the amount of area based decoupling capacitance in a cell. This command is used in generating dynamic port or detailed power-grid views. Similar to "cell_decap" command, if only a port view is created for a cell, the capacitance specified by the "cell_decap_area" command is evenly distributed over the taps in the port view. For detailed views this is different than using the "cell_decap" command, in that the capacitance value assigned by using this command will not be used to scale the capacitance value calculated, since it is assumed that the value calculated by Thunder is more accurate.
The capacitance specified will be used for both the POWER and GROUND nets. If there are multiple POWER nets or GROUND nets, this value will be evenly distributed among those nets.

Note: If user wants to specify a decap value for a GENERIC supply net, one must specify it explicitly onto the net. If user specifies the decap value for the cell it will be applied on only the POWER and GROUND net. If the GENERIC supply net existed in the cell, LibGen will issue an INFO message to indicate that the value will not be used for the GENERIC supply net. If the user specifies the decap value for the cell, and only a generic supply net exists in the cell, LibGen will apply that value onto the generic supply net and issue a warning message.

data: Lists the cell names used to assign a capacitance value. The cell name supports wildcard characters (e.g. * ?).
Specifies the amount of area based decoupling capacitance. Default unit is femtofarads per square micron.
For specifying an optional parameter used to specify the unit of length. LibGen will use square "unit" as the area unit internally. Default unit is micron.

lines: - The following example specifies a decoupling capacitance of 5 femtofarads per square micron for cells starting with letters NTZ: cell_decap_area NTZ* 5
This command specifies the name of a file containing a list of cells and the area based decoupling capacitance value for each.

data: The name of the area based decoupling capacitance file.

lines: Note: If user wants to specify a decap value for a GENERIC supply net, one must specify it explicitly onto the net. If user specifies the decap value for the cell it will be applied on only the POWER and GROUND net. If the GENERIC supply net existed in the cell, LibGen will issue an INFO message to indicate that the value will not be used for the GENERIC supply net. If the user specifies the decap value for the cell, and only a generic supply net exists in the cell, LibGen will apply that value onto the generic supply net and issue a warning message.
The lines of the area based decoupling capacitance file are as follows:

- CAP_UNIT unit_in_farads The UNIT definition is optional. If no UNIT statement is present at the beginning of the file, all capacitance values not followed by a unit specification are assumed to be in femtofarads.
- LENGTH_UNIT unit_in_meters The LENGTH_UNIT is optional. Default length unit is microns

- CELL cell_name cap_value cell_name specifies the name of the cell. cap_value specifies the amount of area based decoupling capacitance. The default unit is femtofarads per square micron. The cellname can contain wildcard characters(e.g. * ?). All capacitance values can be followed by a unit specification. The following units are recognized: millifarads (mF), microfarads (μF), nanofarads (nF), picofarads (pF), femtofarads (fF). If specified this will take priority over the CAP_UNIT specification.
- The following example specifies a file named decap_cell_list that contains a list of cells and the area based decoupling capacitance for each. cell_decap_area_file decap_cell_list

- The following examples illustrate the contents of the area based decoupling capacitance file. This line in the decoupling capacitance file is optional. It assumes that the default units are femtofarads. CAP_UNIT 1.0e-15 In the next part of the file, the area based decoupling capacitance for Cell_A is 100 femtofarads per square micron. CELL Cell_A 100.00 The capacitance specified will be used for both the POWER and GROUND nets. If there are multiple POWER nets or GROUND nets, this value will be evenly distributed among those nets.
cell_ground_names [cell [ cellname +] nets [ ground_net_name +} value [ supply_value ] < bulk >

This command specifies the net names to be used as the ground nets for the named cells and the nominal voltage of those nets. Use this command when any of the generate_*_powerview variables are set to true , that is, when LibGen generates power-grid views.
When you specify the names of cells with cell_ground_names , you must also specify them with cell_power_names . See "cell_power_names" for information on this command.

Note: LibGen only takes nets listed by the cell_ground_names or cell_power_names command as supply nets and treats all other nets as signal nets. This is done even if those nets are listed by the generic_ground_names or generic_power_names commands. If the cell is not in the cell_ground_names or cell_power_names list, LibGen will use LEF and generic_ground_names or generic_power_names settings.

data: Identifies the cell or cells to which the ground net supplies power. No wildcards are allowed for the specification of cell names.
Identifies the ground net or nets that supply power to the named cells. No wildcards are allowed for the specification of net names

Gives the nominal voltage of the ground nets. If you specify the ground net names to use for cells, you must also specify the power net names for those same cells. Refer to "cell_power_names" for more information on the cell_power_names command.
Indicates that all nets specified by the cell_power_names command be considered bulk nets. No current will be distributed to these nets.

lines: - The next example specifies that the gnd ground net supplies a nominal voltage of 0.0 volts to the block_a cell: cell_ground_names cell {block_a} net {gnd} value 0.0
- The next example indicates that the gnd and gnd1 nets supply a nominal voltage of 0.0 volts to the na cell: cell_ground_names cell {na} net {gnd gnd1} value 0.0
- The next example indicates that the gnd_bulk net is a bulk net: cell_ground_names cell {na} net {gnd_bulk} value 0.0 bulk

This command lists the cells that LibGen is not to process. LibGen processes any cell specified by the cell_list or cell_list_file command, unless it is specified by the cell_ignore_list or cell_ignore_list_file command.

data: Specifies the names of the cells that LibGen is not to process.

lines: - In the following example, LibGen will process all cells given by the cell_list or cell_list_file command, except the MUX21X05, MUX22, and MUX01FT3 cells: cell_ignore_list {MUX21X05 MUX22 MUX01FT3}
- An example, if you wanted to process all cells in the design except one named mem_blk1, you would put the following lines in the LibGen command file: cell_list {*} cell_ignore_list {mem_blk1}
This command specifies the name of a file containing a list of cells that LibGen is not to process.

data: The name of a file containing the list of cells that LibGen is to ignore

lines: - The following line specifies a file named ignore_cells.dat , which contains a list of the cells that LibGen is to ignore: cell_ignore_list_file ignore_cells.dat
This command specifies the mapping between a cel l's LEF pin names and the internal names of the net to which those pins are attached. It is used when multiple pins are attached to a single net.

data: Specifies the name of the cell or cells containing multiple pins attached to a single net. This name can include the * and ? wildcard characters.
Specifies the internal name of the net to which multiple pins are attached.
Specifies the LEF name of the pins attached to a single net.

lines: - The following example maps the LEF pin names vdd1, vdd2, and vdd3 to the internal net name vdd in the block_a cell: cell_pin_net_map {block_a} vdd {vdd1 vdd2 vdd3}
- The next example maps the names of any LEF pins starting with vdd to the internal net name vdd in any cell whose name starts with block: cell_pin_net_map {block*} vdd {vdd*}
This command specifies the name of a file containing the mappings between a cell's LEF pin names and the internal names of the net to which those pins are attached.

data: The name of the file containing the mappings. Its format is one net mapping per line.

lines: Each line is as follows for the cell pin net map file:
Cellname and pin_name can include the * and ? wildcard characters.
- The following example specifies the pin_map.txt file as the mapping file: cell_pin_net_map_file pin_map.txt
cell_power_names [cell [ cellname +] nets [ power_net_name +] value supply_value < bulk >

This command specifies the net names to be used as the power nets for the named cells and the nominal voltage of those nets. Use this command when any of the generate_*_powerview variables are set to true , that is, when LibGen generates power-grid views.
When you specify the names of cells with cell_power_names , you must also specify them with cell_ground_names . See "cell_ground_names" for information on this command.

Note: LibGen only takes nets listed by the cell_ground_names or cell_power_names command as supply nets and treats all other nets as signal nets. This is done even if those nets are listed by the generic_ground_names or generic_power_names commands. If the cell is not in the cell_ground_names or cell_power_names list, LibGen will use LEF and generic_ground_names or generic_power_names settings.

data: Identifies the cell or cells to which the power net supplies power. No wildcards are allowed for the specification of cell names.
Identifies the power net or nets that supply power to the named cells. No wildcards are allowed for the specification of net names.

Gives the nominal voltage of the power net. If you specify the power net names to use for cells, you must also specify the ground net names for those same cells. Refer to "cell_ground_names" for more information on the cell_ground_names command.
Indicates that all nets specified by the cell_power_names command be considered bulk nets. No current will be distributed to these nets.

lines: - The following example specifies that the VCC net is the power net for the nand2 cell and that its nominal voltage is 1.0: cell_power_names cell {nand2} net {vcc} value 1.0
- The next example indicates that the VCC and VCC1 nets supply a nominal voltage of 1.0 volts to the na cell: cell_power_names cell {na} net {vcc vcc2} value 1.0
- The next examples indicates that the pwr_bulk and grd_bulk are a bulk nets: cell_power_names cell {na} net {pwr_bulk} value 1.0 bulk cell_power_names cell {na} net {gnd_bulk} value 0.0 bulk

This command specifies the name of the file containing the names of the power and ground nets and their nominal supply values for one or more cells. This file is in ASCII format. Use this command when any of the generate_*_powerview variables are set to true , that is, when LibGen generates detailed power-grid views.

data: The name of the file containing the names of the power and ground nets and their supply values.

lines: The format of the cell supply names file is as follows.
- Use the CELLS keyword, then specify a list of cells that the next POWER and GROUND statements refer to.
- Use the POWER keyword, then specify a voltage value and a list of net names to be used as the power connections for the previously specified cells.
- Use the GROUND keyword, then specify a voltage value and a list of net names to be used as the ground connections for the previously specified cells.
- Use the GENERIC keyword, then specify a list of net names to be used as the generic supply connections which could be connected to either power or ground for the previously specified cells.

The file can contain multiple CELL , POWER , GROUND,and GENERIC statements. No wildcards are allowed for the specification of cell names and net names. LibGen ignores blank lines and lines beginning with the pound sign (#).

Note: If user wants to specify a decap value for a GENERIC supply net, one must specify it explicitly onto the net. If user specifies the decap value for the cell it will be applied on only the POWER and GROUND net. If the GENERIC supply net existed in the cell, LibGen will issue an INFO message to indicate that the value will not be used for the GENERIC supply net. If the user specifies the decap value for the cell, and only a generic supply net exists in the cell, LibGen will apply that value onto the generic supply net and issue a warning message.
- The following example identifies the pwrnets.file as the file containing the names of the power and ground nets: cell_supply_names_file pwrnets.file
current_region <PROP [FLAT | HIER]> cell_name net_name layer_name x1 y1 x2 y2 current_value < cap_value >
current_region cell_name net_name layer_name current_value < cap_value >
This command allows the user to specify an amount of current to be distributed over an area of the power grid.

The input from the current_region_file command and the current_region command will be combined together. See also current_region_file . If there are any conflicts between the commands, the first one specified will be used and a warning will be generated.

The current_region and current_region file will override any values calculated by Thunder/Makedpm . See makedpm chapter of this manual for a discussion on makedpm. The current_region_default will only be used for those cells which are not specified by current_region, current_region_file, or by Thunder/Makedpm .

data:
If the keyword FLAT is applied, LibGen will not perform hierarchical tracing for the given net in this cell. 
 If the keyword HIER is presented, LibGen will perform hierarchical tracing for the given net in this cell. This provides the ability for LibGen to automatically specify a current value to all instances of a cell in the design hierarchy.
