 If the keyword HIER is presented, LibGen will perform hierarchical tracing for the given net in this cell. This provides the ability for LibGen to automatically specify a current value to all instances of a cell in the design hierarchy. The assumption is that all of these instances of the cell will consume the same power. This is used with Quick Detailed View generation. 
 The default is HIER.
Specifies the name of the cell. Wildcards can be used in the cell_name.
Specifies the name of the net. Wildcards can be used in the net_name.

Specifies the name of a via or contact layer. Libgen will create a tap current on the conducting layer below this via or contact layer. The tap will be created on the circuit node attached to the via or contact. The value of the current drawn by the tap will be 1/n of the total current assigned to the current region, where n is the number of taps within the current region. The number of taps within a current region is the same as the number of vias or contacts on the specified layer. 
 The layer_name must be a library layer name. If the layer could not be found, LibGen will issue an error message and exit out. The user must provide a valid layer name for LibGen to run. 
 If the user specified a valid layer, but LibGen does not find any via in the assigned current region, LibGen will issue a warning message, but the process will still keep going and exit successfully. In this case, user may get a 0 current value for the specified cell. 
 LibGen will try to attach a current tap onto the conduct layer directly underneath it (which could be regular metal layer, poly layer or diffusion layer). If the conduct layer directly underneath can not be found, LibGen will search for the first metal layer above this via layer (here, the poly layer will not be considered as metal layer), and assign the current tap onto this layer. 
 If no XTC command file is provided, LibGen requires to use VIA_1 or higher as current region layer since contact layer is part of device forming layers. A LibGen automatically generated XTC command file is not able to handle device forming layers very well because of lack of information. Also, use VIA_1 or higher to reduce the resistor/capacitance count, which will improve the overall performance.

Bounding box coordinates for the region to distribute the current. Values are assumed to be in GDSII coordinate units and will be interpreted according to the scale and precision settings contained in the GDSII file that contains the cell. The default unit of x1 y1 x2 y2 is in database units.
Amount of current that will get distributed.
Optional capacitance value. Default is zero.

lines: Second form of syntax can be used only when there is only one current region specified for a cell. In this case Libgen can automatically use the bounding box of the cell for the region.
The following line specifies a cell called an4_80 , a net grd ,and layer CONT . The cell will have 1.0uA of current and 2.0fF of capacitance.

This command provides a default way to create current regions for cells without explicitly listing all the cell and net names by using the current_region or current_region_file commands. The settings provided by this command are only used for cells which do not have more specific information provided by a current_region or current_region_file commands. The current_region_default will also not override any values calculated by Thunder/Makedpm . See makedpm chapter of this manual for a discussion on makedpm.
This command should only be used when the device_recognition_required variable is set to false .

data:
Specifies the name of a via or contact layer. 
 The layer_name should be the name of the lowest via or contact layer in the design data. 
 For each power net of any cell which does not have specific current_region data defined, LibGen will place a current tap with the given values at the bottom node of each resistor on the specified layer. 
 Note that this is different than the current_region command, where each current tap received 1/n of the value, based on the number of taps being created for the cell. 
 It is mandatory to specify this parameter when you define multiple contact layers, to avoid any errrors due to layer conflict.

Amount of current that will get applied to each tap. The current value will be inverted when applied to ground nets in order to keep the polarity correct. 
 This is optional. It is not needed in lvs-neutral flow because libgen uses thunder calculated current. Not needed in regular spice netlist flow because it uses thunder calculated current and distributes it evenly.
Optional capacitance value. Default is zero. If supplied, the full capacitance value will be applied to each current tap created.

lines: - The following line specifies a current_region_default for layer CONT . Each tap will have 1.0uA of current and 2.0fF of capacitance. current_region_default CONT 1.0uA 2.0fF
This command allows the user to specify in a file a list of regions and the amount of current to be distributed within them for the power grid.

The input from the current_region_file command and the current_region command will be combined together. See also current_region . If there are any conflicts between the commands, the first one specified will be used and a warning will be generated.

The current_region and current_region file will override any values calculated by Thunder/Makedpm . See makedpm chapter of this manual for a discussion on makedpm. The current_region_default will only be used for those cells which are not specified by current_region, current_region_file, or by Thunder/Makedpm .

data: Specifies the name of the file that includes a list of current regions.

lines: - The following line specifies a current_region_file called adder_currents . current_region_file adder_currents

- The following shows the current_region_file format UNIT CURRENT default_unit_in_amps UNIT CAPACITANCE default_unit_in_farads CELL cell_name <PROP [FLAT | HIER]> NET net_name LAYER layer_name [ REGION x1 y1 x2 y2 current_value < cap_value > | REGION current_value < cap_value > ]
See also current_region for a related command.
- Example of current_region_file. UNIT CURRENT uA UNIT CAPACITANCE fF CELL an4_80 NET vcc LAYER CONT REGION 0 14000 28900 17000 3.0 5.0 NET gnd LAYER CONT REGION 1.5e-2mA
This command specifies the amount of decoupling capacitance in a cell. This command is used in generating dynamic port or detailed power-grid views.

If only a port view is created for a cell, the capacitance specified by the cell_decap command is evenly distributed over the taps in the port view. If a detailed view is created for this cell, the capacitance values of the taps calculated during the detailed view generation are scaled up or down as necessary so that the total capacitance of all the taps in the cell matches the value specified by this command. For a related command see cell_decap_area .

data: Specifies the name of the cell.
Specifies the amount of decoupling capacitance associated with the cell, in femtofarads.

lines: Note: If user wants to specify a decap value for a GENERIC supply net, one must specify it explicitly onto the net. If user specifies the decap value for the cell it will be applied on only the POWER and GROUND net. If the GENERIC supply net existed in the cell, LibGen will issue an INFO message to indicate that the value will not be used for the GENERIC supply net. If the user specifies the decap value for the cell, and only a generic supply net exists in the cell, LibGen will apply that value onto the generic supply net and issue a warning message.
- The following example specifies a decoupling capacitance of 5 picofarads for the NTZ3802 cell: cell_decap NTZ3802 5pF

This command specifies to LibGen the names of any extra derived layers, which are layers generated in XTC as a result of logical operations that XTC performs on the GDS layers. It is not necessary to use this command under normal circumstances, because LibGen automatically generates this data from the layer maps and the file specified by the gds_extractor_command_file variable. Its purpose is to assist in debugging.

data: Specifies the names of the extra derived layers.

lines: - The following line specifies that the DIFF, NIMP, and NDIFF layers are extra derived layers: derived_layer_list {DIFF NIMP NDIFF}

This command specifies a list of cells for which to delete the detailed power-grid view from the library. A detailed power-grid view is only deleted if an abstract power-grid view, reduced power-grid view, or both are generated for the same cell, whether or not the cell is included in the list. This command is useful if you are concerned about the amount of disk space required for the power-grid view libraries. However, deleting the detailed power-grid views limits your ability to perform electromigration analysis when you use this library. You cannot perform electromigration analysis on cells that do not have detailed power-grid views.

data: The name of a cell or a list of cells for which to delete detailed views from the library after an abstract view, reduced view, or both have been generated for the same cell.

lines: - In the following example, LibGen generates a detailed power-grid view library, then deletes the cell1 and cell2 cells: discard_detailed_powerview_cell_list {cell1 cell2}

This command specifies the name of a file containing the list of cells for which to delete the detailed power-grid view from the library. A detailed power-grid view is only deleted if an abstract power-grid view, reduced power-grid view, or both are generated for the same cell, whether or not the cell is included in the list. This command is useful if you are concerned about the amount of disk space required for the power-grid view libraries. However, deleting the detailed power-grid views limits your ability to perform electromigration analysis when you use this library. You cannot perform electromigration analysis on cells that do not have detailed power-grid views.

data: The name of the file containing a single-column list of cells for which to delete detailed views from the library after an abstract view, reduced view, or both have been generated for the same cell.

lines: - In the following example, LibGen generates a detailed power-grid view library, then deletes the cells listed in the dv_delete.list file: discard_detailed_powerview_cell_list_file dv_delete.list

This command allows you to map the foreign cell names specified in the LEF file to the corresponding cell names in the GDS file. This command is only necessary if the foreign names in the LEF file are different from the cell names in the GDS file.

data: Specifies the name of the cell in the LEF file.
Specifies the name of the cell in the GDS file.

lines: - The following example maps the foreign XAND2 cell in the LEF file to the AND2 cell in the GDS file: foreign_name_map XAND2 AND2

This command specifies the name of a file containing the foreign cell names in the LEF file and their corresponding cell names in the GDS file. This command is only necessary if the foreign names are different from the cell names in the GDS file.

data: The name of the file containing the list of LEF cell names and their associated GDS cell names.

lines: - This line indicates that lef2gds.map is the name of the file containing foreign LEF cell names and their corresponding GDS cell names: foreign_name_map_file lef2gds.map
This command specifies the names of the LEF layers that LibGen is to ignore when it creates the cell library.

data: The names of the LEF layers that LibGen is to ignore.

lines: - As a result of the following example, LibGen ignores the LEF metal1 and metal3 layers when it creates the cell library: lef_layer_ignore_list {metal1 metal3}
This command specifies the name of a file containing a list of the LEF layers that LibGen is to ignore when it creates the cell library. Each line in the file must consist of a single LEF layer name.

data: The name of the file containing a list of the LEF layers that LibGen is to ignore when it creates the cell library.

lines: - The following line indicates that a file named nolef.txt contains a list of the LEF layer names that LibGen should ignore: lef_layer_ignore_list_file {nolef.txt}
li layer [gds layernum < datatype > <port> <text_only|no_text>|lefdef lefdef_layername | pgdb pgdb_layer_name ]
- The li command maps between the layer name for local interconnect layers used in the LibGen command file and one of the following:
- It maps technology file local interconnect layers when there is a conflict in layer names between two libraries for the same process.

data: Specifies the name of the layer in the cell library.
Maps between the layer specified by layer and a GDSII layer.
Specifies the layer number of the GDSII layer.
Specifies the number of the subclass for the GDSII layer. It is optional. If you do not specify a datatype, LibGen uses all datatypes for that GDS layer.
Converts text labels in this layer to ports.
Includes only the text in the named layer and ignores all geometries.
Maps between the layer specified by layer and a LEF/DEF layer.
Specifies the name of the LEF/DEF layer.
Specifies the name of the pgdb layer.
Specifies the name of the layer in the first cell library.
Specifies the name of second library.
Specifies the name of the second library layer.

lines: - The following line maps between the LI layer in the LibGen command file and GDSII layer 49: li LI gds 49

- The next example would map the local_1 layer of blocks.cl library to the LI_1 layer present in the RCgen techfile provided as input to the LibGen run. It would also map the li_1 layer from the stdcells.cl library to the LI_1 layer. li LI_1 lib blocks.cl local_1 li LI_1 lib stdcells.cl li_1

This command specifies how LibGen uses data generated from VoltageStorm, VoltageStorm PE, or Lightning to create a detailed power-grid view. It uses the data in the power-grid database referenced by the header name to create a detailed power-grid view for the specified net of the specified cell. The header files used in this command are created by the generate_view command in VoltageStorm and VoltageStorm PE or by the prepare_view_generation command in Lightning. These header files typically end with the .vhdr extension.

data: Specifies the name of the LEF cell input to LibGen.
Specifies the name of the net generated in the power-grid view.
Specifies the name of the VoltageStorm, VoltageStorm PE, or Lightning header file referencing the name of the power-grid database to use in generating the power-grid view.

lines: - The following example specifies that LibGen use the data in the CELL2 cell and in the power-grid database referenced by the header file named work/VDD_CELL2.vhdr to create a power-grid view. The VDD net will be generated in this power-grid view. pgv_header_map CELL2 VDD work/VDD_CELL2.vhdr
This command is similar to the pgv_header_map command, but it reads the data for cell, net, and header from a given file.
Each line of this file should contain a cell name, a net name, and a header file name.

data: The name of the file containing the cell name, net name, and header file name required to create a power-grid view.

lines: - The following command specifies a header map file named NFD4Q_pgv : pgv_header_map_file NFD4Q_pgv This file might contain these lines: CELL2 VDD work/VDD_CELL2.vhdr CELL2 VSS work/VSS_CELL2.vhdr CELL3 VSS work/VDD_CELL3.vhdr CELL3 GND work/GND_CELL3.vhdr
poly layer [gds layernum < datatype > <port> <text_only|no_text>|lefdef lefdef_layername | pgdb pgdb_layer_name ]
- The oly command maps between the layer name for polycide layers used in the LibGen command file and one of the following:
- It maps technology file poly layers when there is a conflict in layer names between two libraries for the same process.

data: Specifies the name of the layer in the cell library.
Maps between the layer specified by layer and a GDSII layer.
Specifies the layer number of the GDSII layer.
Is an optional number of the subclass for the GDSII layer. If you do not specify a datatype, LibGen uses all datatypes for that GDS layer.
Converts text labels in this layer to ports.
Includes only the text in the named layer and ignores all geometries.
Maps between layer specified by layer and a LEF/DEF layer.
Specifies the name of the LEF/DEF layer
Specifies the name of the pgdb layer. See examples and the end of this section
Specifies the name of the layer in the first cell library.
Specifies the name of second library.
Specifies the name of the second library layer.

lines: - The following line maps between the POLYCIDE layer in the LibGen command file and GDSII layer 46: poly POLYCIDE gds 46 The first line of the following example maps the POLY01 layer in LibGen command file to the LEF/DEF layer POLY_1. The second line maps the POLY01 layer in LibGen command to pgdb poly1 layer. This is an example where some cells were done using VoltageStorm Cell-Level (lefdef) while others were done using VoltageStorm Transistor-Level (pgdb) and both poly names needed to be mapped. poly POLY01 lefdef POLY_1 poly POLY01 pgdb poly1

- The next example would map the poly_1 layer of blocks.cl library to the POLY_1 layer present in the RCgen techfile provided as input to the LibGen run. It would also map the P_1 layer from the stdcells.cl library to the POLY_1 layer. metal POLY_1 lib blocks.cl poly_1 metal POLY_1 lib stdcells.cl P_1

Thiscommand specifies a file that contains all the information that LibGen needs to create a port power gate view. This includes the names of the power gate cells, the names of the pins in the power gate cells, the on-resistance of the power gate, and the saturation current of the power gate.

data: Specifies the name of the text file that defines the port power gate information.

lines: The following is the format for a port power gate file.
cell cellname supply unswitched_pin_name switched switched_pin_name ron r_value <idsat idsat_value> <ileak ileak_value>
For more complex power gate cells that have multiple switched pins, the cell name is specified first on a line by itself and then the data for the individual power gates is specified.
supply pin1 switched pin2 ron r_ value idsat idsat_ value ileak leak_ value
All resistance values are in ohms and current values in milliamps if not specified.
- The following example specifies the port power gate file called powergates.txt : port_power_gate_file powergates.txt
- Example of port power gate file contents: cell hs65_ls_sw3l60 supply vddalways switched vddswitch ron 3.5 cell pg1 supply vdd switched vdd2 ron 3.5 idsat 15ma ileak 0.1ma ...

This command specifies the name of the port view current file, which contains current distribution information that LibGen can use when it generates port power-grid views. In the floorplanning stage of the design, you may need to distribute current unevenly among the ports. The standard LibGen flow for generating port power-grid views generates a port power-grid view based only on LEF data and assigns each interface node the same unit tap current value. When you know roughly how the current is going to be distributed, you can use a port view current file to specify the current values to assign to the pins to more accurately reflect the current distribution. LibGen distributes the currents automatically when it generates the port power-grid views.

data: Specifies the name of the port view current file.

lines: The port view current file is in the following format:
- cell_name Specifies the name of the macro found in the LEF file.
- pin_name Specifies the name of pin to which the current is being distributed.
- lef_layer_name Specifies the name of the LEF layer on which the current should be placed.
- x , y Are the coordinates within the specific port area (partition). These values are specified in microns and use the same coordinate system as the LEF port data.

- current_value or current_distribution_ratio (based on the use model) Specifies the current value in amperes, or the current distribution ratio ( current distributed on different partitions of a LEF port ). This value is a floating-point number. You can specify the current value in milliamperes (m), microamperes (μ), or nanoamperes (n).
Lines beginning with the pound sign (#) in the port view current file are comment lines.
- The following examples specifies a port view current file called NA310port_pgv.txt : port_view_current_file NA310port_pgv.txt

- As an example of the port view current file, suppose the VDD rail of the AN2 cell has three sets of connected port geometries (partitions) on the upper, middle, and lower portions of a layout. You can create a port view current file such as the following to specify the current distribution: #Current distribution data for cell AN2 CELL AN2 PIN VDD M1 0.0 10.0 1m M2 0.0 5.0 2u M3 0.0 0.0 1.5n After LibGen generates the interface nodes, it assigns 1 milliampere to the interface nodes on first partition, 2 microamperes to the second partition, and 1.5 nanoamperes to the third partition. In each partition, the current value is divided evenly among the interface nodes. For pins in which no ports were specified as having specific current values, all ports are assigned the default current.
- The following is an example of a port view current file with 2 cells: 
 CELL CELL1 PIN VDD M0 0.0285 0.24 1.09222e-05 PIN VDD M0 0.0285 0.72 1.06755e-05 PIN VDD M0 0.0285 1.68 0.000178628 
 CELL CELL2 PIN VDD M0 0.0285 0.24 1.09222e-05 PIN VDD M0 0.0285 0.72 1.06755e-05 PIN VDD M0 0.0285 1.68 0.000178628
Provides a current scaling factor for a specific region.

data: Specifies there coordinates of the current region that the scale factor will be applied to. Units are in Angstroms

Specifies a scale factor. The factor will be applied to the specified region . If one want to specify a quiet region (one with zero current) then the scale factor should be set to 0. 
 Scaling is applied to all the nodes falling in the region and currents reported by thunder are multiples by the scale factor. The scale factor can be 0 or any positive number 
 You can also specify a default scaling factor using the scale_current_region_default command.

lines: - The below command specifies a scale factor of 2 for the region defined by the coordinates provided. scale_current_region 0 0 70000 70000 2
.Specifies a current scaling factor for everything that is not defined by scale_current_region command.

data: Specifies a global scale factor. If global scaling is used, region specific scaling would override global scaling for that region.

lines: - The below command specifies a scale factor of 3 for the any region not defined the scale_current_region command. scale_current_region_default 3
This command specifies the location of the set of Thunder model tables that LibGen uses to generate the current data for the cells in the library.

data: The name of the directory containing the Thunder model tables

lines: - The following example identifies the location of the Thunder model tables: thunder_model_table_directory ../tables/typical_1.2 thunder_model_table_directory ../tables/typical_2.5
well layer [gds layernum < datatype > <port> <text_only|no_text>|lefdef lefdef_layername | pgdb pgdb_layer_name ]
- The ell command maps between the layer name for well layers used in the LibGen command file and either:
- It maps technology file well layers when there is a conflict in layer names between two libraries for the same process.

data: Specifies the name of the layer in the cell library.
Maps between the layer specified by layer and a GDSII layer.
Specifies the layer number of the GDSII layer.
Is an optional number of the subclass for the GDSII layer. If you do not specify a datatype, LibGen uses all datatypes for that GDS layer.
Converts text labels in this layer to ports.
Includes only the text in the named layer and ignores all geometries.
Maps between layer specified by layer and a LEF/DEF layer.
Specifies the name of the LEF/DEF layer
Specifies the name of the pgdb layer. See examples and the end of this section
Specifies the name of the layer in the first cell library.
Specifies the name of second library.
Specifies the name of the second library layer.

lines: - The following line maps between the N_WELL layer in the LibGen command file and GDSII layer 42: well N_WELL gds 42

- The next example would map the well_1 layer of blocks.cl library to the WELL_1 layer present in the RCgen techfile provided as input to the LibGen run. It would also map the W_1 layer from the stdcells.cl library to the WELL_1 layer. well WELL_1 lib blocks.cl well_1 well WELL_1 lib stdcells.cl w_1

Specifies the amount of area based well capacitance in a cell. This command is used in generating dynamic port or detailed power-grid views. Just like the well_cap command, if only a port view is created for a cell, the capacitance specified by the well_cap_area command is evenly distributed and added to the decap taps in the port view. The capacitance specified will be used for both the POWER and GROUND nets. If there are multiple POWER nets or GROUND nets, this value will be evenly distributed among those nets. For a related command see well_cap_area_file .

data: Specifies the amount of well capacitance associated with the cell, in femtofarads per square micron. All capacitance values can be followed by a unit specification. The following units are recognized: millifarads (mF), microfarads (μF), nanofarads (nF), picofarads (pF), femtofarads (fF).

lines: - The following example specifies a well capacitance of 1 picofarads per square micron for the NTZ3802 cell: well_cap_area NTZ3802 1pF
This command specifies the name of a file containing a list of cells and the area based well capacitance. For a related command see well_cap_area .

data: The name of the well cap area file, which is described in below.

lines: The well capacitance area file format:
The lines of the well capacitance area file can be of one of the following formats:

- CAP_UNIT unit_in_farads The CAP_UNIT definition is optional. If no CAP_UNIT statement is present at the beginning of the file, all capacitance values not followed by a unit specification are assumed to be in femtofarads.

- LENGTH_UNIT unit_in_meters The LENGTH_UNIT definition is optional. If no LENGTH_UNIT statement is present at the beginning of the file, all length values not followed by a unit specification are assumed to be in microns.

- CELL cell_name cap_value cell_name specifies the name of the cell. cap_value specifies the amount of area based well capacitance. The default unit is femtofarads per square micron. The cell name can contain wildcard characters (e.g. * ?). All capacitance values can be followed by a unit specification. The following units are recognized: millifarads (mF), microfarads (μF), nanofarads (nF), picofarads (pF), femtofarads (fF). If units are specified, they will take priority over the CAP_UNIT specification.
- The following example specifies a file named wellcapsareacelllist that contains a list of cells and the area based well capacitance for each. well_cap_area_file wellcapsareacelllist

- The following example shows the contents of an area based well capacitance file: CAP_UNIT 1.0e-15 CELL Cell_A 100.00 The first line in the well capacitance file is optional. It assumes that the default units are femtofarads.The second line specifies the area based well capacitance for Cell_A to be 100 femtofarads per square micron. The capacitance specified will be used for both the POWER and GROUND nets. If there are multiple POWER nets or GROUND nets, this value will be evenly distributed among those nets.
This command specifies the amount of well capacitance in a cell. This command is used in generating dynamic port or detailed power-grid views.

In both the port and detailed views, the capacitance specified by the well_cap command is evenly distributed over the taps and appended onto the existign C1 for each tap. If a detailed view is created for this cell, the capacitance values of the taps calculated during the detailed view generation are scaled up or down as necessary so that the total capacitance of all the taps in the cell matches the value specified by this command.

data: Specifies the name of the cell.

Specifies the amount of well capacitance associated with the cell, in femtofarads. All capacitance values can be followed by a unit specification. The following units are recognized: millifarads (mF), microfarads (μF), nanofarads (nF), picofarads (pF), femtofarads (fF)

lines: - The following example specifies a well capacitance of 1 picofarads for the NTZ3802 cell: well_cap NTZ3802 1pF

This variable enables LibGen to process LEF files in which FOREIGN statements are not defined as part of the macro definition. When generating detailed power-grid views, LibGen assumes that FOREIGN statements are defined as part of the macro definition in the LEF file. When this variable is set to true , LibGen processes the LEF file by using the name defined by the MACRO statement rather than requiring a FOREIGN statement.

The assume_foreigns variable ensures that the FOREIGN statement is placed at 0,0. Therefore, if the MACRO statement has an offset, you must provide a FOREIGN statement that contains an offset. This variable works for offsets that are 0,0 or the inverse of the origin. See assume_foreigns_mode for setting it to the inverse.

data:
When set to true , LibGen searches the GDS file for the name defined by the MACRO statement in the LEF file. 
 If set to false , LibGen cannot process the LEF file and issues an error message. 
 The default is false .

lines: - The following example allows LibGen to process a LEF file for which FOREIGN statements are not defined as a part of the macro definition: setvar assume_foreigns true
This variable determines how LibGen processes cells for which FOREIGN statements are assumed.

data: If set to 0 , LibGen assumes that the cell for which FOREIGN statements are assumed is placed at (0,0). When you set it to 1 , LibGen assumes that the FOREIGN cell is placed at the inverse of the macro origin. The default is 1 .

lines: When you set this variable to 0 , LibGen assumes that the cell for which FOREIGN statements are assumed is placed at (0,0). When you set it to 1 , LibGen assumes that the FOREIGN cell is placed at the inverse of the macro origin. The default is 1 .

- The following example specifies that FOREIGN statements are to be assumed for cells with no FOREIGN statements and that the FOREIGN cells should be placed at the inverse of the origin: setvar assume_foreigns true setvar assume_foreigns_mode 1

- In the next example, FOREIGN statements are to be assumed for cells with no FOREIGN statements, and the FOREIGN cells should be placed at (0,0): setvar assume_foreigns true setvar assume_foreigns_mode 0

This variable determines whether LibGen interprets the data in the LEF file as case-sensitive or case-insensitive if the first LEF file does not include a NAMESCASESENSITIVE statement. Normally, LibGen reads the case-sensitivity setting for data such as the layer names, cell names, pin names, and so forth from the NAMESCASESENSITIVE statement in the first LEF file that it processes.

data:
When this variable is set to true , LibGen interprets the data in the LEF file as case-sensitive, if the file does not contain a NAMESCASESENSITIVE statement. 
 When this variable is set to false , LibGen interprets the data in the LEF file as case-insensitive, if the file does not contain a NAMESCASESENSITIVE statement. 
 The default is true .

lines: - In the following example, LibGen handles the data in the LEF file as case-sensitive since the file does not contain a NAMESCASESENSITIVE statement: setvar case_sensitivity_default true
This variable specifies the name of a file containing data about the electrical characteristics of cells. Thunder requires this data to generate currents during power-grid view generation.

data: The name of the file containing the electrical characteristics data.

lines: - The file containing the electrical data about the cell is called cell_char.file in the following example: setvar cell_accura_data_file cell_char.file
This variable determines whether LibGen handles data in the LibGen command file as case-sensitive or case-insensitive.

Since this variable directly affects the way that LibGen interprets the data in the command file, it is position-dependent. Place it as the first line in the command file if you want LibGen to handle the command data as case-insensitive.
Note: Command and variable names are always case-insensitive. The cmd_data_case_sensitive variable only applies to the data specified by commands or variables. Currently this only applies to:

data:
When this variable is set to true , LibGen interprets the data in the command file as case-sensitive. 
 If it is set to false , LibGen interprets the data as case-insensitive. 
 The default is true .

lines: - In the following example, LibGen interprets the data in the LibGen command file as case-insensitive: setvar cmd_data_case_sensitive false
This variable provides guidance to LibGen when creating collapsed powerviews.

data:
Defines a value that will be used for determining which current taps get discarded, when creating a collapsed powergrid view. 
 Valid values are between 0 and 0.2 (0 and 20%). 
 The default is 0.05 (5%).

lines: Note: This collapsed power grid view was created for tool performance reasons. Current designs in many cases contain an embedded memory for boosting performance. The bit cells of the memory in general contribute to creation of a lot of current taps, however, they do not contribute to the IR much, but will affect the tool performance. This collapsed view suppresses those current taps which have a very small current value thus improving both memory usage and performance of the tool.
- In the following example sets the current threshold to 0.1 when generating a collapsed powerview: setvar collapsed_view_current_threshold 0.1
This variable generates a technology file using LEF technology data. You must set this variable when a technology file is unavailable for generating port power-grid views.
This variable is reserved for specialized flows and at no time should be used to generate graybox data for capacitance extraction or to generate detailed power-grid view libraries.

data:
If this variable is set to true , LibGen generates a technology file for the library layers listed in the LEF layer map section in the LibGen command file. 
 Setting this variable to false requires that a technology file be specified by using the tech_file variable. For a complete definition of the tech_file variable, refer to "tech_file" . 
 The default is false .

lines: - The following example indicates that a technology file is to be generated by using the LEF layers mapped from the LibGen command file and the technology data from the layer definition section in the LEF file: setvar create_dummy_tech_file true

This variable specifies the number of internal database units corresponding to one micron. All coordinates in the cell libraries are stored in 32-bit integers, and the setting of this variable affects the minimum feature size that can be resolved. The default setting of this variable is 10000, which means that 1 micron (1 LEF unit) corresponds to 10000 units in the cell library. Each cell library unit therefore represents 1e-10 meter, or one angstrom.

Some common valid values for the database precision setting, other than the default value of 10000, would be 1000 or 100. The magnitude of the maximum coordinate value in the LEF data multiplied by the factor specified by database_precision must be less than 2,147,483,647. For example, if a maximum LEF coordinate is 248304, a database_precision factor of 1000 would be appropriate, because 248304 X 1000 = 248,304,000, which is less than the maximum value of 2,147,483,647.

Under normal circumstances, you do not need to change the database_precision setting from the default setting. However, for certain applications or if you are designing in lambda units, where 1 LEF unit != 1 micron, you may want to set this variable to another value to prevent internal overflow. It is recommended that you reduce the setting from its default value by factors of 10 as necessary to get the data to fit into the appropriate range, but no further.

If you change the setting of database_precision to a non-default value, you must place the variable near the beginning of the LibGen command file, before any other commands that input coordinate or layout data, such as the source_location_file command or the port_view_current_file command.

data:
The data base precision. 
 It must be a positive number greater than 0

lines: - The following example sets the number of internal database units corresponding to one micron at 1000: setvar database_precision 1000

Thisvariable specifies that the parasitic extraction step should be skipped. This forces the skipping of the parasitic extraction step when rerunning LibGen. See other debug_* commands for skipping other processing steps.

data:
When this variable is set to true, the parasitic extraction step is skipped. 
 The default is false .

lines: - The following line specifies that the parasitic extraction step should be skipped. setvar debug_skip_parasitic_extraction true
Thisvariable specifies that the thunder step should be skipped. This forces the skipping of the thunder step when rerunning LibGen. See other debug_* commands for skipping other processing steps.

data:
When this variable is set to true the thunder step is skipped. 
 The default is false .

lines: - The following line specifies that the thunder step should be skipped. setvar debug_skip_thunder true
Thisvariable specifies that the xtc step should be skipped. This forces the skipping of the xtc step when rerunning LibGen. See other debug_* commands for skipping other processing steps.

data:
When this variable is set to true, the xtc step is skipped. 
 The default is false .

lines: - The following line specifies that the xtc step should be skipped. setvar debug_xtc_fire true
This variable specifies the default return voltage value used for ground nets during power-grid view generation.

data:
This variable sets the ground value, in volts. 
 The default is 0.0 volts

lines: - The following example sets the default value for the ground nets to 0.0 volts: setvar default_ground_voltage 0.0
This variable specifies the default spacing value used for all metal layers for which a spacing rule is not defined in the LEF file.
This variable is valid only when you set the create_dummy_tech_file variable to true .
This variable is reserved for specialized flows and at no time should be used to generate graybox data for capacitance extraction or to generate detailed power-grid view libraries.

data:
This variable sets the default spacing value, in microns. 
 The default spacing is 0.18 microns.

lines: - The following example indicates that for all metal layers that do not have a spacing rule specified in the LEF file, the default_lef_min_spacing used will be 0.20 microns: setvar default_lef_min_spacing 0.20
This variable specifies the default width value that is used for all metal layers that do not have a width value defined in the LEF file.
This variable is valid only when you set the create_dummy_tech_file variable to true .
This variable is reserved for specialized flows and at no time should be used to generate graybox data for capacitance extraction or to generate detailed power-grid view libraries.

data:
Specifies the default width value, in microns, to use for metal layers that do not have a value specified in the LEF file. 
 The default width is 0.18 microns.

lines: - The following example indicates that for all metal layers that do not have a width specified in the LEF file, the default width value used will be 0.19 microns: setvar default_lef_min_width 0.19
This variable specifies the default power voltage value used for power nets during power-grid view generation.

data:
Specifies the default power value, in volts. 
 The default value is 1.2 volts

lines: - The following example sets the default value for the power nets to 1.0 volts: setvar default_power_voltage 1.0
This variable specifies a default resistance value to use for LEF vias for which no resistance is specified in the LEF file.

data:
Specifies a default via resistance value, a floating-point number, in ohms. 
 The default value is 5 ohms

lines: - The following example specifies a default resistance value of 4.5 ohms for LEF vias: setvar default_via_resistance 4.5
This variable determines whether LibGen retains the escape characters (\) in the names in the LEF file when it reads this file.

data:
When this variable is set to true , LibGen retains the escape characters in the LEF file names when it reads the file. 
 When this variable is set to false , LibGen removes these escape characters. 
 The default is true .

lines: - As a result of the following example, LibGen removes the escape characters from the LEF file names: setvar disable_lef_parser_escape_check false
This variable determines whether LibGen optimizes the views of standard cells.

data:
When this variable is set to true , LibGen optimizes the views of standard cells. 
 When this variable is set to false , LibGen does not optimize these views. 
 The default is true .

lines: - In the following example, LibGen does not optimize the standard cell views: setvar do_optimized_views false
This variable determines whether LibGen performs a resistance extraction in parallel over the network by using the parallel script or performs the extraction on the local machine.

data:
When this variable is set to true , LibGen invokes the parallel script to run the resistance extraction. 
 When this variable is set to false , LibGen extracts all cells on a local machine. 
 The default is false .

lines: - This line specifies that LibGen perform the resistance extraction in parallel over the network: setvar do_rextract_in_parallel true
This variable determines the algorithm that LibGen uses to create port geometries in the cell library.

data:
When this variable is set to true , LibGen calculates the port geometries internally rather than invoking XTC to calculate them. 
 When this variable is set to false , LibGen invokes XTC to calculate the port geometries. 
 The default is true .

lines: - In the following example, LibGen calculates the port gometries internally: setvar emulate_lef_parsing true
This variable overrides the extension appended to GDS cell names when they are stored in the library.

data:
This variable overrides the default extension that is appended to GDS cell names when they are stored in the library. 
 If you do not specify an extension, LibGen uses a default of _SG . Normally, you should not have to set this variable.

lines: - The following example appends an extension of _gds to the GDS cell names: setvar gds_cell_extension _gds
This variable identifies the executable used as a GDS extractor for LibGen.

data:
This variable identifies the executable used as a GDS extractor for LibGen. 
 If it is not specified, LibGen defaults to XTC.

lines: - The following example sets /home/bin/xtc as the GDS extractor executable: setvar gds_extractor /home/bin/xtc

This variable specifies the name of the command file to be submitted to the GDS extractor. This file includes the layer-map and device-forming commands required by XTC. LibGen verifies that this file is valid when checking the LibGen command file.

data: The name of the command file for the GDS extractor.

lines: - In the following example, xtc.cmd is the command file submitted to the GDS extractor: setvar gds_extractor_command_file xtc.cmd
This variable determines how deeply LibGen traverses the structural GDS hierarchy to calculate the port geometries.

data:
The level of structural GDS hierarchy that LibGen traverses to calculate the port geometries. 
 The default is 0.

lines: When emulate_lef_parsing is set to true , and you specify a hierarchy depth with gds_port_explostion_depth , LibGen explodes the port geometries through the hierarchy. For example, suppose that the VDD net port geometries are defined in the annotated Assura GDS file for structure A, as well as for structure B. B is instantiated in A as a structural reference. When you set gds_port_explosion_depth to 1, only the port geometries in A will be part of the VDD net in the cell library. If you set this variable to 2, the port geometries in B will also go into the cell library with transformation taken into account. If you set this variable to 0, LibGen traverses every leaf of this hierarchy to calculate the port geometries. There is no negative effect if depth is larger than the maximum depth of a design.
If emulate_lef_parsing is set to false , the gds_port_explosion_depth variable has no effect.

- In the following example, LibGen descends to a maximum of three levels in the GDS hierarchy, starting from the top structure, while computing the port geometries: setvar emulate_lef_parsing true setvar gds_port_explosion_depth 3
This variable specifies the name of the command file to use when you run a Synopsys LEF/GDSII flow. It is optional.

data: The name of the command file used to run the Synopsys LEF/GDSII flow.

lines: The Synopsys LEF/GDSII flow processes data in two passes. The first pass generates LEF output for any cell that does not already have a LEF description. The second pass generates the netlist and stripes to be used for the analysis.
Because of this two-pass process, you may need two separate XTC command files to reduce the total run time.

LibGen uses the optional command file specified by the gds_to_lef_gds_extractor_command_file variable during the first pass to reduce run time when generating LEF from GDS. For example, if the GDS file does not contain the complete port geometry at the top level, you would set the XTC extract_flat variable to true . If you continued to use the XTC command file with the extract_flat variable set to true during the second pass, you would unnecessarily increase the total run time, which could be significant.
If you do not specify a command file for this variable and must generate LEF, LibGen uses the command file specified by the gds_extractor_command_file variable for both passes.
- The following example specifies a command file named transform_lef.cmd for the Synopsys LEF/GDSII flow: setvar gds_to_lef_gds_extractor_command_file transform_lef.cmd
This variable overrides the name of the GDS file that LibGen generates when creating holding cells.

data:
The name of the GDS file. This file will be placed in the current working directory. 
 If you do not specify a file name, LibGen uses the default value of LibgenGeneratedForeigns.gds . Normally, you should not have to set this variable and a file called LibgenGeneratedForeigns.gds will be placed in the library_name _temp directory.

lines: - The following example specifies a GDS file named lx_cell.gds which will be placed in the current working directory: setvar generated_gds_file lx_cell.gds
This variable aborts processing if LibGen encounters a FOREIGN statement referencing a cell that is not included in the GDSII files submitted as input.

data:
If this variable is set to true , LibGen stops processing if it finds a reference to a GDSII cell that is not identified in the GDSII input files. 
 If this variable is set to false , LibGen continues processing but issues warning messages. 
 The default is false .

lines: - The next line causes LibGen to stop processing if it finds a reference to a GDSII cell that is not identified in the GDSII input files: setvar halt_on_missing_foreign_data true

This variable controls how LibGen behaves while it parses the LEF file. If a layer referenced in the LEF file is not defined in the layer map in the LibGen command file and this variable is set to true , LibGen aborts processing. If this variable is set to false , LibGen issues a warning about the undefined layer and continues processing.

data:
If this variable is set to true , LibGen stops processing if it finds an undefined LEF layer in the layer map section of the command file. 
 If this variable is set to false , LibGen continues processing but issues a warning message. 
 The default is false .

lines: - In the following example, LibGen stops processing if it finds an undefined LEF layer in the layer-map section of the command file: setvar halt_on_undefined_lef_layer true

This variable directs LibGen to ignore the USE statements in the LEF files. This feature is useful in situations where the data in the LEF file is not correct, and signal pins are defined with USE POWER or USE GROUND .

data:
If this variable is set to true , LibGen ignores the USE statements in the LEF files. 
 When you set it to false , it processes the USE statements. 
 The default is false .

lines: - As a result of the following example, LibGen ignores the USE statements in the LEF files: setvar ignore_lef_use true
LibGen currently does checking for layer definition on lef, lefdef_layermap and the tech file. If a layer is missing in tech file LibGen will report an error message like the following:
This variable can be set to prevent this error.

data:
If this variable is set to true , LibGen will not generate an error message if a layer is missing in the tech file. 
 When this variable is set to false and a layer is missing in the tech file an error will be generated. 
 The default is false .

lines: - As a result of the following example, LibGen ignores missing layer information in the tech file and does not generate an error: setvar ignore_missing_via_definition true
This variable retains all temporary files that LibGen generates during library creation to aid in debugging problems.

data:
When this variable is set to true , LibGen retains the temporary files. 
 When it is set to false , LibGen discards the temporary files. 
 The default is false .

lines: - In the following example, LibGen keeps the temporary files that it generates during library creation: setvar keep_debug_files true
Thisvariable specifies whether or not the netlist will be retained as part of the library.

data:
When this variable is set to true , LibGen retains the netlist as part of the library. 
 When this variable is set to false , LibGen discards the netlist as part of the library. For those users who are not using a power-up flow, this variable can be set to false and added to a LibGen command file to save the space taken up by the netlist. 
 The default is true .

lines: - In the following example, LibGen does not keep the netlist as part of the library: setvar keep_netlist_in_library

This variable is a floating point number used to control the actual scale factor, when layout scaling is performed. If the scale factor has been specified in the technology file, LibGen will issue a warning message and use the one in the technology file to overwrite the user specified value. This variable is used in conjunction with the variable output_wlt .

data: The factor that will be used for scaling the design. It is a floating point number.

lines: - The following example set the layout scale to 0.8 (80%): setvar layout_scale_factor 0.8
This variable specifies the path to the LibGen executable.

data:
The name of the LibGen executable that will be invoked. 
 The default value is libgen , which assumes that the libgen executable can be found on your path.

lines: - The following example specifies a LibGen executable called /home/bin/libgen : setvar libgen_executable /home/bin/libgen
This variable sets the maximum number of errors that LibGen can encounter before it aborts with a fatal error.

data:
Specifies the maximum number of errors that LibGen can encounter before it aborts with a fatal error. It can be an integer in the range of 1 through 10,000, inclusive. 
 The default in 1000.

lines: - The following example sets the maximum number of errors to 500: setvar maxerrors 500
This variable sets the maximum distance between interface nodes when they get generated in LibGen.

data:
The maximum distance between interface nodes in microns. 
 The default is 50 microns.

lines: LibGen creates interface nodes on port geometries in LEF and ZX, uses them for top level connectivity tracing and also to connect the cell/block onto top level routing. The interface node generation is purely based on port geometries (tap points are placed one per port). For ports greater than max_itfnode_dist , they are placed at max_itfnode_dist apart. If the port pin geometry in LEF is bigger than the setting of the max_itfnode_dist variable, at least two nodes will get generated per port geometry.
- The following example sets the distance between interface nodes to 10 microns: setvar max_itfnode_dist 10.0
This variable specifies the length of the longest resistor that LibGen generates during resistance extraction.

data:
Specifies the length of the longest resistor that LibGen generates during resistance extraction. 
 The default value is 50 microns.

lines: - The following example specifies a maximum resistor length of 40 microns: setvar max_resistor_length 40
This variable determines if output performance data of intermediate tools are output to log file.

data:
When this variable is set to true , resource usage data (performance data) of intermediate tools (e.g. XTC, ZX, Thunder) called by LibGen is logged. 
 The default is false .

lines: - In the following example, LibGen outputs performance data of intermediate tools to the log file: setvar output_tool_performance_data true

This variable is used to control whether the resistor width and length should be reported as drawn data or as the actual silicon value. This variable is used in conjunction with the variable layout_scale_factor .

data:
When this variable is set to drawn , the drawn geometry will be output for L and W. 
 If this variable is set to silicon the silicon geometry will be output for width and length. 
 The default is drawn .

lines: - In the following example, LibGen outputs silicon W and L geometry values: setvar output_drawn_geometry silicon
This variable selects the executable that LibGen invokes to perform parasitic extraction.

data:
The path to the executable that LibGen invokes to perform parasitic extraction. 
 If you do not set this variable, LibGen uses the zx executable (Fire & Ice ZX gate-level extractor) to perform the extraction.

lines: - In the following example LibGen invokes fire as the executable to the parasitic extractor: setvar use_old_parasitic_extractor true setvar parasitic_extractor /sev32/bin/fire
This variable specifies the name of the directory where LibGen generates the command files for use during the parasitic extraction.

data:
The name of the directory where LibGen generates the command files for use during the parasitic extraction. 
 The default value is ExtractorCmdFiles in the libname _temp directory. There is usually no reason to override this default value.

lines: - In the following example, the command files are located in the /users/eng/rextract/cmdfiles directory: setvar parasitic_extractor_command_files_directory /users/eng/rextract/cmdfiles

This variable specifies the name of a port file that LibGen uses in generating LEF pin data from GDS. If a port file is specified in a Synopsys LEF/GDSII flow, it is passed to XTC unaltered. If it is specified in a mixed LEF/GDS flow, LibGen modifies the data read from the port file to create a modified port file that matches the impostor cell names and passes all necessary data to XTC so that the LEF file can be created properly.

data: Specifies the name of the port file, which contains a set of ports.

lines: - The following line specifies a port file named lef_pins.dat : setvar port_file lef_pins.dat

This variable specifies the offset in the X direction, between the power-grid database data and the LEF file data, in microns. This offset is used to align these two sets of data when the hierarchical power-grid view is generated. Often the DEF file input to VoltageStorm or VoltageStorm PE is specified in a centered coordinate system, and the LEF file for the cell is specified in a first-quadrant coordinate system (that is, its lower left corner is at 0, 0). VoltageStorm PE passes these two variables to LibGen. When LibGen loads power-grid view data, it adds the values of prpgv_x_offset and prpgv_y_offset to the location of the nodes to place them in the LEF coordinate system.

data:
The prpgv_x_offset variable specifies the offset in the X direction, between the power-grid database data and the LEF file data, in microns. 
 The default value is 0 microns.

lines: - Given these specifications in the DEF file for a cell: UNITS DISTANCE MICRONS 100 DIEAREA ( -5000 -5000 ) ( 5000 5000 ) and this data in the LEF MACRO for the same cell: ORIGIN ( 0 0 ) SIZE 100 BY 100 the appropriate setting of these variables would be as follows: setvar prpgv_x_offset 50 setvar prpgv_y_offset 50 These settings would shift the power-grid data extracted from the DEF file 50 microns to the right and 50 microns up to align with the LEF macro data.

This variable specifies the offset in the Y direction, between the power-grid database data and the LEF file data, in microns. This offset is used to align these two sets of data when the hierarchical power-grid view is generated. Often the DEF file input to VoltageStorm or VoltageStorm PE is specified in a centered coordinate system, and the LEF file for the cell is specified in a first-quadrant coordinate system (that is, its lower left corner is at 0, 0). VoltageStorm PE passes these two variables to LibGen. When LibGen loads power-grid view data, it adds the values of prpgv_x_offset and prpgv_y_offset to the location of the nodes to place them in the LEF coordinate system.

data:
The prpgv_y_offset variable specifies the offset in the Y direction, between the power-grid database data and the LEF file data, in microns. 
 The default value is 0 microns.

lines: - Given these specifications in the DEF file for a cell: UNITS DISTANCE MICRONS 100 DIEAREA ( -5000 -5000 ) ( 5000 5000 ) and this data in the LEF MACRO for the same cell: ORIGIN ( 0 0 ) SIZE 100 BY 100 the appropriate setting of these variables would be as follows: setvar prpgv_x_offset 50 setvar prpgv_y_offset 50 These settings would shift the power-grid data extracted from the DEF file 50 microns to the right and 50 microns up to align with the LEF macro data.

This variable specifies a value, in microns, that determines whether LibGen optimizes the ports of a cell. By default, LibGen optimizes the ports of any cell whose X or Y dimension is less than the value specified. This process reduces the number of geometries and interface nodes on the ports, which can improve the performance of the extraction tools using the data.

data:
Specifies a value in microns, that determines whether LibGen optimizes the ports of a cell. 
 The default is 10 microns.

lines: - As a result of the following line, LibGen optimizes the ports of any cell whose X or Y dimension is less than 5 microns: setvar std_cell_size_limit 5

This variable specifies the number of stripes to run together when LibGen performs resistance extraction using the parallel script. The parallel resistance extraction may fail in some cases if there are large objects that straddle this number of stripes. If that happens, increase the number of stripes.

data:
The number of stripes to process together. It is an integer. 
 The default is 3.

lines: - The following example sets the number of stripes to 7: setvar stripe_step_size 7
This variable determines whether the layer map that LibGen passes to XTC includes the regular GDS layers.

data:
When this variable is set to true and the emulate_lef_parsing variable is set to false , LibGen ignores all the regular GDS layers and only passes the special layers to XTC to generate the pseudo-LEF file. 
 If this variable is set to false , XTC processes all the regular GDS geometries, and all regular GDS geometries that are connected to the special layer port geometries are considered part of the ports. If emulate_lef_parsing is set to true , the suppress_regular_gds_layer variable has no effect. 
 The default is false .

lines: - In the following example, LibGen passes all the GDS layers in the layer map file to XTC: setvar emulate_lef_parsing false setvar suppress_regular_gds_layer false

This variable specifies the name of the technology file generated by RCgen. This file is copied into the cell library for future reference. It is required input for power-grid view generation and is recommended if you create cell libraries for extraction-for-timing flows.

Note: RCgen is the preferred tool for technology file generation. However, technology files generated by IceCaps can still be used for backward compatibility. RCgen must be used for ZX4.1 and VSPE 4.1.

data:
The name of the technology file. 
 There is no default file name.

lines: - The following example gives the path name of the tetra.tch technology file: setvar tech_file /users/lxd/pwrvw/tetra.tch

This variable specifies the name and location of the temporary directory, where LibGen places the temporary library files that it creates. Normally the name of this directory is libname _temp , where libname is the name of the library created. This variable can be useful if the disk space on your machine is running low and you want to put the temporary files at a location other than the default location.

data: The name of the temporary directory where LibGen places the temporary library files.

lines: - In the following example, LibGen places the temporary library files that it creates in the /usr/src5/vs320 directory: setvar temp_directory_name /usr/src5/vs320
Specifies temperature value in degree Celsius.

This variable specifies the name of a file that passes additional user commands to Thunder during detailed power-grid view generation. These commands may be needed, for example, when conflicts with the hierarchy separator occur and you need to re-define the hierarchy separator used by Thunder.

data:
The name of the file containing the additional Thunder commands. 
 There is no default name.

lines: - The following example specifies a Thunder command file named libgen2thunder.txt : setvar thunder_command_file libgen2thunder.txt
This variable specifies the program used for current estimation during power-grid view generation.

data:
Specifies the program used for current estimation during power-grid view generation. 
 The default Thunder executable is thunder.tty .

lines: - The following example specifies ../thunder as the executable:
Specifies that current data for vstorm2 will come from power-grid view.

data: If set to true current data will be used from power-grid view.

lines: - The following example specifies the current data will come from the power-grid view: setvar use_current_data_from_pgv true
Interface nodes are nodes which connect the top level routing with the cell internal resistor network. This variable controls the algorithm used for interface nodes generation.

data:
If this variable is true , interface nodes are generated using only the port geometry data from the LEF files. This is done before extraction has been done. 
 If this variable is false, interface nodes are generated after extraction has been completed. Libgen decides on interface nodes based on the resistor network combining the information from the LEF and GDS files. 
 Default : true

lines: - The following example specifies that interface nodes are generated after extraction has been completed.: setvar use_lef_only_itfnodes false

This variable takes the technology data from one of the libraries in the list of libraries to link or merge other than the first library or from a separate LEF file. Normally LibGen obtains the technology data from the first library in the list of libraries to link or merge. If you use a separate LEF file, you must set up a correct layer map in the LibGen command file corresponding to this LEF file.
This variable is optional when linking or merging libraries with the input_type link_library or input_type merge_library commands, respectively.

data: Specifies the name of the cell library from which to take the technology data.
Specifies the name of the LEF file from which to take the technology data.

lines: - The following example specifies that LibGen take the technology data from the pgvu_360 cell library: setvar use_lef_technology pgvu_360

This variable determines whether LibGen uses the default parser or a parser that can read LEF files created before version 5.6. If the default parser issues warning messages when you try to process a LEF file created before version 5.6, you can invoke another parser to read the LEF file.

data:
If this variable is set to true , LibGen uses a parser that can read a LEF file created before version 5.6. 
 When this variable is set to false , it uses a parser that cannot read a LEF file created before version 5.6. 
 The default is false .

lines: - In this example, LibGen uses a parser that can read a LEF file created before version 5.6:
This variable is for setting ipeak based current distribution in detailed views.

data:
If this variable is set to true , LibGen uses ipeak based current distribution in detailed views. 
 When this variable is set to false , LibGen creates Iavg detailed views using Accura. 
 The default is false .

lines: - In this example, LibGen uses ipeak based current distribution in detailed views: setvar use_pwernet_ipeak true
This variable specifies the name of the directory where XTC will create the stripes to be used during the resistance extraction.

data:
Specifies the name of the directory where XTC will create the stripes to be used during the resistance extraction. 
 The default directory is stripes . There is usually no reason to override this setting.

This section discusses the activity commands:

Use activity to specify activity levels for regions (gates) and to generate average current information reports for analysis. Library Simulator uses activity levels in conjunction with cycle times, VDD to VSS range, and capacitance information to determine the average power-grid tap currents of transistors. Library Simulator applies the specification of activity levels for nodes or devices to the entire region that contains the node or device. If multiple activities are defined at the same node or device, or different activities are defined at nodes or devices in a region, the activity of the region is set to the highest activity of any node specified in the region.
The activity cycle_time command sets the base cycle time of the clocks in the design.

Note: If the design uses multiple clocks with different frequencies, you can use the activity scale command to scale the activity level on gates operating at the different frequencies. (See "activity scale" .)
Value specifies the value, in seconds, to which the simulator sets the base cycle time of the clocks in the design. The default is 5 ns.
The following line sets the base cycle time of the design clocks to 0.3 ns:

The activity default command sets the default activity level for all regions to the specified value. It sets the default activity levels for regions for which the specific activity information is unknown.
The activity default command overwrites activity levels that may have been set by other commands, so you should use this command before using other detailed activity commands.

Value specifies the value to which the simulator sets the default activity level for all regions. A typical value might be in the range of 0.2-0.5. The default is 0.2, and the range should be 0.0 to 1.0.
The following line sets the default activity level for all regions to 0.03:

The activity filed command loads device activity levels from files that have been computed by external tools such as a Verilog or VHDL simulator. A common source of these activity files is a derivation of toggle-count data from high-level simulators.
This command sets the activity levels for the devices specified in the file.
Filename specifies the name of the file containing the device for which the simulator sets the activity level. The contents of the file have the following format:

Activity_level is the average toggle ratio of the node (or devices attached to the node) over one clock period. The value is usually between 0 and 1. If a net toggles more often or you want to take glitches into account, you can specify an activity level larger than 1.
