The following line sets the activity level for the regions based on the device activity level provided in the device_activity.dat file:

The activity filen command loads node activity levels from files that have been computed by external tools such as a Verilog or VHDL simulator. A common source of these activity files is a derivation of toggle-count data from gate-level simulators. This command sets the activity levels for the nodes specified in the file.
Filename specifies the name of the file containing the node for which the simulator sets the activity level. This file has the following format:

Activity_level is the average toggle ratio of the node (or devices attached to the node) over one clock period. The value is usually between 0 and 1. If a net toggles more often or you want to take glitches into account, you can specify an activity level larger than 1.
Here are some examples of the activity-level format:
The following line sets the activity level of the regions on the basis of the activity level of the nodes provided in the node_activity.dat file:

The activity report command generates the power-grid tap data for the specified voltage source node name. Library Simulator considers the specified activity levels, cycle time, and VDD range in conjunction with the total regional capacitances to generate the output file, which is compatible with Lightning (VST). The name of the output file is vsrc_name.iavg .

Library Simulator uses the width-length ratio for all devices connected to a specified voltage source node in a specific region to determine how the average current is distributed between multiple devices in one region. If a region contains two devices, one with a width-length ratio of 2 and a second with a width-length ratio of 1, the first device is assigned two-thirds of the average current consumed by this region, and the second device is assigned one-third of the average current.
This syntax contains the following parameters:

- format Specifies the format of the output report file. Valid options are binary or ASCII. The default is binary, because you use binary output files from the simulator as input to Lightning (VST) for power-grid analysis. It is also more efficient to use the binary file.
- vsrc_name Specifies the name of the voltage source.
The following line generates the power-grid tap data for the VDD voltage source:
The activity reset command resets the activity levels of all regions in the circuit to the default activity level set by the activity default command. (See the activity default .)

The activity scale command scales all activities in the circuit by the given value. This multiplicative factor enables you to add conservatism to the circuit as a whole by scaling all activity levels by a fixed amount, such as 1.1.
You also can use the scaling factor to relax the activity level by specifying a scale value smaller than 1. The activity scale is global; it scales the activity levels for all nodes and devices.
Value specifies the multiplicative factor by which the simulator scales all activities in the circuit. This value must be positive.
The following line scales all activities in the circuit by a factor of 1.2:
The activity scaled command scales the activity levels for the regions containing the specified devices by the given value.
This syntax contains the following parameters:
- value Specifies a floating-point number larger than 0.
- device | dgroup Specifies the name of a device or predefined group of devices. Use the group device command to establish device groups.
The following line scales all activities in the region containing the dev_grp7 device group by 1.1:
The activity scalen command scales the activity levels for the regions containing the specified nodes by the given value.
This syntax contains the following parameters:
- value Specifies a floating-point number larger than 0.
- ngroup Specifies the name of a predefined group of nodes. You establish node groups by using the group node command.
The following line scales all activities in the region containing the node5 node by 1.2:

The activity setd command sets the activity levels for the regions containing the specified devices to the given value. It enables you to set the activity level of specific regions when you know the exact activity.
This syntax contains the following parameters:
- value Specifies a floating-point number, usually between 0 and 1, inclusive, but it can be larger.
- device | dgroup Specifies the name of a device or predefined group of devices. Use the group device command to establish device groups.
The following line sets the activity level for the regions containing devices specified in the dev_grp2 group to 0.03:

The activity setn command sets the activity levels for the regions containing the specified nodes to the given value. It enables you to set the activity level of specific regions when you know the exact activity.
This syntax contains the following parameters:
- value Specifies the value to which the simulator sets the region.
- ngroup Specifies the name of a predefined group of nodes. You establish node groups using the group node command.
The following line sets the activity level for the region containing node3 to 0.027:
The activity vdd_range command sets the base voltage range of the gates in the design. This is the voltage range of VDD-VSS.
Note: If the design uses multiple voltage ranges, you can use the activity scale command to scale the activity level on gates operating at different voltage ranges. (See "activity scale" .)
Value specifies the value to which the simulator sets the base voltage range. The default is 3.3 V.
The following line sets the base voltage range to 3.3 V:

The check command determines when Library Simulator performs node and device checks. The simulator uses these checks to print error messages whenever a node or device exhibits some behavior outside the bounds specified for the node or device. For example, you might want to know whether a node's rise time exceeds a specified limit, or if the node experiences any glitches during simulation.
The check command includes the following:
The check on command activates (and check off deactivates) all checks. The default is checkon.

The check tglon command activates (and check tgloff deactivates) all checks in the simulator when any specified node toggles high or low, depending on which switch you specify, for count number of times. If you do not specify the high or low switch, the simulator counts all toggles. If you do not specify count, the simulator activates (or deactivates) all checks each time a specified node toggles.

The optional toggle-timing parameter definitions provide the logic voltage level for the toggle time. If you do not give a switching timing parameter name, the program uses the default provided by the parameter set. You build toggle-count parameter sets by using the tglcntparams command. (See "tglcnt params" .)
This syntax contains the following parameters:

- params Finds the vt threshold voltage from a specific set of parameters. You build toggle-count parameter sets by using the tglcnt params command. (See "tglcnt params" .) If you omit this argument, the simulator uses default toggle-count parameters. You can find the defaults in the description of tglcnt params .
- tglcnt_param Specifies the name of the parameter set to measure against.
- label Labels each message generated by this command.
- " label " Specifies the label text. Enclose this string in double quotation marks.
- n Specifies the number of node toggles to count before activating or deactivating checks.
- high Counts the number of times that a node toggles high.
- low Counts the number of times that a node toggles low.
- ngroup Specifies the name of a predefined group of nodes. You establish node groups by using the group node command.
The following line turns on all checks on the third rising toggle of the clock node:
The check time command activates all performance monitor types between the specified times.
Time specifies the number of units of time. Include the unit identifier, which can be one of the following:
The following line activates performance monitors between 1.0 ns and 10.0 ns:
The devc command reports device capacitance information.
The devc commands are the following:
The devc table command reports the capacitances associated with the specified devices in tabular form.
Note: Library Simulator currently reports only capacitances associated with MOSFET transistors.
Note: The braces ({ }) enclose the voltage parameters for each node voltage.
This syntax contains the following parameters:
- append Appends the report to the specified file.
- file= filename Specifies the name of the file to which the simulator appends the table.
- vd Specifies the voltage for the drain node. The default value is 0.0 volts.
- vg Specifies the voltage for the gate node. The default value is 0.0 volts.
- vs Specifies the voltage for the source node. The default value is 0.0 volts.
- vb Specifies the voltage for the bulk node. The default value is 0.0 volts.
- v_start Specifies the starting voltage value for a specific node used in generating the table.
- v_end Specifies the end voltage value for a specific node to use in generating the table. If v_end is missing, the simulator uses a DC voltage value of v_start .
- v_step Specifies the voltage step at which table data is reported. If v_step is missing, the simulator uses a DC voltage value of v_start .
- device | dgroup Specifies the name of a device or predefined group of devices. Use the group device command to establish device groups.
The following line reports device capacitances for mos10 and mos15 in the mos.cap file:
devc table file = mos.cap vd = {3.3v} vg = {0.0v, 3.3v, 0.3v} mos10 mos15

In this example, the simulator reports the capacitances for a DC voltage of 3.3 V at the drain node. The gate voltage increases from 0.0 V to 3.3 V in steps of 0.3 V. The source and bulk nodes are at 0.0 V by default.
The devc tran command includes the capacitances of specified devices in the dynamic waveform output file during dynamic analysis.
Note: Library Simulator currently reports only capacitances associated with MOSFET transistors.
Device | dgroup specifies the name of a device or predefined group of devices. Use the group device command to establish device groups.
The following line displays the dynamic device capacitances for the mos20 MOSFET:
In this example, the simulator reports the capacitances between the gate, drain, source, and bulk nodes of the mos20 transistor during dynamic analysis.

The pwrmeter command generates the average tap currents for all devices connected to a power grid or the average power consumption for all instances connected to a voltage source. It is based on a proprietary technology called Accura, which provides activity-based static analysis, current distribution within a cell based on charging and discharging paths, probabilistic calculation of switching activities, recognition of clock domains, and special handling of memory blocks. You can apply this static analysis at the block or the chip level.
Note: The pwrmeter command in the simulator is different from Cadence's PowerMeter tool.

Confusion may arise between PowerMeter and pwrmeter because VoltageStorm PE invokes both PowerMeter and LibGen, which in turn invokes the simulator when it creates the detailed power-grid views. In addition, VoltageStorm Transistor-Level PGS invokes the simulator in its flow.
The pwrmeter command is transistor-based and relies on the simulator's built-in gate-level recognition (regions) and clock-tracing algorithms. It uses the power equation I = CAVF , where:
- C is the capacitance, which is a combination of the device capacitance calculated from the CV tables generated by Tablegen and optionally the capacitance loading read in from the Ice database.

- A is the activity that the Accura technology calculates using a propagation algorithm applied to the gate-level recognition. The Accura technology is the same as that in the PowerMeter tool in the simulator version 4.0.2 or later. In releases prior to 4.0.2, the simulator used a different technology.
- F is the highest input clock frequency to which to set the output signal for gates that Accura does not recognize.

Once the Accura algorithm calculates the current on each node, it analyzes the region driving the current to generate the current breakdown for each device within the region. It calculates the basic switching current and leakage at each node. It does not compute state-dependent leakage or short-circuit (crowbar) current. In summary, the pwrmeter command takes into account capacitance loading, SPICE models, and clocks and activity.

PowerMeter is the next stage in the development of the Accura technology. It uses different input data than the simulator. By using a library, which can have comprehensive power information, as well as functional behavior (complete gate recognition), it takes into account input pin slew rates, capacitance loading on pins and wires, clocks and activity, and the probability of signals being 1. PowerMeter allows state-dependent leakage. Like the pwrmeter command, it uses a propagation algorithm to calculate the switching activity, but it also propagates the probability of a signal being a 1, enabling it to calculate state-dependent leakage. Using the input pin slew rates, it can also calculate the short-circuit (crowbar) current that is part of the internal power and is captured in the cell's power tables. These tables are based on input slew rates and output capacitance loading.
This syntax contains the following parameters:
- FREQ= value Specifies the clock frequency for the entire chip used to compute the average currents.

- CLK_FILE= filename Specifies the name of the file containing the clock nodes, subcircuit definitions, and subcircuit instance names and their frequencies. You can specify multiple file names. For the format of this file, see "Format of the Clock File" .

- FA= value Specifies the default nodal activity to be used instead of Accura. This value is used at each node unless it is overridden by a value in the file specified by FA_FILE . It is given as an activity factor. A value of 0.20 would be 20% of the default frequency.

- FA_FILE= filename Specifies the name of the file containing nodes and their activity. See FA = value above for how values are specified. You can specify multiple file names. For the format of this file, see "Format of the Frequency-Activity File" .
- AVG_PWR= value Specifies the total average power dissipation of the circuit connected to the voltage source.
- AVG_PWR_FILE= filename Specifies the name of two files:
- VCD_FILE= filename Specifies the name of the VCD file input to Accura.
- OUTFILE= filename Specifies the name of the output file. The default is the name of the voltage source. The .cavg extension is appended to the file name.
- vsrc_name Specifies the name of the power-grid voltage source.
The following example sets the clock frequency at 10 MHz for the VDD voltage source:

The next example sets the clock frequency to 10 MHz, specifies the name of the clock file and the output file, and sets the total average power dissipation of the devices connected to vdd2 at 1.0 watt:
pwrmeter device freq=10mhz CLK_FILE=clk.frq AVG_POWER=1.0w OUTFILE=test vdd2
The clock file has the following format:
file : rows EOF //a file consists of rows
line : CLK name freq_value //a clock node specification
| INST name freq_value //a subcircuit instance specification
| INST name freq_value activity //a subcircuit instance with activity
| CELL name freq_value //a subcircuit definition specification
| CELL name freq_value activity //a subcircuit definition with activity
CLK , INST , and CELL are keywords.
- If you use the CLK keyword, the node name is treated as the root of a clock running at the given frequency.

- If you use the INST keyword, all input and output nodes--that is, node names on the instance card--are set to toggle at the specified frequency. The activity of the nodes internal to the subcircuit are computed by using Accura. If you give an optional activity value, Accura is disabled and the activities of all the nodes are set to the specified value; all nodes toggle at the time represented by the product of the specified frequency and the activity.
- If you use the CELL keyword, all instances of the cell are processed instead of a single instance.
CLK has precedence over INST , and INST overrides CELL information to resolve redundant specifications.
The frequency-activity file has the following format:
file :rows EOF //a file consists of rows of information
comment :^[\t]*\*.* EOL //a line starting with * is a comment
line :name FA_value //a node with transitional frequency
- Name can be the name of any node in the circuit.
- FA_value is given as an activity factor. A value of 0.20 would be 20% of the default frequency.
The average-power file has the following format:
file :rows EOF //a file consists of rows of information
comment :^[\t]*\*.powerFA_value //a subcircuit instance with average power
|CELL name power_value //a subcircuit definition with average power

The CELL keyword specifies that the name following it is a subcircuit definition and that all instances of the cell dissipate the same average power. If the file does not contain the CELL keyword, the name is an instance of a subcircuit dissipating the specified average power.
The pwrmeter plot command plots the current data generated by the pwrmeter command.
Note: You can only execute the pwrmeter plot command after the pwrmeter command. If you perform any other analysis between these two commands, the data becomes invalid.
This syntax contains the following parameters:
- avgi Plots the average device current calculated.
- frequency Plots the frequency calculated for each node.
- activity Plots the activity calculated for each node.
- primary Plots the location of the primary input node. Primary input nodes are the following:

The pwrnet command generates power-grid tap current data for Lightning (VST), the power-grid analysis tool. Power-grid taps consist of all transistors connected to the power node.

Typical power analysis involves reporting the total power consumed or current delivered by voltage sources in SPICE-type circuit analysis. This information is the first step in power analysis. The pwrnet command provides the next step in power analysis: reporting where power is consumed in the circuit so that you can perform region-wide power and power-grid analyses using the data.
The pwrnet command includes the following commands:
The pwrnet clear command clears all power net tally data.
The pwrnet ipeak command prints the maximum current through transistors that are connected to a power net.

This command performs a device current evaluation by using |vgs|=vdd*vgs_fact and |vds|=vdd*vds_fact to obtain the peak IDS of the transistor. Library Simulator then reports ids * ipeak_fact in the output file as the peak current of the transistor.
The format of the output file is a list of transistor names followed by the scaled peak current of the transistor.
This syntax contains the following parameters:

- format Specifies the output report file format. Valid options are binary or ASCII. The default is binary, because you use binary output files from the simulator as input to Lightning (VST) for power-grid analysis.

- file Specifies an output file other than the default of vsrc_name .ipeak. If you use the pwrnet specify command to provide a power specification before using the pwrnet ipeak command, the simulator splits the output file into two files:
- filename Specifies the name of the file to which the simulator prints the information.
- vds_fact Specifies a multiplication factor used with the drain-to-source voltage. The default is 1.
- vgs_fact Specifies a multiplication factor used with the gate-to-source voltage. The default is 1.
- ipeak_fact Specifies a multiplication factor used with the peak current through the device. The default is 1.
- vsrc_name Specifies the name of the voltage source: VDD or another source name.
The following line prints the worst-case current through all the devices connected to VDD to a file named vdd.ipeak :
The pwrnet on command activates (and pwrnet off deactivates) the storing and tallying of power-net device current data during dynamic analysis. The default is pwrnet on.

The pwrnet report command generates a report containing the RMS, maximum, and average results of individual device current tallies. Initiate tallies by using the pwrnet tally command. (See "pwrnet tally" .) Library Simulator reports device currents only if the current is greater than 1.0e -20 amperes.
Vsrc_name specifies the name of the voltage source for which you previously performed the pwrnet tally command, and the prefix for the .rms , .max , and .avg output files.
The following line prints tallied current RMS, maximum, and average information to files named vdd.rms , vdd.max , and vdd.avg , respectively:

The pwrnet specify command calculates the total power consumption for blocks or the current for individual devices. This current value is used during static power-grid analysis. You can use the pwrnet specify command to pre-assign a current consumption number for both analog and digital circuitry.
When you use the pwrnet specify command to provide a power specification before using the pwrnet ipeak command, the simulator splits the output into two files:
- vsrc_name .aipeak , which contains the current data for all the devices or the calculated total power consumption for blocks. This information is obtained by using the pwrnet specify command.
- vsrc_name .ipeak , which contains the current data for the rest of the devices.
In Lightning (VST), load the .ipeak file first, and scale the current data. Lightning (VST) then loads the .aipeak file.
This syntax contains the following parameters:
- DEV = device_name Specifies the name of the device for which the power or current value is specified.
- DEF = subcircuit_definition Specifies the name of the definition of a subcircuit for which the power or current value is specified. The specified values apply to all instances of the subcircuit.
- INST = subcircuit_instance Specifies the name of an instance of a subcircuit for which the power or current value is specified.

- group_file = filename Specifies the name of the file containing analog circuits described as device groups. The format of the group file is as follows: list_of_groups : group | list_of_groups group : group_name group_attributes { list_of_layout_devices } EOF This syntax contains the following parameters:

- bjt_ratio = value Specifies the ratio of total current drawn by all bipolar junction transistors (BJTs) in the subcircuit. You can only specify this value with the DEF or INST option. If you do not specify a value, the simulator uses the default value set by the PwrnetSpecifyBjtRatio environment variable.

- res_ratio = value Specifies the ratio of the total current drawn by all resistors in the subcircuit. You can only specify this value with the DEF or INST option. If you do not specify a value, the simulator uses the default value set by the PwrnetResRatio environment variable.

- diode_ratio = value Specifies the ratio of total current drawn by all diodes in the subcircuit connected to a power grid. You can only specify this value with the DEF or INST option. If you do not specify a value, the simulator uses the default value set by the PwrnetDiodeRatio environment variable.
- POWER = value Specifies the power consumption for the specified subcircuit definition, instance, or device.
- CURRENT = value Specifies the current drawn by the specified subcircuit definition, instance, or device.
In the following example, a device named m1 draws a current of 0.2 μA:
In the next example, a subcircuit block named x1 dissipates 0.1 mW of power. The specified power value is divided among all the devices connected to voltage source nodes in the block.
In the example that follows, all subcircuit instances of the nand2 cell draw 0.1 μA of current:
pwrnet specify DEF = nand2 current = 0.1u bjt_ratio = 0.8

The cell contains BJT devices, and bjt_ratio is defined as 80 percent of the total (0.1 μA) current. This percentage is divided among the BJTs, and the remaining 20 percent is divided among the rest of the devices connected to the voltage supply node. Similarly, if the device contains resistors or diodes, the percentage of the total current defined by res_ratio or diode_ratio is divided among the resistors or diodes. The percentage of the total current remaining is then divided among the other devices, for example, MOSFETs.
Finally, in this example, the simulator reads a group file named file.grp :
The contents of file.grp are as follows:

The group file contains two device groups. In group1 , the specified devices, m1 and m2 , both collectively draw 0.2 μA of current. In group2 , the two devices dissipate 0.1 μW of power. The distribution of current in individual devices is computed as described earlier in this section. If the same device appears in multiple groups, the device current computed in the last group is used.

The pwrnet tally command tallies the average, maximum, and RMS currents of all transistors connected to the specified voltage source for the current dynamic simulation. The pwrnet report command creates a report containing these tallies.
Vsrc_name specifies the name of the voltage source.
The following line tallies current drawn by all the transistors connected to the vdd voltage source:

The pwrnet tallyint command reports the dynamic current of all devices connected to the specified voltage source during dynamic simulation. The current information is used by Lightning (VST) for dynamic analysis of the power grid connected to the voltage source.

Use pwrnet tallyint before running a dynamic simulation. Library Simulator calculates peak, RMS, and average current for each device during each interval, and averages these measurements over as many periods as fit into the duration of the simulation.
Library Simulator stores the data obtained in three files:
- The first file, filename .ptimax , contains the peak dynamic current of all devices connected to the voltage source.
- The second file, filename .ptirms , contains the RMS current of all devices connected to the voltage source.
- The third file, filename .ptiavg , contains the average current.
In all cases, the default file name is the name of the voltage source. You can specify another file by using the file option.
This syntax contains the following parameters:
- filename Specifies the output file name.
- method Specifies the method that the simulator uses in performing tallyint calculations. It is a three-position variable, with each position containing a 0 or a 1:

- intervals Specifies how many intervals the simulator tallies. Library Simulator divides each period into n number of intervals; calculates peak, RMS, and average current for each device during each interval; and averages these measurements over the periods of the simulation.

- period Specifies the portion of the dynamic simulation for which the simulator tallies the current of each device. Library Simulator divides the duration of the simulation by time to derive as many periods as it takes to cover the entire simulation. Each period is in seconds.
- time Specifies the number of units of time. Include the unit identifier:
- vsrc_name Specifies the voltage source connected to the power grid.

The following line tallies the currents for each of the devices connected to the vdd voltage source. Library Simulator tallies for 100 intervals over a period of 10 ns, reporting the maximum average and RMS current over all periods. The simulator calculates the average current for each interval as the average over all periods, and calculates the maximum and RMS current for each interval as the maximum current in every interval over all periods. The current information generated by the tallyint command is used by Lightning (VST) to perform power-grid analysis.
pwrnet tallyint file=vdd method=010 intervals=100 period=10ns vdd

The pwrnet tglon command activates (and pwrnet tgloff deactivates) all power-net tallying during dynamic simulation. Library Simulator performs power-net tallying when any specified node toggles high or low, depending on your specification, for count number of times. If you do not specify the high or low switch, the simulator counts all toggles. If you do not specify count , the simulator activates (or deactivates) all power-net tallying each time a node toggles.
This syntax contains the following parameters:

- params Measured power-net information against a specific set of parameters. You build toggle-count parameter sets by using the tglcnt params command. (See the "tglcnt params" .) If you omit this argument, the simulator uses default toggle-count parameters. You can find the defaults in the description of tglcnt params .
- tglcnt_param Specifies the name of the parameter set from which the simulator determines vt .
- n Specifies the number of node toggles that the simulator registers before activating or deactivating power-net tallying and output.
- high Counts the number of times that a node toggles high.
- low Counts the number of times that a node toggles low.
- ngroup Specifies the name of a predefined group of nodes for which the simulator registers toggling. Use the group nodes command to establish node groups.
- node Specifies the name of a node for which the simulator registers toggling.
The following line turns power-net checking off at the fourth high toggle of the node named clock:
pwrnet tgloff params=tgl_prm count=4 high clock
The pwrnet time command turns power-net checking on or off during the specified time interval. The default is on.
Time specifies the number of units of time. Include the unit identifier:
The following line turns power-net checking on 30 ns into a simulation:
The region command works with region tolerances.
The region commands are the following:

The region params command defines a set of region tolerances. You can control tolerances on a region-by-region basis. This command enables you to define or change tolerance parameters. You then can use the region setn and region setd commands to assign these parameters to specific regions.
This syntax contains the following parameters:
- default Specifies the default parameter set. Specify default to change the default values for the duration of this session.
- param_name Specifies the name of the parameter set containing a specific value for one or more parameters.

- rtol= voltage Specifies the relative region voltage tolerance. This tolerance is the relative voltage tolerance in the region linear relaxation convergence calculation. The default is set by the DefaultRegionRelativeTolerance .simplexrc environment variable.

- atol= voltage Specifies the absolute region voltage tolerance. This tolerance is the absolute voltage tolerance in the region linear relaxation convergence calculation. The default is set by the DefaultRegionAbsoluteTolerance .simplexrc environment variable.

- vstep= voltage Specifies the voltage step, which determines how often a region and its fanouts are processed with respect to the voltage changes in the region. Smaller voltage steps yield higher accuracy in region behavior at the cost of higher processing time. Small voltage steps are recommended for regions containing nodes exhibiting smaller voltage fluctuations, such as analog nodes and sense amplifiers. The default is set by the DefaultRegionEventThreshold .simplexrc environment variable.

- iter_max= n Specifies the maximum number of iterations, which is the maximum number of iterations allowed in region linear relaxation calculation. The default is set by the DefaultRegionIterationLimit .simplexrc environment variable.
- minstep= stepsize Specifies the minimum time step size used to evaluate the region. The default is set by the DefaultRegionMinTimeStep .simplexrc environment variable.
The following line resets two default region parameters, vstep and iter_max , for the duration of the current session:
The region setd command sets region tolerances for regions containing the specified devices.
This syntax contains the following parameters:

- params Applies specified region tolerances to the regions confining the specified devices. If you omit this argument, the simulator uses default region parameters. You can find the defaults in "region params" .

The stats command observes the status of a Library Simulator session. The resulting report contains information about the circuit elements, memory usage, and the time taken by various operations.

In this example, the simulator reports the number of circuit nodes and elements and the memory used by the circuit along with the circuit loading, partitioning, and analysis times.
The tglcnt command works with toggle count information.

A toggle is either rising past the logic voltage level or falling past the logic voltage level. You can define the logic voltage level by using the optional tglcnt params command. (See "tglcnt params" .)
The tglcnt commands are the following:

The tglcnt check command checks the toggle count using the parameters specified in the tglcnt_param parameter set. Use this command to detect nodes that toggle excessively in an interval of time. Excessive toggling is associated with circuit hazards.
This syntax contains the following parameters:

- params Measures toggle information by using a specific set of parameters. Use the tglcnt params command to build toggle parameter sets. (See "tglcnt params" .) If you omit this argument, the simulator uses default toggle parameters. You can find the defaults in the description of tglcnt params .
- tglcnt_param Specifies the name of the parameter set that the simulator measures with.
- label Labels each message generated by this command.
- " label " Specifies the label text. Enclose this string in double quotation marks.
- ngroup Specifies the name of a predefined group of nodes. You establish node groups by using the group node command.
- node Specifies the name of a node.
The following line checks toggle frequency for node2 and node3 by using the parameters in the tglprm2 parameter set:
The tglcnt clear command clears toggle count information from tallies.
The tglcnt on command activates (and tglcnt off deactivates) the tallying and checking of toggling in the specified circuit. The default is tglcnt on.
The tglcnt params command specifies the voltage level used to define the toggle time of the specified node.
Use this command to define one or many timing sets. Because this command supports multiple node sensitivities, each timing set can be applied to any number of nodes.
This syntax contains the following parameters:
- default Specifies the default parameter set. Specify default to change the default values for the duration of this session.
- tglcnt_param Specifies the name of the parameter set containing a specific value for one or more parameters.
- vt= voltage Specifies the intermediate-logic threshold. The default is set by the DefaultParamVt .simplexrc environment variable.
- interval Specifies the duration of time during which the simulator counts toggles using the int_count option. The default is 0, resulting in every toggle counted.
- time Specifies the number of units of time. Include the unit identifier:

- int_count Specifies the number of toggles that the simulator counts within the interval specified by interval . The default is 1. Library Simulator takes a specified action when the toggle count is equal to or greater than the specified number within an interval.
The following line defines a trigger condition as two toggles within 1 ns:
The tglcnt report command reports the results of the individual node toggle count.
This syntax contains the following parameters:
- append Appends the report to the specified file.
- filename Specifies the name of the file to which the simulator appends the report.
The following line reports the results of the individual node toggle count:
The tglcnt tally command counts the number of toggles at a specified node.
If you do not specify params , the simulator uses the default, vt , which is set by the DefaultParamVt .simplexrc environment variable.
This syntax contains the following parameters:

- params Measures toggle tally information by using a specific set of parameters. Use the tglcnt params command to build toggle tally parameter sets. (See "tglcnt params" .) If you omit this argument, the simulator uses default timing tally parameters. You can find the defaults in the description of tglcnt params .
- tglcnt_param Specifies the name of the parameter set that the simulator uses to measure.
- label Labels each message generated by this command.
- " label " Specifies the label text. Enclose this string in double quotation marks.
- ngroup Specifies the name of a predefined group of nodes. Use the group nodes command to establish node groups.
- node Specifies the name of a node.
The following line tallies toggles for node1 and node2 using the parameters specified in the tgl_param1 parameter set:tglcnt tally params=tgl_param1 node1 node2

The vcd command controls input toggling to the simulator by using data specified in VCD (value change dump) format. Library Simulator reads the VCD file and, by default, drives the nodes listed in that file with the values listed in it.
In addition, the vcd command enables you to use the data from the VCD file to compare logic-level simulation with results from the simulator.
This syntax contains the following parameters:

- signal_filename Specifies the name of the signal declaration file, which defines directions for the signals placed in the VCD files. If you do not specify a signal declaration file, the simulator considers all signals in the VCD file to be of type input and therefore all values in the VCD file to be stimuli. See "Signal Declaration File" for complete information on signal declaration files.
- action_filename Specifies the name of the action file. This file defines the following:
- scope_id Defines the scope of the command. This parameter acts as a selector for the signal declaration and action file. If you do not specify a scope, the default scope is the entire VCD file.
- reset Executes the reset command before executing the vcd command.
- vcd_filename Specifies the name of the VCD file. See "VCD File" for information on the VCD file.
The following line uses dump1 as the VCD input and resets the circuit before driving the node:
The VCD file is the standard Verilog VCD file described in OVI documents and IEEE 1364. Library Simulator uses the following conventions to translate VCD values into input stimuli.
Within the VCD file, these VCD values have the following meanings during driving:
- 0 Forces the associated node to logic low.
- 1 Forces the associated node to logic high.
- X Forces the associated node to logic unknown.

The following extensions of the VCD format are supported. You can use these command extensions in the comment section of a VCD file to specify node characteristics; however, Simplex recommends that you specify node sets and parameters in the action file.
Use these extensions to specify properties such as input voltages and rise and fall times of nodes listed in the VCD file.
- *scope Sets the scope of the nodeset command by using the scope command. Scope_string is prepended to the nodes listed in the nodeset command. The syntax is as follows: *scope scope_string *end

- *nodeset Groups nodes in the VCD file into node sets. You can then set properties of the nodes in the node set by using the params command on the node set. The syntax is as follows: *nodeset set_number [ node_name ]+ *end

- *params Assigns parameters to sets of nodes by using the params command. The syntax is as follows: *params set_number <vin0= voltage > <vin1= voltage > <vinx= voltage ><tir= time > <tif= time > *end

- list_of_signal_names Lists the simple signal identifiers. Separate multiple names with commas.

The action file defines the times and events at which the simulator performs checking, executes commands, and toggles the state of the bidirectional signals. The action file contains the following kinds of statements.
The nodeset statement defines a set of signals for which a specific parameter set is applied.
Note: Braces ( { } ) enclose the signal names. You must enter the braces.
This syntax contains the following parameters:
- set_number Specifies the number of the set that you are defining with this nodeset statement.

- signal_name Identifies a simple signal. Separate multiple names with commas. If you do not specify a signal name, the simulator checks all signals of type output and bidirectional that are toggled into the output state. The signal name must be defined in the VCD file.
- keyword Specifies the signal direction. Library Simulator applies the parameter set to all signals of this signal type. It can be any of the following:
Note: All nodeset and parameter statements must be set before the program's first check or the assign statement.
Following are three examples of the nodeset statement.
params 1 vin0=0.0 vin1=3.3 tir=0.1ns tif=0.1ns voutl=(-0.5,+0.5) vouth=(3.0,3.6) vth=(1.6,1.7);

All signals that are listed between the brackets are grouped into the node set labeled 1 . The label is used as the identifier for the following params statement, defining the voltage ranges for stimuli and checking the identified signals.
params 1 vin0=0v vin1=2.25v vinx=1.125v tir=0.1ns tif=0.1ns;

The second example uses keyword identifiers. All signals declared of type input are labeled with the node set identifier 1 . The signal direction can be either defined in the VCD file or declared in the signal file. The node set identifier specifies the stimuli waveform parameter. The second set groups all signals declared as output signals in the VCD file into node set 2 and defines the voltage ranges for checking during the simulation.
params 1 vin0=0v vin1=1.8v voutl=(-0.2v,0.2v) vouth=(1.6v,2.0v) tir=80ps tif=80ps;

An empty signal list for the nodeset statements groups into one node set all signals of type output, as well as all bidirectional signals that are toggled into output mode. The node set label defines the voltage ranges for validation.

This section discusses the activity commands:

Use activity to specify activity levels for regions (gates) and to generate average current information reports for analysis. Library Simulator uses activity levels in conjunction with cycle times, VDD to VSS range, and capacitance information to determine the average power-grid tap currents of transistors. Library Simulator applies the specification of activity levels for nodes or devices to the entire region that contains the node or device. If multiple activities are defined at the same node or device, or different activities are defined at nodes or devices in a region, the activity of the region is set to the highest activity of any node specified in the region.
The activity cycle_time command sets the base cycle time of the clocks in the design.

Note: If the design uses multiple clocks with different frequencies, you can use the activity scale command to scale the activity level on gates operating at the different frequencies. (See "activity scale" .)
Value specifies the value, in seconds, to which the simulator sets the base cycle time of the clocks in the design. The default is 5 ns.
The following line sets the base cycle time of the design clocks to 0.3 ns:

The activity default command sets the default activity level for all regions to the specified value. It sets the default activity levels for regions for which the specific activity information is unknown.
The activity default command overwrites activity levels that may have been set by other commands, so you should use this command before using other detailed activity commands.

Value specifies the value to which the simulator sets the default activity level for all regions. A typical value might be in the range of 0.2-0.5. The default is 0.2, and the range should be 0.0 to 1.0.
The following line sets the default activity level for all regions to 0.03:

The activity filed command loads device activity levels from files that have been computed by external tools such as a Verilog or VHDL simulator. A common source of these activity files is a derivation of toggle-count data from high-level simulators.
This command sets the activity levels for the devices specified in the file.
Filename specifies the name of the file containing the device for which the simulator sets the activity level. The contents of the file have the following format:

Activity_level is the average toggle ratio of the node (or devices attached to the node) over one clock period. The value is usually between 0 and 1. If a net toggles more often or you want to take glitches into account, you can specify an activity level larger than 1.

The activity filen command loads node activity levels from files that have been computed by external tools such as a Verilog or VHDL simulator. A common source of these activity files is a derivation of toggle-count data from gate-level simulators. This command sets the activity levels for the nodes specified in the file.
Filename specifies the name of the file containing the node for which the simulator sets the activity level. This file has the following format:

Activity_level is the average toggle ratio of the node (or devices attached to the node) over one clock period. The value is usually between 0 and 1. If a net toggles more often or you want to take glitches into account, you can specify an activity level larger than 1.

The activity report command generates the power-grid tap data for the specified voltage source node name. Library Simulator considers the specified activity levels, cycle time, and VDD range in conjunction with the total regional capacitances to generate the output file, which is compatible with Lightning (VST). The name of the output file is vsrc_name.iavg .

Library Simulator uses the width-length ratio for all devices connected to a specified voltage source node in a specific region to determine how the average current is distributed between multiple devices in one region. If a region contains two devices, one with a width-length ratio of 2 and a second with a width-length ratio of 1, the first device is assigned two-thirds of the average current consumed by this region, and the second device is assigned one-third of the average current.
This syntax contains the following parameters:

- format Specifies the format of the output report file. Valid options are binary or ASCII. The default is binary, because you use binary output files from the simulator as input to Lightning (VST) for power-grid analysis. It is also more efficient to use the binary file.
- vsrc_name Specifies the name of the voltage source.
The following line generates the power-grid tap data for the VDD voltage source:
The activity reset command resets the activity levels of all regions in the circuit to the default activity level set by the activity default command. (See the activity default .)

The activity scale command scales all activities in the circuit by the given value. This multiplicative factor enables you to add conservatism to the circuit as a whole by scaling all activity levels by a fixed amount, such as 1.1.
You also can use the scaling factor to relax the activity level by specifying a scale value smaller than 1. The activity scale is global; it scales the activity levels for all nodes and devices.
Value specifies the multiplicative factor by which the simulator scales all activities in the circuit. This value must be positive.

This syntax contains the following parameters:

- device | dgroup Specifies the name of a device or predefined group of devices. Use the group device command to establish device groups.

The check command determines when Library Simulator performs node and device checks. The simulator uses these checks to print error messages whenever a node or device exhibits some behavior outside the bounds specified for the node or device. For example, you might want to know whether a node's rise time exceeds a specified limit, or if the node experiences any glitches during simulation.
The check command includes the following:
The check on command activates (and check off deactivates) all checks. The default is checkon.

The check tglon command activates (and check tgloff deactivates) all checks in the simulator when any specified node toggles high or low, depending on which switch you specify, for count number of times. If you do not specify the high or low switch, the simulator counts all toggles. If you do not specify count, the simulator activates (or deactivates) all checks each time a specified node toggles.

The optional toggle-timing parameter definitions provide the logic voltage level for the toggle time. If you do not give a switching timing parameter name, the program uses the default provided by the parameter set. You build toggle-count parameter sets by using the tglcntparams command. (See "tglcnt params" .)
This syntax contains the following parameters:

- params Finds the vt threshold voltage from a specific set of parameters. You build toggle-count parameter sets by using the tglcnt params command. (See "tglcnt params" .) If you omit this argument, the simulator uses default toggle-count parameters. You can find the defaults in the description of tglcnt params .
- tglcnt_param Specifies the name of the parameter set to measure against.
- label Labels each message generated by this command.
- " label " Specifies the label text. Enclose this string in double quotation marks.
- n Specifies the number of node toggles to count before activating or deactivating checks.
- high Counts the number of times that a node toggles high.
- low Counts the number of times that a node toggles low.
- ngroup Specifies the name of a predefined group of nodes. You establish node groups by using the group node command.

- vg Specifies the voltage for the gate node. The default value is 0.0 volts.

In this example, the simulator reports the capacitances for a DC voltage of 3.3 V at the drain node. The gate voltage increases from 0.0 V to 3.3 V in steps of 0.3 V. The source and bulk nodes are at 0.0 V by default.

Device | dgroup specifies the name of a device or predefined group of devices. Use the group device command to establish device groups.

The pwrmeter command generates the average tap currents for all devices connected to a power grid or the average power consumption for all instances connected to a voltage source. It is based on a proprietary technology called Accura, which provides activity-based static analysis, current distribution within a cell based on charging and discharging paths, probabilistic calculation of switching activities, recognition of clock domains, and special handling of memory blocks. You can apply this static analysis at the block or the chip level.
Note: The pwrmeter command in the simulator is different from Cadence's PowerMeter tool.

Confusion may arise between PowerMeter and pwrmeter because VoltageStorm PE invokes both PowerMeter and LibGen, which in turn invokes the simulator when it creates the detailed power-grid views. In addition, VoltageStorm Transistor-Level PGS invokes the simulator in its flow.
The pwrmeter command is transistor-based and relies on the simulator's built-in gate-level recognition (regions) and clock-tracing algorithms. It uses the power equation I = CAVF , where:
- C is the capacitance, which is a combination of the device capacitance calculated from the CV tables generated by Tablegen and optionally the capacitance loading read in from the Ice database.

- A is the activity that the Accura technology calculates using a propagation algorithm applied to the gate-level recognition. The Accura technology is the same as that in the PowerMeter tool in the simulator version 4.0.2 or later. In releases prior to 4.0.2, the simulator used a different technology.
- F is the highest input clock frequency to which to set the output signal for gates that Accura does not recognize.

Once the Accura algorithm calculates the current on each node, it analyzes the region driving the current to generate the current breakdown for each device within the region. It calculates the basic switching current and leakage at each node. It does not compute state-dependent leakage or short-circuit (crowbar) current. In summary, the pwrmeter command takes into account capacitance loading, SPICE models, and clocks and activity.

PowerMeter is the next stage in the development of the Accura technology. It uses different input data than the simulator. By using a library, which can have comprehensive power information, as well as functional behavior (complete gate recognition), it takes into account input pin slew rates, capacitance loading on pins and wires, clocks and activity, and the probability of signals being 1. PowerMeter allows state-dependent leakage. Like the pwrmeter command, it uses a propagation algorithm to calculate the switching activity, but it also propagates the probability of a signal being a 1, enabling it to calculate state-dependent leakage. Using the input pin slew rates, it can also calculate the short-circuit (crowbar) current that is part of the internal power and is captured in the cell's power tables. These tables are based on input slew rates and output capacitance loading.
This syntax contains the following parameters:
- FREQ= value Specifies the clock frequency for the entire chip used to compute the average currents.

- CLK_FILE= filename Specifies the name of the file containing the clock nodes, subcircuit definitions, and subcircuit instance names and their frequencies. You can specify multiple file names. For the format of this file, see "Format of the Clock File" .

- FA= value Specifies the default nodal activity to be used instead of Accura. This value is used at each node unless it is overridden by a value in the file specified by FA_FILE . It is given as an activity factor. A value of 0.20 would be 20% of the default frequency.

- FA_FILE= filename Specifies the name of the file containing nodes and their activity. See FA = value above for how values are specified. You can specify multiple file names. For the format of this file, see "Format of the Frequency-Activity File" .
- AVG_PWR= value Specifies the total average power dissipation of the circuit connected to the voltage source.
- AVG_PWR_FILE= filename Specifies the name of two files:
- VCD_FILE= filename Specifies the name of the VCD file input to Accura.
- OUTFILE= filename Specifies the name of the output file. The default is the name of the voltage source. The .cavg extension is appended to the file name.
- vsrc_name Specifies the name of the power-grid voltage source.
The following example sets the clock frequency at 10 MHz for the VDD voltage source:

The next example sets the clock frequency to 10 MHz, specifies the name of the clock file and the output file, and sets the total average power dissipation of the devices connected to vdd2 at 1.0 watt:
pwrmeter device freq=10mhz CLK_FILE=clk.frq AVG_POWER=1.0w OUTFILE=test vdd2
The clock file has the following format:
file : rows EOF //a file consists of rows
line : CLK name freq_value //a clock node specification
| INST name freq_value //a subcircuit instance specification
| INST name freq_value activity //a subcircuit instance with activity
| CELL name freq_value //a subcircuit definition specification
| CELL name freq_value activity //a subcircuit definition with activity
CLK , INST , and CELL are keywords.
- If you use the CLK keyword, the node name is treated as the root of a clock running at the given frequency.

- If you use the INST keyword, all input and output nodes--that is, node names on the instance card--are set to toggle at the specified frequency. The activity of the nodes internal to the subcircuit are computed by using Accura. If you give an optional activity value, Accura is disabled and the activities of all the nodes are set to the specified value; all nodes toggle at the time represented by the product of the specified frequency and the activity.
- If you use the CELL keyword, all instances of the cell are processed instead of a single instance.
CLK has precedence over INST , and INST overrides CELL information to resolve redundant specifications.
The frequency-activity file has the following format:
file :rows EOF //a file consists of rows of information
comment :^[\t]*\*.* EOL //a line starting with * is a comment
line :name FA_value //a node with transitional frequency
- Name can be the name of any node in the circuit.
- FA_value is given as an activity factor. A value of 0.20 would be 20% of the default frequency.
The average-power file has the following format:
file :rows EOF //a file consists of rows of information
comment :^[\t]*\*.powerFA_value //a subcircuit instance with average power
|CELL name power_value //a subcircuit definition with average power

The CELL keyword specifies that the name following it is a subcircuit definition and that all instances of the cell dissipate the same average power. If the file does not contain the CELL keyword, the name is an instance of a subcircuit dissipating the specified average power.

| INST name freq_value //a subcircuit instance specification
| INST name freq_value activity //a subcircuit instance with activity
| CELL name freq_value //a subcircuit definition specification
| CELL name freq_value activity //a subcircuit definition with activity

- If you use the INST keyword, all input and output nodes--that is, node names on the instance card--are set to toggle at the specified frequency. The activity of the nodes internal to the subcircuit are computed by using Accura. If you give an optional activity value, Accura is disabled and the activities of all the nodes are set to the specified value; all nodes toggle at the time represented by the product of the specified frequency and the activity.
- If you use the CELL keyword, all instances of the cell are processed instead of a single instance.
CLK has precedence over INST , and INST overrides CELL information to resolve redundant specifications.

file :rows EOF //a file consists of rows of information
comment :^[\t]*\*.* EOL //a line starting with * is a comment
line :name FA_value //a node with transitional frequency
- Name can be the name of any node in the circuit.
- FA_value is given as an activity factor. A value of 0.20 would be 20% of the default frequency.

comment :^[\t]*\*.powerFA_value //a subcircuit instance with average power
|CELL name power_value //a subcircuit definition with average power

The CELL keyword specifies that the name following it is a subcircuit definition and that all instances of the cell dissipate the same average power. If the file does not contain the CELL keyword, the name is an instance of a subcircuit dissipating the specified average power.
The pwrmeter plot command plots the current data generated by the pwrmeter command.
Note: You can only execute the pwrmeter plot command after the pwrmeter command. If you perform any other analysis between these two commands, the data becomes invalid.
This syntax contains the following parameters:
- avgi Plots the average device current calculated.
- frequency Plots the frequency calculated for each node.
- activity Plots the activity calculated for each node.
- primary Plots the location of the primary input node. Primary input nodes are the following:

The pwrnet command generates power-grid tap current data for Lightning (VST), the power-grid analysis tool. Power-grid taps consist of all transistors connected to the power node.

Typical power analysis involves reporting the total power consumed or current delivered by voltage sources in SPICE-type circuit analysis. This information is the first step in power analysis. The pwrnet command provides the next step in power analysis: reporting where power is consumed in the circuit so that you can perform region-wide power and power-grid analyses using the data.
The pwrnet command includes the following commands:
The pwrnet clear command clears all power net tally data.
The pwrnet ipeak command prints the maximum current through transistors that are connected to a power net.

This command performs a device current evaluation by using |vgs|=vdd*vgs_fact and |vds|=vdd*vds_fact to obtain the peak IDS of the transistor. Library Simulator then reports ids * ipeak_fact in the output file as the peak current of the transistor.
The format of the output file is a list of transistor names followed by the scaled peak current of the transistor.
This syntax contains the following parameters:

- format Specifies the output report file format. Valid options are binary or ASCII. The default is binary, because you use binary output files from the simulator as input to Lightning (VST) for power-grid analysis.

- file Specifies an output file other than the default of vsrc_name .ipeak. If you use the pwrnet specify command to provide a power specification before using the pwrnet ipeak command, the simulator splits the output file into two files:
- filename Specifies the name of the file to which the simulator prints the information.
- vds_fact Specifies a multiplication factor used with the drain-to-source voltage. The default is 1.
- vgs_fact Specifies a multiplication factor used with the gate-to-source voltage. The default is 1.
- ipeak_fact Specifies a multiplication factor used with the peak current through the device. The default is 1.
- vsrc_name Specifies the name of the voltage source: VDD or another source name.
The following line prints the worst-case current through all the devices connected to VDD to a file named vdd.ipeak :
The pwrnet on command activates (and pwrnet off deactivates) the storing and tallying of power-net device current data during dynamic analysis. The default is pwrnet on.

The pwrnet report command generates a report containing the RMS, maximum, and average results of individual device current tallies. Initiate tallies by using the pwrnet tally command. (See "pwrnet tally" .) Library Simulator reports device currents only if the current is greater than 1.0e -20 amperes.
Vsrc_name specifies the name of the voltage source for which you previously performed the pwrnet tally command, and the prefix for the .rms , .max , and .avg output files.
The following line prints tallied current RMS, maximum, and average information to files named vdd.rms , vdd.max , and vdd.avg , respectively:

The pwrnet specify command calculates the total power consumption for blocks or the current for individual devices. This current value is used during static power-grid analysis. You can use the pwrnet specify command to pre-assign a current consumption number for both analog and digital circuitry.
When you use the pwrnet specify command to provide a power specification before using the pwrnet ipeak command, the simulator splits the output into two files:
- vsrc_name .aipeak , which contains the current data for all the devices or the calculated total power consumption for blocks. This information is obtained by using the pwrnet specify command.
- vsrc_name .ipeak , which contains the current data for the rest of the devices.
In Lightning (VST), load the .ipeak file first, and scale the current data. Lightning (VST) then loads the .aipeak file.
This syntax contains the following parameters:
- DEV = device_name Specifies the name of the device for which the power or current value is specified.
- DEF = subcircuit_definition Specifies the name of the definition of a subcircuit for which the power or current value is specified. The specified values apply to all instances of the subcircuit.
- INST = subcircuit_instance Specifies the name of an instance of a subcircuit for which the power or current value is specified.

- group_file = filename Specifies the name of the file containing analog circuits described as device groups. The format of the group file is as follows: list_of_groups : group | list_of_groups group : group_name group_attributes { list_of_layout_devices } EOF This syntax contains the following parameters:

- bjt_ratio = value Specifies the ratio of total current drawn by all bipolar junction transistors (BJTs) in the subcircuit. You can only specify this value with the DEF or INST option. If you do not specify a value, the simulator uses the default value set by the PwrnetSpecifyBjtRatio environment variable.

- res_ratio = value Specifies the ratio of the total current drawn by all resistors in the subcircuit. You can only specify this value with the DEF or INST option. If you do not specify a value, the simulator uses the default value set by the PwrnetResRatio environment variable.

- diode_ratio = value Specifies the ratio of total current drawn by all diodes in the subcircuit connected to a power grid. You can only specify this value with the DEF or INST option. If you do not specify a value, the simulator uses the default value set by the PwrnetDiodeRatio environment variable.
- POWER = value Specifies the power consumption for the specified subcircuit definition, instance, or device.
- CURRENT = value Specifies the current drawn by the specified subcircuit definition, instance, or device.
In the following example, a device named m1 draws a current of 0.2 μA:
In the next example, a subcircuit block named x1 dissipates 0.1 mW of power. The specified power value is divided among all the devices connected to voltage source nodes in the block.
In the example that follows, all subcircuit instances of the nand2 cell draw 0.1 μA of current:
pwrnet specify DEF = nand2 current = 0.1u bjt_ratio = 0.8

The cell contains BJT devices, and bjt_ratio is defined as 80 percent of the total (0.1 μA) current. This percentage is divided among the BJTs, and the remaining 20 percent is divided among the rest of the devices connected to the voltage supply node. Similarly, if the device contains resistors or diodes, the percentage of the total current defined by res_ratio or diode_ratio is divided among the resistors or diodes. The percentage of the total current remaining is then divided among the other devices, for example, MOSFETs.
Finally, in this example, the simulator reads a group file named file.grp :
The contents of file.grp are as follows:

The group file contains two device groups. In group1 , the specified devices, m1 and m2 , both collectively draw 0.2 μA of current. In group2 , the two devices dissipate 0.1 μW of power. The distribution of current in individual devices is computed as described earlier in this section. If the same device appears in multiple groups, the device current computed in the last group is used.

The pwrnet tally command tallies the average, maximum, and RMS currents of all transistors connected to the specified voltage source for the current dynamic simulation. The pwrnet report command creates a report containing these tallies.
Vsrc_name specifies the name of the voltage source.
The following line tallies current drawn by all the transistors connected to the vdd voltage source:

The pwrnet tallyint command reports the dynamic current of all devices connected to the specified voltage source during dynamic simulation. The current information is used by Lightning (VST) for dynamic analysis of the power grid connected to the voltage source.

Use pwrnet tallyint before running a dynamic simulation. Library Simulator calculates peak, RMS, and average current for each device during each interval, and averages these measurements over as many periods as fit into the duration of the simulation.
Library Simulator stores the data obtained in three files:
- The first file, filename .ptimax , contains the peak dynamic current of all devices connected to the voltage source.
- The second file, filename .ptirms , contains the RMS current of all devices connected to the voltage source.
- The third file, filename .ptiavg , contains the average current.
In all cases, the default file name is the name of the voltage source. You can specify another file by using the file option.
This syntax contains the following parameters:
- filename Specifies the output file name.
- method Specifies the method that the simulator uses in performing tallyint calculations. It is a three-position variable, with each position containing a 0 or a 1:

- intervals Specifies how many intervals the simulator tallies. Library Simulator divides each period into n number of intervals; calculates peak, RMS, and average current for each device during each interval; and averages these measurements over the periods of the simulation.

- period Specifies the portion of the dynamic simulation for which the simulator tallies the current of each device. Library Simulator divides the duration of the simulation by time to derive as many periods as it takes to cover the entire simulation. Each period is in seconds.
- time Specifies the number of units of time. Include the unit identifier:
- vsrc_name Specifies the voltage source connected to the power grid.

The following line tallies the currents for each of the devices connected to the vdd voltage source. Library Simulator tallies for 100 intervals over a period of 10 ns, reporting the maximum average and RMS current over all periods. The simulator calculates the average current for each interval as the average over all periods, and calculates the maximum and RMS current for each interval as the maximum current in every interval over all periods. The current information generated by the tallyint command is used by Lightning (VST) to perform power-grid analysis.
pwrnet tallyint file=vdd method=010 intervals=100 period=10ns vdd

The pwrnet tglon command activates (and pwrnet tgloff deactivates) all power-net tallying during dynamic simulation. Library Simulator performs power-net tallying when any specified node toggles high or low, depending on your specification, for count number of times. If you do not specify the high or low switch, the simulator counts all toggles. If you do not specify count , the simulator activates (or deactivates) all power-net tallying each time a node toggles.
This syntax contains the following parameters:

- params Measured power-net information against a specific set of parameters. You build toggle-count parameter sets by using the tglcnt params command. (See the "tglcnt params" .) If you omit this argument, the simulator uses default toggle-count parameters. You can find the defaults in the description of tglcnt params .
- tglcnt_param Specifies the name of the parameter set from which the simulator determines vt .
- n Specifies the number of node toggles that the simulator registers before activating or deactivating power-net tallying and output.
- high Counts the number of times that a node toggles high.
- low Counts the number of times that a node toggles low.
- ngroup Specifies the name of a predefined group of nodes for which the simulator registers toggling. Use the group nodes command to establish node groups.
- node Specifies the name of a node for which the simulator registers toggling.
The following line turns power-net checking off at the fourth high toggle of the node named clock:
pwrnet tgloff params=tgl_prm count=4 high clock
The pwrnet time command turns power-net checking on or off during the specified time interval. The default is on.
Time specifies the number of units of time. Include the unit identifier:
The following line turns power-net checking on 30 ns into a simulation:
The region command works with region tolerances.
The region commands are the following:

The region params command defines a set of region tolerances. You can control tolerances on a region-by-region basis. This command enables you to define or change tolerance parameters. You then can use the region setn and region setd commands to assign these parameters to specific regions.
This syntax contains the following parameters:
- default Specifies the default parameter set. Specify default to change the default values for the duration of this session.
- param_name Specifies the name of the parameter set containing a specific value for one or more parameters.

- rtol= voltage Specifies the relative region voltage tolerance. This tolerance is the relative voltage tolerance in the region linear relaxation convergence calculation. The default is set by the DefaultRegionRelativeTolerance .simplexrc environment variable.

- atol= voltage Specifies the absolute region voltage tolerance. This tolerance is the absolute voltage tolerance in the region linear relaxation convergence calculation. The default is set by the DefaultRegionAbsoluteTolerance .simplexrc environment variable.

- vstep= voltage Specifies the voltage step, which determines how often a region and its fanouts are processed with respect to the voltage changes in the region. Smaller voltage steps yield higher accuracy in region behavior at the cost of higher processing time. Small voltage steps are recommended for regions containing nodes exhibiting smaller voltage fluctuations, such as analog nodes and sense amplifiers. The default is set by the DefaultRegionEventThreshold .simplexrc environment variable.

- iter_max= n Specifies the maximum number of iterations, which is the maximum number of iterations allowed in region linear relaxation calculation. The default is set by the DefaultRegionIterationLimit .simplexrc environment variable.
- minstep= stepsize Specifies the minimum time step size used to evaluate the region. The default is set by the DefaultRegionMinTimeStep .simplexrc environment variable.
The following line resets two default region parameters, vstep and iter_max , for the duration of the current session:
The region setd command sets region tolerances for regions containing the specified devices.
This syntax contains the following parameters:

- params Applies specified region tolerances to the regions confining the specified devices. If you omit this argument, the simulator uses default region parameters. You can find the defaults in "region params" .
- region_param Specifies the name of the region parameter set to use in region processing. You build region parameter sets by using the region params command.
- device | dgroup Specifies the name of a device or predefined group of devices. Use the group device command to establish device groups.
The following line sets regions containing the r1 and mos2 devices to the tolerances defined by the rgn#1 parameter set:
The region setn command sets region tolerances for regions owning the specified nodes.
This syntax contains the following parameters:

- params Applies specified region tolerances to the region containing the specified nodes. If you omit this argument, the simulator uses default region parameters. You can find the defaults in the description of region params . (See "region params" .)
- region_param Specifies the name of the region parameter set that the program uses in region processing. Use the region params command to build region parameter sets.
- ngroup Specifies the name of a predefined group of nodes. Use the group nodes command to establish node groups.
- node Specifies the name of a node.

The typical output of a stats report follows:
