The tglcnt command works with toggle count information.

A toggle is either rising past the logic voltage level or falling past the logic voltage level. You can define the logic voltage level by using the optional tglcnt params command. (See "tglcnt params" .)
The tglcnt commands are the following:

The tglcnt check command checks the toggle count using the parameters specified in the tglcnt_param parameter set. Use this command to detect nodes that toggle excessively in an interval of time. Excessive toggling is associated with circuit hazards.
This syntax contains the following parameters:

- params Measures toggle information by using a specific set of parameters. Use the tglcnt params command to build toggle parameter sets. (See "tglcnt params" .) If you omit this argument, the simulator uses default toggle parameters. You can find the defaults in the description of tglcnt params .
- tglcnt_param Specifies the name of the parameter set that the simulator measures with.
- label Labels each message generated by this command.
- " label " Specifies the label text. Enclose this string in double quotation marks.
- ngroup Specifies the name of a predefined group of nodes. You establish node groups by using the group node command.
- node Specifies the name of a node.
The following line checks toggle frequency for node2 and node3 by using the parameters in the tglprm2 parameter set:
The tglcnt clear command clears toggle count information from tallies.
The tglcnt on command activates (and tglcnt off deactivates) the tallying and checking of toggling in the specified circuit. The default is tglcnt on.
The tglcnt params command specifies the voltage level used to define the toggle time of the specified node.
Use this command to define one or many timing sets. Because this command supports multiple node sensitivities, each timing set can be applied to any number of nodes.
This syntax contains the following parameters:
- default Specifies the default parameter set. Specify default to change the default values for the duration of this session.
- tglcnt_param Specifies the name of the parameter set containing a specific value for one or more parameters.
- vt= voltage Specifies the intermediate-logic threshold. The default is set by the DefaultParamVt .simplexrc environment variable.
- interval Specifies the duration of time during which the simulator counts toggles using the int_count option. The default is 0, resulting in every toggle counted.
- time Specifies the number of units of time. Include the unit identifier:

- int_count Specifies the number of toggles that the simulator counts within the interval specified by interval . The default is 1. Library Simulator takes a specified action when the toggle count is equal to or greater than the specified number within an interval.
The following line defines a trigger condition as two toggles within 1 ns:
The tglcnt report command reports the results of the individual node toggle count.
This syntax contains the following parameters:
- append Appends the report to the specified file.
- filename Specifies the name of the file to which the simulator appends the report.
The following line reports the results of the individual node toggle count:
The tglcnt tally command counts the number of toggles at a specified node.
If you do not specify params , the simulator uses the default, vt , which is set by the DefaultParamVt .simplexrc environment variable.
This syntax contains the following parameters:

- params Measures toggle tally information by using a specific set of parameters. Use the tglcnt params command to build toggle tally parameter sets. (See "tglcnt params" .) If you omit this argument, the simulator uses default timing tally parameters. You can find the defaults in the description of tglcnt params .
- tglcnt_param Specifies the name of the parameter set that the simulator uses to measure.
- label Labels each message generated by this command.
- " label " Specifies the label text. Enclose this string in double quotation marks.
- ngroup Specifies the name of a predefined group of nodes. Use the group nodes command to establish node groups.
- node Specifies the name of a node.
The following line tallies toggles for node1 and node2 using the parameters specified in the tgl_param1 parameter set:tglcnt tally params=tgl_param1 node1 node2

The vcd command controls input toggling to the simulator by using data specified in VCD (value change dump) format. Library Simulator reads the VCD file and, by default, drives the nodes listed in that file with the values listed in it.
In addition, the vcd command enables you to use the data from the VCD file to compare logic-level simulation with results from the simulator.
This syntax contains the following parameters:

- signal_filename Specifies the name of the signal declaration file, which defines directions for the signals placed in the VCD files. If you do not specify a signal declaration file, the simulator considers all signals in the VCD file to be of type input and therefore all values in the VCD file to be stimuli. See "Signal Declaration File" for complete information on signal declaration files.
- action_filename Specifies the name of the action file. This file defines the following:
- scope_id Defines the scope of the command. This parameter acts as a selector for the signal declaration and action file. If you do not specify a scope, the default scope is the entire VCD file.
- reset Executes the reset command before executing the vcd command.
- vcd_filename Specifies the name of the VCD file. See "VCD File" for information on the VCD file.
The following line uses dump1 as the VCD input and resets the circuit before driving the node:
The VCD file is the standard Verilog VCD file described in OVI documents and IEEE 1364. Library Simulator uses the following conventions to translate VCD values into input stimuli.
Within the VCD file, these VCD values have the following meanings during driving:
- 0 Forces the associated node to logic low.
- 1 Forces the associated node to logic high.
- X Forces the associated node to logic unknown.

The following extensions of the VCD format are supported. You can use these command extensions in the comment section of a VCD file to specify node characteristics; however, Simplex recommends that you specify node sets and parameters in the action file.
Use these extensions to specify properties such as input voltages and rise and fall times of nodes listed in the VCD file.
- *scope Sets the scope of the nodeset command by using the scope command. Scope_string is prepended to the nodes listed in the nodeset command. The syntax is as follows: *scope scope_string *end

- *nodeset Groups nodes in the VCD file into node sets. You can then set properties of the nodes in the node set by using the params command on the node set. The syntax is as follows: *nodeset set_number [ node_name ]+ *end

- *params Assigns parameters to sets of nodes by using the params command. The syntax is as follows: *params set_number <vin0= voltage > <vin1= voltage > <vinx= voltage ><tir= time > <tif= time > *end
Following is a sample VCD file named vcd . It is the same as that shown in "stats" but is in VCD format.
The signal declaration file defines directions for the signals placed in the VCD files. The signal declaration file contains blocks of the following form.
This syntax contains the following parameters:
- keyword Specifies the signal direction. It can be any of the following:
- list_of_signal_names Lists the simple signal identifiers. Separate multiple names with commas.
The following line checks the signal levels in the out[1] , out[2] , and out[3] signals:

The action file defines the times and events at which the simulator performs checking, executes commands, and toggles the state of the bidirectional signals. The action file contains the following kinds of statements.
The nodeset statement defines a set of signals for which a specific parameter set is applied.
Note: Braces ( { } ) enclose the signal names. You must enter the braces.
This syntax contains the following parameters:
- set_number Specifies the number of the set that you are defining with this nodeset statement.

- signal_name Identifies a simple signal. Separate multiple names with commas. If you do not specify a signal name, the simulator checks all signals of type output and bidirectional that are toggled into the output state. The signal name must be defined in the VCD file.
- keyword Specifies the signal direction. Library Simulator applies the parameter set to all signals of this signal type. It can be any of the following:
Note: All nodeset and parameter statements must be set before the program's first check or the assign statement.
Following are three examples of the nodeset statement.
params 1 vin0=0.0 vin1=3.3 tir=0.1ns tif=0.1ns voutl=(-0.5,+0.5) vouth=(3.0,3.6) vth=(1.6,1.7);

All signals that are listed between the brackets are grouped into the node set labeled 1 . The label is used as the identifier for the following params statement, defining the voltage ranges for stimuli and checking the identified signals.
params 1 vin0=0v vin1=2.25v vinx=1.125v tir=0.1ns tif=0.1ns;

The second example uses keyword identifiers. All signals declared of type input are labeled with the node set identifier 1 . The signal direction can be either defined in the VCD file or declared in the signal file. The node set identifier specifies the stimuli waveform parameter. The second set groups all signals declared as output signals in the VCD file into node set 2 and defines the voltage ranges for checking during the simulation.
params 1 vin0=0v vin1=1.8v voutl=(-0.2v,0.2v) vouth=(1.6v,2.0v) tir=80ps tif=80ps;

An empty signal list for the nodeset statements groups into one node set all signals of type output, as well as all bidirectional signals that are toggled into output mode. The node set label defines the voltage ranges for validation.
Used for for netlist based detailed power-grid view generation. If set to true subckt is supported.

The errorIfDeviceIsBelowMinW_L variable determines whether the simulator terminates and issues an error message or continues processing and issues a warning if it encounters a device whose length or width is below the minimum length or width defined in the table models.

When you set this variable to true , the simulator terminates and issues an error message when it encounters a device whose length or width is below the minimum length or width defined in the table models. When you set it to false , the simulator issues a warning message and continues processing. The default is true .

In the following example, the simulator issues a warning message and continues processing when it encounters a device whose length or width is below the minimum length or width defined in the table models:
The forwardDiodeIpeakCurrent variable defines the current to apply for forward-biased parasitic MOS diodes.
As a result of this example, the simulator applies a current of 2e+6 amperes to forward-biased parasitic MOS diodes:

The gatePowerNetconnection variable determines if devices, whose only gate node is tied to the power supply, should also be reported in the tap file. If set to true all such devices will be reported as tap devices.
The groundName variable establishes the alternate name (besides 0, gnd, and ground) used to define the circuit ground node. This variable is used by every load command.
There is no default node name.
This line specifies a ground node name of VSS:
The hierarchySeparator variable establishes the hierarchical delimiter.
The default character is a period (.).
This example specifies a pipe (|) as the hierarchical delimiter:
Enables to write out all devices (including the zero cap and current) into the current region file whenever detailed dynamic views are created by LibGen with currents from UltraSim.
Library Simulator writes all the devices (even with zero cap and current) to the current region file.
The keepTopCell variable determines whether the simulator removes the top cell instantiation when writing out the current data, if the top level is made up of a single instance.

When you set this variable to true , the simulator retains the top cell instantiation when writing out the current data. When you set it to false , it removes the top cell instantiation. The default is false .
In the following example, the simulator keeps the top cell instantiation when writing the current data:
The logLimit variable controls the number of error messages (of each type) to be displayed.
The default value is 0, which causes no suppression of error messages.
The following line specifies a limit of 10 error messages:
The minimumCapacitance variable specifies the minimum capacitance value allowed in the simulator. Library Simulator issues a warning when the capacitance is less than this value.
The minimum capacitance is 0.05 farads in this example:
The minimumInductance variable specifies the minimum inductance value allowed in the simulator. Library Simulator issues a warning when the inductance is less than this value.
This example sets a minimum inductance value of 1e-20 henrys:

The minimumNodeCapacitance variable specifies the minimum total node capacitance allowed in the simulator. Library Simulator changes the node capacitance by this minimum value if the node capacitance is less than the specified value.
This line specifies a minimum node capacitance of 2e-18 farads:
The minimumResistance variable specifies the minimum resistance allowed in the simulator.
The netlistInlineComment variable specifies the comment character used for comments on input cards in the netlist.
This line specifies the pound sign (#) as the comment character:
The netlistTopCell variable specifies the top cell name in the netlist that must be defined in the command file during power calculation if the same netlist is defined inside a sub-circuit block.
The pwrnetSpecifyBjtRatio variable represents the ratio of the total current or power dissipated by all BJT devices in a subcircuit. It is used with the pwrnet specify command.
This example sets the BJT ratio to 0.5:

The pwrnetSpecifyCapCurrent variable can be used to allow specifying capacitor current in the pwrnet specify command. To allow specifying capacitor current set this value to true before loading the circuit.
The pwrnetSpecifyDiodeRatio variable represents the ratio of the total current or power dissipated by all diodes in a subcircuit. It is used with the pwrnet specify command.
The following example specifies a ratio of 0.02:
The pwrnetSpecifyResistorRatio variable represents the ratio of the total current or power dissipated by all resistors in a subcircuit. It is used with the pwrnet specify command.
This line sets the ratio at 1.0:
The quitOnCmdError variable determines whether the simulator terminates and issues an error message if an error occurs during the execution of a command defined in a command file.
When you set this variable to true , the simulator aborts the execution when it encounters an error; when you set it to false , the simulator continues processing. The default is false .
In the following case, the simulator stops execution when it encounters an error:

This section lists the PowerMeter options (Power Objects) that do not have an equivalent option in Voltus. PowerMeter uses Power Objects, which indicate the kind of power, the type of method used to calculate the instance-based power consumption, the default for the frequency and voltage, the interconnect parasitic used, the clock specification for the design, and many others. These Power Objects can be included in Voltus using the set_power_include_file command.
Allows the user to force triangular estimation on certain cells, even when the ECSM information for those cells is available.

data: Specifies the name of the power object. It can be any TCL string.
The name of the cell that will use triangular waveform estimates rather than the enhanced ECSM information in the .lib file.

lines: - The following example causes PowerMeter to ignore the ECSM extension information for the xor cells: chip_Pwr calCellsToUseWaveEst xor
Allows the user to force triangular estimation on certain cells, even when the ECSM information for those cells is available.

data: Specifies the name of the power object. It can be any TCL string.
Specifies the file that includes the names of cells that will use triangular waveform estimates rather than the enhanced ECSM information in the .lib file.

lines: - The following example causes PowerMeter to ignore the ECSM extension information for the cells listed in triangular_file.txt : chip_Pwr calCellsToUseWaveEstFile triangular_file.text
You can also specify the name of a file that contains the transition time for specific pins or instances in the design.

data: Specifies the name of the power object. It can be any TCL string.
Specifies the name of the file containing the transition time for the pins or instances in the design. See "Example" for the format of this file and an example of it.

lines: Note: There are three ways that PowerMeter gets transition times (slews): 1) from TWF, 2) a transition time file ( pin_transition_time_file ) , or 3) a default transition time ( rDefaultTransitionTime ). Other than TWF where thresholds are given, the slew thresholds for pin transition time file and default transition time will be assumed to be 0 to 100 by default. If rUseLibAdjustTransitionTime switch is on, the slew thresholds will be assumed to be the ones defined in the first liberty (.lib) file.
Note: Any negative transition time read from command file or other input file will generate a fatal error.
- The following example specifies a pin transition-time file named mydesign.slew : ChipPwr calPinTransitionTime mydesign.slew

- Here is an example of the pin transition-time file: VERSION "1.0" CREATION "12" CREATOR "NOIDEA" PROGRAM "power.tcl" DIVIDERCHAR "_" DELIMITERCHAR "/" DESIGN "PG22E2_audclk" UNITS "TIME 1ps" InstanceName/PinName Max-Rise Max-Fall Min-Rise Min-Fall ---------------------------------------------------------- SLOPES ; - Logic0/input 0.000000 0.000000 0.000000 0.000000 - BITCLK 0.519086 0.350941 0.519086 0.350941 - U123/A 0.519086 0.350941 0.519086 0.350941 - sm2288_clkdiv_110_clk_reg/Q 0.100453 0.062533 0.100453 0.062533 - sm2288_clkdiv_111_U70/A 0.291355 0.098510 0.291355 0.098510 - sm2288_clkdiv_111_U70/B 0.092222 0.070174 0.092222 0.070174 - sm2288_clkdiv_111_U73/A 0.101057 0.073799 0.101057 0.073799 END ; The format of the pin transition-time file is as follows: instance_name/pin_name max_rise max_fall min_rise min_fall This syntax contains the following parameters:
1. Perform a delay or timing calculation before outputting the transition times.

- Read in the DSPF or SPEF file by using the read_parasitics command.

Sometimes PowerMeter may guess that a particular net is a power rail when it is not. For these cases the calRailExclude power object may be used.

data: Specifies the name of the power object. It can be any TCL string.
The name of the net that is not a rail.

lines: - This example specifies that vdd_dummy is not a rail. chipPwr calRailExclude vdd_dummy

When you use the vectorless timing-windows-based method of dynamic power-consumption calculation, you can switch all cell types in the design from 0 to 1 or 1 to 0 to observe the impact of simultaneously switching outputs on them. This feature is useful when you want to focus on a particular problem involving different cell types.

You can specify the name of a file that contains the names of all cell types to be switched from 0 to 1 or 1 to 0 and the minimum loading capacitance for each. The instance will be selected for power calculation only when its loading capacitance exceeds the minimum capacitance. See "rDynamicSwitchAllClockBuffers" for the format and an example of this file.

data: Specifies the name of the power object. It can be any TCL string.

Specifies the name of the file containing the optional minimum loading capacitance value for each cell type in the design. The instance will be selected for power calculation only when its loading capacitance exceeds the minimum capacitance. See "rDynamicSwitchAllClockBuffers" for information on the format of this file and an example of it.

lines: The switching cells file is an optional input to PowerMeter for dynamic analysis. It is an ASCII file. Each line is in the following format:
- The following example specifies a switching cells file called switch_599 : ChipPwr calSwitchingCells switch_599
- Switching cells file example cakbuf1 20fF ff1 15fF drv1
The instance will be selected for power calculation only when its loading capacitance exceeds the minimum capacitance.
Specifies a default net capacitance value if no parasitic capacitance is available.

data: Specifies the name of the power object. It can be any TCL string.
Specifies the default net capacitance. Default is 0.

lines: - The following example specifies a default net capacitance of 0.5 picofarads: ChipPwr rDefaultNetCap 0.5e-12

data: Specifies the name of the power object. It can be any TCL string.
Specifies the default pin capacitance. Default is 0.

lines: - The following example specifies a default pin capacitance of 10 femtofarads: ChipPwr rDefaultPinCap 0.01e-12

When you use the vectorless timing-windows-based method of dynamic power-consumption calculation, you can direct PowerMeter to switch all buffer cells that have a loading capacitance greater than the specified value and observe the impact.

When this debugging command is issued, only these specified cells are switched during dynamic analysis. rDynamicSwitchAllBuffers only schedules the buffer cells in the design giving user the visibility of the buffers as they switch. Scheduling is still done using the timing window of the buffer pins in TWF.

data: Specifies the name of the power object. It can be any TCL string.

Specifies a loading capacitance, in farads, for the buffers, above which PowerMeter switches them from 0 to 1 or 1 to 0. If the threshold value is less than 0.0, the switching feature is considered off.

lines: - In the following example, PowerMeter switches all buffers whose loading capacitance is greater than 30 femtofarads: ChipPwr rDynamicSwitchAllBuffers 30fF
When you use the vectorless timing-windows-based method of dynamic power-consumption calculation, you can switch all clock buffers in the design and observe the impact.

When this debugging command is issued, only these specified cells are switched during dynamic analysis. rDynamicSwitchAllClockBuffers only schedules the clock buffers in the design giving user the visibility of the clock network switching. Scheduling is still done using the timing window of the clock buffer pins in TWF.

data: Specifies the name of the power object. It can be any TCL string.

If set to 1, turns off the switching of the clock buffers 
 If set to 0, turns on the switching of the clock buffers. 
 This command has no default setting.

lines: - The following example directs PowerMeter to switch the clock buffers: ChipPwr rDynamicSwitchAllClockBuffers 1

When you use the vectorless timing-windows-based method of dynamic power-consumption calculation, you can direct PowerMeter to switch all flip-flops and latches that have a loading capacitance greater than the specified value and observe the impact.

When this debugging command is issued, only these specified cells are switched during dynamic analysis. rDynamicSwitchAllFlops only schedules the flip-flops and latches in the design giving user the visibility of the flip-flop and latches as they switch. Scheduling is still done using the timing window of the flip-flop and latch pins in TWF.

data: Specifies the name of the power object. It can be any TCL string.

Specifies a loading capacitance, in farads, for the flip-flops and latches, above which PowerMeter switches them from 0 to 1 or 1 to 0. If the threshold value is less than 0.0, the switching feature is considered off. 
 This command has no default setting

lines: - In the following example, PowerMeter switches all flip-flops and latches whose loading capacitance is greater than 20 femtofarads: ChipPwr rDynamicSwitchAllFlops 20fF
To specify whether blackbox propagation takes clock pins and timing arcs into account.
The following definitions are needed for the output activity descriptions in the syntax section below.
- Blackbox A blackbox is any instance that has no functional information. For PowerMeter there are two types of blackbox instances:
- Designated clock pin A pin on an instance that is designated as a clock because of a .lib clock tag (e.g. clock: true)
- Propagated clock pin A pin on an instance (with or without a .lib) that has no clock tag, but has a clock network propagated to it.
- Related pin An input pin that has a timing arc related to an output pin.

data: Specifies the name of the power object. It can be any TCL string.

If set to 0, blackbox propagation does not take clock pins and timing arcs into account. 
 If set to 1, see behavior described below. 
 Default is 0.

lines: If rEhancedBlackboxMode is set to 1, the output activity will be as follows:
- Cell with .lib and timing arcs but no functional information.
- Cell with .lib but with no timing arcs or functional information.

If a cell has multiple designated clock pins, the one with the highest activity will be considered to be the clock pin and any other clock pins will be treated as normal inputs. Propagated clock pins have lower priority and will be treated as normal inputs as well.

If a cell has no designated clock pins but multiple propagated clock pins, the propagated clock pin with the highest activity will take precedence. Other propagated clock pins will be treated as normal inputs.
- The following example specifies that clock pins and timing arcs are taken into account for blackbox propagation: chip_Pwr rEnhancedBlackboxMode 1
Specifies to use the average toggle rate of related inputs for enhanced blackbox propagation.

data: Specifies the name of the power object. It can be any TCL string.

When you set this to 1, PowerMeter uses the average toggle rate of related inputs for enhanced blackbox propagation. When you set this to 0, this functionality is not considered. 
 Default : 0

lines: Specifies to use the maximum toggle rate of related inputs for enhanced blackbox propagation.

data: Specifies the name of the power object. It can be any TCL string.

When you set this to 1, PowerMeter uses the maximum toggle rate of related inputs for enhanced blackbox propagation. When you set this to 0, this functionality is not considered. 
 Default : 0
Specifies the name of the power object. It can be any TCL string.

A value of 1 ignores the capacitance of bidirectional pins in switching power calculation (internal power calculation remains unaffected). 
 Default : 0

lines: When using clock domain-based assignments (ChipPwr rNoClockDomains 0) and if a net is assigned to multiple clock domains, the rUseFastestClockDomain setting will determine if the highest or lowest frequency will be used.

data: Specifies the name of the power object. It can be any TCL string.

With a value of 0, PowerMeter will use the lowest frequency for clock domain-based activity assignment. 
 With a value of 1, PowerMeter will use the highest frequency for clock domain-based activity assignment. The default is 1.

lines: This command will be ignored if rNoClockDomains is set to 1.

Note: When the activity of an input signal is unknown (i.e. no input activity from a file such as VCD or TCF), it will use the product of the default input activity and the frequency of the fastest (or slowest) clock associated with any pin connected to the net.
- The following example specifies that PowerMeter will use the highest frequency for domain-based activity. ChipPwr rUseFastestClockDomain 1
Provides a way of telling PowerMeter to use the transition threshold from the first .lib.

data: Specifies the name of the power object. It can be any TCL string.

When set to 0, PowerMeter will treat slew thresholds as 0-100%. 
 When set to 1, slews specified by rDefaultTransitionTime and pin transition time file will use thresholds specified in the first .lib. 
 The default is 0.

lines: Note: For TWF flow, the voltage thresholds given in TWF will be used irrespective of the value of this switch.
- The following example specifies that the transition threshold should be 0 - 100: chip_Pwr rUseLibAdjustTransitionTime 0
Determines whether PowerMeter uses Enhanced Port or Quick Detailed Views or not.

data: Specifies the name of the power object. It can be any TCL string.

If set to 0 enhanced port views are ignored. 
 If set to 1, enhanced port or quick detailed views take precedence over all other views (including .lib). 
 The default is 0 .

lines: - The following example tells PowerMeter not to use Enhanced Port views: ChipPwr rUseUserDefinedCurrent 0
Allows the user to force triangular estimation on certain cells, even when the ECSM information for those cells is available.

data: Specifies the name of the power object. It can be any TCL string.
The name of the cell that will use triangular waveform estimates rather than the enhanced ECSM information in the .lib file.

lines: - The following example causes PowerMeter to ignore the ECSM extension information for the xor cells: chip_Pwr calCellsToUseWaveEst xor
Allows the user to force triangular estimation on certain cells, even when the ECSM information for those cells is available.

data: Specifies the name of the power object. It can be any TCL string.
Specifies the file that includes the names of cells that will use triangular waveform estimates rather than the enhanced ECSM information in the .lib file.

lines: - The following example causes PowerMeter to ignore the ECSM extension information for the cells listed in triangular_file.txt : chip_Pwr calCellsToUseWaveEstFile triangular_file.text
You can also specify the name of a file that contains the transition time for specific pins or instances in the design.

data: Specifies the name of the power object. It can be any TCL string.
Specifies the name of the file containing the transition time for the pins or instances in the design. See "Example" for the format of this file and an example of it.

lines: Note: There are three ways that PowerMeter gets transition times (slews): 1) from TWF, 2) a transition time file ( pin_transition_time_file ) , or 3) a default transition time ( rDefaultTransitionTime ). Other than TWF where thresholds are given, the slew thresholds for pin transition time file and default transition time will be assumed to be 0 to 100 by default. If rUseLibAdjustTransitionTime switch is on, the slew thresholds will be assumed to be the ones defined in the first liberty (.lib) file.
Note: Any negative transition time read from command file or other input file will generate a fatal error.
- The following example specifies a pin transition-time file named mydesign.slew : ChipPwr calPinTransitionTime mydesign.slew

Specifies the text of the label.
Specifies the location of the text label, in microns.
Specifies the name of the layer on which the text label resides. This name must be the same as that in the technology file generated by RCgen.

lines: - The following example indicates that a text label called vdd will identify a port located at 8.0, 0.2 on the metal_4 layer: label port vdd 8.0 0.2 METAL_4
- In the next example, a text label called vdd identifies a net with a geometrical shape located at 8.5, 5.8 on the metal_1 layer: label net vdd 8.5 5.8 METAL_1
This command defines layer mapping in order to create physical layers from multiple GDS layers.
The layer commands are forwarded to extraction and influence only the top-level extraction.

data:
Maps an input layer name to a target layer name. This command is used to map the layer name used in the input DEF file to a layer name in the technology file. 
 def_layer_name Specifies the name of the layer in the DEF file. tch_layer_name Specifies the name of the layer in the technology file.

Maps the layer name and the layer type in the GDS file to a physical layer. 
 gds_layer_name Specifies the layer name in the GDS file. gds_layer_number Specifies the layer number in the GDS file. It must be an integer between 0 and 255. gds_data_type Specifies an optional number of the subclass for the GDSII layer. It must be an integer between 0 and 255. [port|text] Determines whether the geometrical shapes on the given layer are used to generate ports or the layer is used to attach text to a specific physical layer.

lines: - The following example maps the DEF layer name, METAL1, to the layer name in the technology file, METAL_1 : layer map METAL1 METAL_1
- The next example specifies the layer name, the layer type, and the data type in the GDS file: layer gds METAL1 16 0

- The last example specifies the layer name, the layer type, and the data type in the GDS file and indicates that the layer be used to attach text to a specific physical layer: layer gds METAL1 16 0 text

This command sets a bias value, in microns, by which to undersize all wires on a defined layer. It must be in the range of -1 to 1. A negative value will make the wire larger. This value is applied to each edge of the wire, decreasing the width by two times the bias value. You can select a different bias value for resistance extraction and capacitance extraction.

data: Specifies the name of the layer containing the wires to be biased.
Specifies the amount, in microns, by which to bias the wires. A positive value undersizes the width of the wire.

lines: - The following example biases each edge of the wires in the m5 layer by two tenths of a micron: layer_bias m5 0.2 The results would resemble the diagram shown in the following figure:
This command specifies the name of the OpenAccess 2.2 database generated by Cadence Innovus or Virtuoso or other applications that is to be used as input to VoltageStorm PE.
You must use the oa_view_name and top_cell commands with this command. See "oa_view_name" for information on the oa_view_name command.

data: Specifies the name of the OpenAccess database.

Specifies the path to the lib.defs file, which contains the path name of the OpenAccess database. This parameter is optional. Use it if the OpenAccess database resides in a directory other than the default directory.

lines: - The following example directs VoltageStorm PE to search for the cpu library in the ../home/cpu_pc788 directory: oa_library cpu ../home/cpu_pc788/lib.defs oa_view_name netlist top_cell NFD2QP
This command specifies the type of view to use when an OpenAccess 2.2 database is input to VoltageStorm PE.
You must use the oa_library and top_cell commands with this command. See "oa_library" for information on the oa_library command.

data: Specifies the type of view. This command has no default, but abstract is usually used.

lines: - This example specifies that the netlist view be used: oa_library cpu oa_view_name netlist top_cell NFD2QP
print iv filename <critical> [best_case | worst_case | average] <effective> < timing_window >

This command places in a specified file the results of filtering all power-grid data for the specified analysis type and the specified area, layer, or selected nodes or resistors. It can also print the worst-case data for the specified analysis types, as well as the time that the worst case occurred. It is similar to the list command, except that it places the data in a file rather than displaying it on the screen. The filter for the analysis type must be set for the print command to report data.
The output data contains the following information:
- The data value of the element or node
- The element name or node name
[WARNING] The output file can be very large for large netlists.

data: Specifies one of the analysis types shown below.
Specifies the name of the output file.

The command " print -iv filename" is for printing instance-based supply voltage data. This data contains instance-specific IR drop information suitable for use in Tempus. 
 Note: When setvar save_voltage_waveforms true environment variable is set before a print iv command, a binary file is created, not ascii instance supply voltage file. If this variable is not set before a print iv command, then the output will be an ascii instance supply voltage file. 
 The switching-window is the time interval during which non-leakage currents flow into the power and ground nets or when the gate is switching. In VCD flow the time is where a specific pin/net toggle is specified in the VCD file. PowerMeter then reads this information, computes the current waveform corresponding to the toggling and puts this waveform at the time specified in the VCD file. The switching window is the time interval of the current waveform due to the toggling. Similiarly in vectorless flow, when a pin toggles , PowerMeter computes a current waveform corresponding to the switching. The difference between vectorless flow and VCD flow is that in vectorless flow the toggling time for the pins are determined by Powermeter while in VCD flow the toggling time is given by the VCD file. 
 <critical> prints the instance voltage values at critical instances (Those with a negative slack value).This is important for timing aware decap analysis. 
 Using best_case provides an option to write the best-case supply voltage per instance over the time intervals defined by timing_window . The best-case supply voltage for an instance is the pin voltage of the connnected pin with the minimum IR -drop or ground-bounce over the timing-window time intervals (timing_window is required). 
 If none of the parameters <critical> <best_case | worst_case | average> < timing_window > is specified then the default dynamic behavior is the same as print iv filename worst_case . Since a timing window is not specified, the value may be too pessimistic for IR-drop aware delay calculation (i.e. Tempus).
Generates an ASCII report of all power gate cell instances. See discussion below.

Plots the available decoupling capacitance in the specified area. The available capacitance is calculated based on the available filler cells in the specified area and their matching decoupling cap cells.
Prints the decoupling density, which is the amount of decoupling capacitance in a given area of the design.

Prints the decoupling capacitance effectiveness measured by the ratio peak IR drop divided by the average IR drop. This option applies only to dynamic analysis and generates a warning message if only static current data is available. VoltageStorm PE calculates the average IR drop over the whole transient analysis.
Prints the decap required, which is the suggested amount of decap to add to fix IR-drop violations.
Prints the filler-cell density, which is the number of filler cells per given area.
Prints jrms current density data based on Irms current. Only supported for dynamic.

Prints instance-based supply voltage data. This data contains instance-specific IR drop information suitable for use in Tempus. 
 In order for VoltageStorm PE to print the file of instance-based supply voltage data, the print iv command must be preceded by either the analyze iv or filter iv auto command. 
 The format of this file is: 
 INSTANCESUPPLY no_inst bus_name nom_volt [P | G] 
 instance voltage cell [ power_pin [ signal_pins ]] 
 ... 
 no_inst is the number of instances attached to the bus bus_name is the name of the power or ground line nom_volt is the nominal supply voltage P | G defines power or ground instance is the instance name voltage is the instance voltage cell is the name of the cell power_pin is the name of the power pin (for multi supply multi voltage cells) signal_pin is the name of the signal pin (for multi supply multi voltage cells) 
 See " print iv filename <worst_case | average> <effective> < timing_window > " described above, for additional details for iv option. 
 Note: You must also set the variable report_msmv_format to create IR drop files for multi voltage cells.
Prints the current vs. IDsat and is used for seeing if too much current is flowing through power switches.
Prints voltage drop across power switches. See Power Gating chapter of VoltageStorm Cell_Level Rail Analysis User Guide for details on Power Gating.
Prints the voltage drop across resistors.
Prints the voltage drop across the package model for both static and dynamic analysis and the worst-case IR drop for the limit and linear plots.
Prints voltage across contacts and vias data.

lines: - print pi filename instances Generates an ASCII report of all power gate cell instances. Reports the instance name, cell name, Idsat threshold, total current and status. Reports the worst case voltage drop and suggests cell changes or cell additions. The report format is shown below:
Instance CellType Location Threshold Current IR Status ChangeCell AddCells
- The next line places all instance-based voltage data in the iv_results file, whose syntax follows: print iv iv_results

- and output file looks like ... bic2efc__ebGntDst_reg_ECO0NO1_2_L3_I1 1.754707e+00 BUFX8 ; bic2efc__ebGntDst_reg_ECO0NO1_2_L3_I12 1.756817e+00 BUFX8 ; bic2efc__ebGntDst_reg_ECO0NO1_2_L3_I11 1.756721e+00 BUFX8 ; bic2efc__ebGntDst_reg_ECO0NO1_2_L3_I21 1.756154e+00 BUFX8 ;
In the above figure we show an instance, INST1, of a multi voltage cell, CELL1, with a VDD1 of 3 volts and VDD2 of 5 volts. The following output files will result when doing a print iv iv_results
This command prints the settings of all the environment variables used in the current session of VoltageStorm PE.
- This example prints the setting of all the environment variables used in the current session. printvar
This command directs VoltageStorm PE to use the instance-based power-consumption data output in the state directory by previous runs.

Instances in the previous run are identified by placement and orientation rather than by instance names, so power data can be reused, even if instance names have changed. For example, instance names may change when you switch from DEF-based to mixed DEF and GDS or GDS-based runs. You can only use this command if the placement between the two design iterations has not been changed.
Note: reuse_state is only supported for static analysis.

data: Specifies the name of the state directory where the information on the instance-based power-consumption data from the previous run resides.

lines: - This example specifies that VoltageStorm PE reuse the data in the output files in the vs2_output directory: reuse_state ../vs2_output
Suppress the output of the messages for the message ids that are specified.

data:
The messages with the specified message ids will not be output. 
 Default : No messages are suppressed.

lines: - The below command suppresses the messages with the ID VST-1001 . suppress_message VST-1001
This command generates a current data file to use as a power-grid macromodel.

To obtain power-grid macromodeling current data, identify a via layer that divides the power grid into upper and lower portions. VoltageStorm PE characterizes the current drawn into this via layer from the grid above it and creates the file.
When VoltageStorm PE solves the upper portion of the grid, it uses the current data in these files to macromodel the effects of the lower portion of the grid on the upper portion.

data: Specifies the name of a via layer.
Specify the coordinates of a rectangular area for which via current data is to be generated.
Specifies the name of the output file.

lines: - This example creates a file called block.ivia, which contains the current characterization data from a rectangular area defined by the coordinates 65040, 385400, 790000, and 583000 in the via_1 layer: viacurrent via_1 650400 385400 790000 583000 block.ivia
This command produces ready-to-run command files that set up any of the following for all layers:
- Modifications performed on the power grid

data:
This parameter is equivalent to the following Voltus command parameter: create_die_model -type two_port . 
 For more information, refer to create_die_model in the Voltus Text Command Reference .

Writes the current filter settings for all analysis types using the filter command, where the filter command is written as follows: 
 filter analysis_type filter_id min max

This parameter is equivalent to the following Voltus command parameter: create_die_model -max_ports_per_net . 
 For more information, refer to create_die_model in the Voltus Text Command Reference .

Writes the current model parameters using the model command, where the model command is written as follows: 
 model layer < parameters = values >+

This parameter is equivalent to the following Voltus command parameter: create_die_model -output_directory . 
 For more information, refer to create_die_model in the Voltus Text Command Reference .

This parameter is equivalent to the following Voltus command parameter: create_die_model - rail_analysis_domain domain_name | -net net_name . 
 For more information, refer to create_die_model in the Voltus Text Command Reference .

A value of true enables port grouping. The default is true . 
 A value of false disables port grouping.
Writes the voltage sources in a power pin location file format.

Specifies the probe node file containing the probe point on a die. 
 Example: 
 write reduced_model output_directory nport_dir state_directory dom12_25C_dynamic_1 probing_node_file probe_file.txt power_domain dom12

Writes the voltage sources using the addvs command, where the addvs command is written as follows: 
 addvs vs_name x y layer res resistance
Generates a command file that you can use to restore modifications performed on the power grid. This file includes such commands as changeres , addres , addvia , addmvia , select , and unselect .
Specifies the name of the command file.

Writes the voltage of the interface nodes for a specific instance to the block boundary voltage file specified by filename . Instance_name indicates the name of the instance for which to specify voltage source locations. 
 The block boundary voltage file contains the voltage at the boundaries of a specific instance in the design. You can use it as input to the analysis of the block itself by using the block_boundary_voltage_file variable instead of the powerpin_location_file variable to specify the location and the value of the voltage at the boundaries of the block. 
 You can use this approach for the verification of hierarchical designs. At the full-chip level, use an abstract view of a block. Use the write block_boundary_voltage command to capture the boundary conditions of this block after the analysis. Then, with the block_boundary_voltage_file variable, verify the block by using the detailed view of the contents with the same boundary conditions as for the full-chip level. 
 The format of the block boundary voltage file is as follows: 
 putvsrc layer_name vsrc_name voltage x y

Adds a voltage source to the netlist at a specified location. 
 layer_name Specifies the name of the layer to which Lightning applies the voltage source. vsrc_name Specifies the name of the voltage source. voltage Specifies the value of the voltage source. x , y Specify the coordinates of the location at which to place the voltage source. The x , y coordinates are in units of 1/1000 of a micron.
Specifies the name of the layer to which Lightning applies the voltage source.

This parameter is equivalent to the following Voltus command parameter: create_die_model -type n_port . 
 For more information, refer to create_die_model in the Voltus Text Command Reference .

This parameter is equivalent to the following Voltus command parameter: create_die_model -repeat . 
 For more information, refer to create_die_model in the Voltus Text Command Reference .

This parameter is equivalent to the following Voltus command parameter: create_die_model -state_directory . 
 For more information, refer to create_die_model in the Voltus Text Command Reference .

This parameter is equivalent to the following Voltus command parameter: create_die_model -use_sigrity_repeat . 
 For more information, refer to create_die_model in the Voltus Text Command Reference . 
 The following list gives the use model mapping between Voltus and VoltageStorm for this parameter: 
 -repeat value ( Voltus ) => write [abstract_model | reduced_model] repeat= value ( VoltageStorm ) -repeat value -use_sigrity_repeat ( Voltus ) => write [abstract_model | reduced_model] use_sigrity_repeat= value ( VoltageStorm ) -use_sigrity_repeat ( Voltus ) => write [abstract_model | reduced_model] use_sigrity_repeat ( VoltageStorm - implying infinity repeat) no -repeat and no -use_sigrity_repeat ( Voltus ) => write [abstract_model | reduced_model] ( VoltageStorm )
Specifies the name of the voltage source.
Specifies the value of the voltage source.
Specify the coordinates of the location at which to place the voltage source. The x , y coordinates are in microns.

Forces the command to write additional dynamic block boundary voltage waveforms for the instance specified, in case dynamic analysis has been or will be performed. 
 When using the -watch option the setting of the save_transient_states variable should be taken into consideration. Below are two examples to help clarify this; 
 The first line of the below example tells VoltageStorm not to save the transient states. Next it specifies that the block boundary voltage waveforms are to be saved in files inst1.bbv and inst2.bbv for inst1 and inst2 respectively. Finally the dynamic analysis is run. By setting up the analysis in this way, less memory will be used, but at the cost of not being able to generate movies. 
 setvar save_transient_states false 
 ... 
 write block_boundary_voltage inst1 inst1.bbv -watch 
 write block_boundary_voltage inst2 inst2.bbv -watch 
 ... 
 analyze vdd 1.8 dynamic 1.65 ... 
 In this second example the first line tells VoltageStorm to save all the transient states. Next dynamic analysis is performed. Because all the transients states were saved, there is no need to use the -watch option of the write block_boundary_voltage command. The boundary voltage waveforms will be extracted from the saved transient states. 
 setvar save_transient_states true 
 ... 
 analyze vdd 1.8 dynamic 1.65 ... 
 ... 
 write block_boundary_voltage inst1 inst1.bbv 
 write block_boundary_voltage inst2 inst2.bbv

Generates a single TCL script with the specified file name containing ECO changes. 
 Following is an example of this file: 
 putvsrc metal_2 Vbc_1 1.688599e+00 1500.720 1556.320 
 putvsrc metal_2 Vbc_2 1.699221e+00 1500.720 0.440 
 putvsrc metal_2 Vbc_3 1.690036e+00 50.000 1556.440 
 putvsrc metal_2 Vbc_4 1.699057e+00 50.000 0.370

lines: - The following line creates a command file named cmd3 using the current filter settings: write filters cmd3
- The next line writes commands to restore all the resistor modifications and additions to the changes_for_ir.cmd file: write gridchanges changes_for_ir.cmd
- This line writes the voltage of the chip/h2/U1_T1 instance to the U1_T1.bbv file: write block_boundary_voltage chip/h2/U1_T1 U1_T1.bbv
This variable enables VoltageStorm PE to support auto via scaling.

data:
When scaling resistance on a specific metal layer, via resistance on the adjacent via layers are automatically scaled with the same scaling factor. 
 If set to false , via resistances are kept un-scaled. 
 Default: true

lines: The below variable specifies to perform auto via scaling.

This variable specifies the value of the area-based capacitance to apply to the static power-grid views for dynamic analysis. VoltageStorm PE applies this capacitance to all leaf cells that do not contain capacitance information. You may want to use this variable to specify capacitance by area if no decoupling capacitance was extracted by LibGen.

data:
Specifies the area-based capacitance to apply to the static power-grid views, in femtofarads per micron squared. 
 The default is 0 femtofarads

lines: - The following example specifies an area-based capacitance of 1 femtofarad per micron squared: setvar cell_area_capacitance 1 fF/um 2

This variable determines whether VoltageStorm PE automatically generates a label that identifies a net from the GDS file. Generate net labels only if the GDS file does not contain attributes or labels identifying the nets of interest for connectivity and parasitic extraction. Incorrectly placed voltage sources may cause false shorts when you generate net labels. Setting this variable is not required for a pure DEF flow.

data:
When this variable is set variable is set to true , VoltageStorm PE automatically generates labels that identify nets from the GDS file. 
 If you set this variable to false , VoltageStorm PE does not automatically generate net labels. 
 The default is false .

lines: - In the following example, VoltageStorm PE creates net labels for unidentified nets from the GDS file: setvar create_label_from_vsrc true
This variable specifies if the areas where static IR-drop violation occur are either to be excluded or to be included during dr decap analysis.

data:
When this variable is set to true , VoltageStorm PE will exclude areas where static IR-drop violations occur. 
 When this variable is set to false, VoltageStorm PE will not exclude areas where static IR-drop violations occur. 
 The default is true .

lines: - In the following example, areas where static IR-drop violations occur will be excluded when doing dr decap analysis: setvar decap_exclude_static_violation_area true
This variable controls the size of the windows used in the vs2fe_eco.tcl ECO file for adding the decoupling capacitance.

data: This variable defines the window size in microns when performing decoupling capacitance analysis (with dr option). The default is 200 microns.

lines: - In the following example, VoltageStorm PE sets the window size to 100 microns for adding decoupling capacitance: setvar decap_window_size 100

This variable determines how VoltageStorm PE handles any physical shorts that it detects in the DEF file. It can either merge the geometries of the shorted nets, treating them as electrically connected, or it can ignore them.

data:
When this variable is set to true , VoltageStorm PE merges the geometries of the shorted nets and treats them as electrically connected. 
 When this variable is set to false , VoltageStorm PE ignores the geometries of the nets not of interest. 
 The default is false.

lines: When you set this variable to true , VoltageStorm PE merges the geometries of the shorted nets and treats them as electrically connected. When you set it to false , VoltageStorm PE ignores the geometries of the nets not of interest. The default is false .
- As a result of this line, VoltageStorm PE merges the geometries of any shorted nets: setvar DEF_merge_supply_shorts true

A clip wire (tie-high or tie-low net) is connected by a small number of vias to the power stripe. The signal pin in a cell of a clip wire usually has almost zero current, so the via of a clip wire usually has no impact on the em check.
This variable provides the ability to discard the EM effect due to a clip wire.

data:
A value of false tells VoltageStorm to discard clip wires for em check. 
 A value of true will tell VoltageStorm to look at the current to see if it should be ignored. See "em_part_current_threshold" for setting the threshold value. 
 The default is false.

lines: - This example tells VoltageStorm not to check the current: setvar em_part_check_current false
This sets a threshold to be considered when "em_part_check_current" is set to true.
- This example sets the threshold to 0.5e-10 setvar em_part_current_threshold 0.5e-10.

This variable determines whether VoltageStorm PE issues an error message and terminates if the average power consumption is used for a dynamic analysis and the IR drop is outside the IR-drop limit, indicating an existing weakness in the power grid. You can use this variable during optimization of the power grid to override the peak power-consumption values that VoltageStorm PE uses by default. This variable only takes effect if the use_average_power_for_op variable is set to true . See "use_average_power_for_op" for more information on the use_average_power_for_op variable.

data:
When this variable is set to true , VoltageStorm PE issues an error message and terminates if the average power consumption is used for a dynamic analysis and the IR drop is outside the IR-drop limit. 
 When this variable is set to false , VoltageStorm continues processing if the IR drop is outside the IR-drop limit. 
 The default is false .

lines: - In this example, VoltageStorm PE issues an error message and terminates when the IR drop is outside the IR-drop limit: use_average_power_for_op true setvar error_on_op_analysis true
This variable specifies the executable to use for DEF- or GDS-based resistance extraction. In most cases, this executable is zx , which is Cadence's next-generation Fire & Ice extractor.

data:
Specifies the executable to use for DEF- or GDS-based resistance extraction. 
 The default executable name is zx, located in the installation directory.

lines: - The following example selects the Fire & Ice ZX extractor located in ../bin as the resistance extractor: setvar extractor ../bin/zx
This variable specifies the name of a file containing commands to submit to the DEF or GDS extractor.

data:
Specifies the name of a file containing commands to submit to the DEF or GDS extractor. 
 This variable has no default setting.

lines: - The following example identifies a file called ptgn_def_cmd , which is to be input to the DEF extractor. setvar extractor_include ptgn_def_cmd
This variable specifies if an analysis of a power grid without cell connections should halt or continue.

data:
If this variable is set to true the analysis of a power grid without cell connections will not continue. 
 If this variable is set to false the analysis of a power grid without cell connections will continue. This is useful to analyze feed-through power grids. 
 The default value is true.

lines: - This line causes VoltageStorm to halt on nets with no cell connections. setvar halt_on_net_with_no_cell_connections true
This variable specifies the separator between the net names in a hierarchical block.

data:
Specifies the separator beween the net names in a hierarchical block. 
 The default is a slash (/).

lines: - The following example sets the net name separator to be a pipe slash: setvar hierarchy_char | An example of a resulting net name is top|net23|net23_51.
This variable determines whether the internal cell power-grid nodes are visible during analysis when the analysis type is ir or tc .

data:
A value of true renders the internal cell and macro power-grid nodes visible and checks their voltage. 
 A value of false does not make them visible or check them. 
 The default is true .

lines: - The example that follows makes the internal cell and macro power-grid nodes invisible and does not check their voltage levels: setvar internal_nodes_visible false
This variable determines whether the internal cell power-grid resistors are visible and checks their current density and electromigration risk when the analysis type is rj , er , rc , or vv .

data:
A value of true renders the internal cell and macro power-grid resistors visible and checks their current density and electromigration risk. 
 A value of false does not check them or make them visible. 
 The default is true .

lines: - The following example makes the internal cell and macro power-grid resistors visible and checks their current density and electromigration risk: setvar internal_resistors_visible true

This variable specifies the name of the LibGen executable. LibGen is the library generator used by VoltageStorm PE when it builds a power-grid view library during execution of the generate_view command.

data: The name of the LibGen executable to use. The default executable name is libgen

lines: - The following line specifies a LibGen executable of libgen : setvar libgen_executable ../exec/libgen
This variable specifies the name of a file containing any extra LibGen commands to pass to LibGen when the generate_view command in VoltageStorm PE invokes LibGen to create a power-grid view.

data: The name of the file containing the LibGen commands.

lines: - As a result of the following example, VoltageStorm PE passes the commands in the pgv_u1236.txt file to LibGen when the generate_view command is used to invoke LibGen: setvar libgen_include pgv_u1236.txt
This variable specifies the expected lifetime of the design in years. It is useful in assessing electromigration risk.

data:
Specifies the expected lifetime in years. 
 The default value is 10 years.

lines: - The following example indicates that the expected lifetime of the design is 15 years: setvar lifetime 15
This variable sets the maximum number of error messages issued by VoltageStorm PE during the execution of a single command.

data:
This variable sets the maximum number of error messages issued by VoltageStorm PE during the execution of a single command. It must be an integer in the range of 1 through 10,000, inclusive. If an error occurs, VoltageStorm PE reports the total number of errors found after it executes a command 
 The default is 100.

lines: - The following example sets the maximum number of error messages at 500: setvar max_error_messages 500
This variable sets the maximum number of warning messages that VoltageStorm PE issues during the execution of a single command.

data:
This variable sets the maximum number of warning messages issued by VoltageStorm PE during the execution of a single command. It must be an integer from 1 through 10,000, inclusive. 
 The default is 100.

lines: - The following example sets the maximum number of warning messages at 50: setvar max_warning_messages 50
This variable specifies the name of the merging utility that will generate the RC database.

data:
This variable specifies the name of the merging utility that will generate the RC database. 
 The default executable is mergegrid .

lines: - Here is an example showing MergeGrid as the name of the utility that generates the RC database: setvar merge_util ../bin/mergegrid
This variable specifies a character used internally by VoltageStorm PE to separate instance names and pin names.

data:
This variable specifies a character used internally by VoltageStorm PE to separate instance names and pin names. 
 The default is a colon (:). If a colon is part of any pin name or instance name, select another character to use as the internal separator. 
 Do not use a backslash (\) as the separator character; it causes VoltageStorm PE to issue an error message.

lines: - An example is a design with a pin named vdd: or an instance named adder:2. Because the pound sign character (#) is not used by any pin name or instance name in the design, the following syntax selects it as the separator character: setvar pin_char #

This variable specifies the number of decimal places in the instance-based supply voltage data generated by the print iv command. This variable enables you to control the precision of the output. If you do not set this variable, VoltageStorm PE generates the output with four digits of precision.

data:
Specifies the number of decimal places in the instance-based supply voltage data generated by th p rint iv command. It is an integer. 
 There is no default value.

lines: - As an example, this line specifies values of six digits: setvar precision_digits 6 The output values will appear with six digits to the right of the decimal point, like this: 2.996926

This and prpgv_y_offset variables specify the offset in the X and Y directions, respectively, between the power-grid database data and the LEF file data, in microns. They are used to align these two sets of data in generating the hierarchical power-grid view. Often the DEF file input to VoltageStorm PE is specified in a centered coordinate system, and the LEF file for the cell is specified in a first-quadrant coordinate system (that is, its lower left corner is at (0 0)). VoltageStorm PE passes these two variables to LibGen. When LibGen loads power-grid view data, it adds the values of prpgv_x_offset and prpgv_y_offset to the location of the nodes to place them in the LEF coordinate system.

data:
This variables specify the offset in the X direction, between the power-grid database data and the LEF file data, in microns. 
 The default value f is 0 microns.

lines: - Given these specifications in the DEF file for a cell: UNITS DISTANCE MICRONS 100 DIEAREA ( -5000 -5000 ) ( 5000 5000 ) and this data in the LEF MACRO for the same cell: ORIGIN ( 0 0 ) SIZE 100 BY 100 the appropriate setting of these variables would be as follows: setvar prpgv_x_offset 50 setvar prpgv_y_offset 50 These settings would shift the power-grid data extracted from the DEF file 50 microns to the right and 50 microns up to align with the LEF macro data.

The prpgv_x_offset and prpgv_y_offset variables specify the offset in the X and Y directions, respectively, between the power-grid database data and the LEF file data, in microns. They are used to align these two sets of data in generating the hierarchical power-grid view. Often the DEF file input to VoltageStorm PE is specified in a centered coordinate system, and the LEF file for the cell is specified in a first-quadrant coordinate system (that is, its lower left corner is at (0 0)). VoltageStorm PE passes these two variables to LibGen. When LibGen loads power-grid view data, it adds the values of prpgv_x_offset and prpgv_y_offset to the location of the nodes to place them in the LEF coordinate system.

data:
This variables specify the offset in the Y direction, between the power-grid database data and the LEF file data, in microns. 
 The default value f is 0 microns.

lines: - Given these specifications in the DEF file for a cell: UNITS DISTANCE MICRONS 100 DIEAREA ( -5000 -5000 ) ( 5000 5000 ) and this data in the LEF MACRO for the same cell: ORIGIN ( 0 0 ) SIZE 100 BY 100 the appropriate setting of these variables would be as follows: setvar prpgv_x_offset 50 setvar prpgv_y_offset 50 These settings would shift the power-grid data extracted from the DEF file 50 microns to the right and 50 microns up to align with the LEF macro data.
Ignores disconnected pins when doing effective resisance (reff) analysis. Reff analysis determines the effective resistance from voltage source to an instance pin.

data:
If set to true , disconnected pins will be ignored for reff (effective resistance) analysis. 
 Default: false

lines: - The below variable specifies that disconnected pins should be ignored when doing reff analysis. setvar reff_ignore_disconnected_pins true
This variable determines whether the average power consumption is used as the operating-point value--that is, time 0--for dynamic analysis.

data:
When this variable is set to true , VoltageStorm PE uses the average power consumption as the operating-point value for dynamic analysis. 
 When this variable is set to false , VoltageStorm PE initializes itself at time 0. 
 The default is false .

lines: - In the following example, VoltageStorm PE uses an operating-point value of 0: setvar use_average_power_for_op false
Thisvariable specifies whether coupling capacitance effects between power and ground are taken into account or not.

data:
When this variable is set to true coupling capacitance effects between power and ground are taken into account. 
 When this variable is set to false coupling capacitance effects between power and ground are not taken into account. 
 The default is false .

lines: - In the following example, VoltageStorm PE takes into account the coupling capacitance between powet and ground: setvar use_capacitor adjustment true
This variable determines if the current data should be taken from the cell library.

data:
When this variable is set to true and no cell_power_file was provided, then the current data from the cell library will be used. If a cell_power_file was provided, and this file contains data for the cell in question, then it should override the current value stored in the cell library. 
 When this variable is set to false, then the current data from the cell library will not be used. 
 The default is false .

lines: - In the following example, VoltageStorm PE uses the current data from the power grid view if there is no cell_power_file or the data is not provided in the cell_power_file : setvar use_current_data_from_pgv true

This variable allows you to use an RC database generated in a previous VoltageStorm PE run. You may want to use such a database for testing purposes, to skip the resistance extraction step, or in case the power-grid merging step failed during a VoltageStorm PE run because of missing power-grid views.

Even if this variable is set to true , the resistances are automatically re-extracted if the timestamp on the DEF file is later than the timestamp on the RC database, if the timestamp on the cell library is more recent than the timestamp for the RC database, or if the setting of the pin_char has been changed.

data:
When this variable is set to true, VoltageStorm uses an RC database previously generated by VoltageStorm PE. 
 If specified as false it generates a new RC database. 
 The default is true .

lines: - The following example generates a new RC database: setvar use_existing_rcdb false
This variable determines whether VoltageStorm PE reads the attributes in the GDS file.

data:
When this variable is set to true , VoltageStorm PE reads the attributes in the GDS file. 
 When you set it to false , it does not read the attributes in the GDS file. 
 The default is false .

lines: - In the following case, VoltageStorm PE does not read the attributes in the GDS file: setvar use_gds_annotate_attributes false
This variable controls whether to use port voltage to report Instance Voltage (IV) at the port for instances in the detailed view.

data:
When this variable is set to true , VoltageStorm PE uses the port/interface node voltages to report IV at the port for instances in the detailed view. 
 When you set it to false , VoltageStorm PE does not use the port voltage for IV calculation and reporting. 
 The default is false .

lines: This variable controls the pwl boundary waveform output.

data:
When set to true , it writes the block boundary voltages in the pwl format, into a new *.pwl file. 
 Default : true

lines: This variable disables waveform and iv generation for filler and decap cells.

data:
When set to false , the variable disables waveform and iv generation for filler and decap cells. 
 Default : false

lines: This command specifies location of text labels to the extractor. These labels are used to identify specific nets, port locations, or both.

data: Indicates that the label identifies a port.
Indicates that the label identifies a specific net. The net must have a geometrical shape at the specified location.
Specifies the text of the label.
Specifies the location of the text label, in microns.
Specifies the name of the layer on which the text label resides. This name must be the same as that in the technology file generated by RCgen.

lines: - The following example indicates that a text label called vdd will identify a port located at 8.0, 0.2 on the metal_4 layer: label port vdd 8.0 0.2 METAL_4
- In the next example, a text label called vdd identifies a net with a geometrical shape located at 8.5, 5.8 on the metal_1 layer: label net vdd 8.5 5.8 METAL_1
This command defines layer mapping in order to create physical layers from multiple GDS layers.
The layer commands are forwarded to extraction and influence only the top-level extraction.

data:
Maps an input layer name to a target layer name. This command is used to map the layer name used in the input DEF file to a layer name in the technology file. 
 def_layer_name Specifies the name of the layer in the DEF file. tch_layer_name Specifies the name of the layer in the technology file.

Maps the layer name and the layer type in the GDS file to a physical layer. 
 gds_layer_name Specifies the layer name in the GDS file. gds_layer_number Specifies the layer number in the GDS file. It must be an integer between 0 and 255. gds_data_type Specifies an optional number of the subclass for the GDSII layer. It must be an integer between 0 and 255. [port|text] Determines whether the geometrical shapes on the given layer are used to generate ports or the layer is used to attach text to a specific physical layer.

lines: - The following example maps the DEF layer name, METAL1, to the layer name in the technology file, METAL_1 : layer map METAL1 METAL_1
- The next example specifies the layer name, the layer type, and the data type in the GDS file: layer gds METAL1 16 0

- The last example specifies the layer name, the layer type, and the data type in the GDS file and indicates that the layer be used to attach text to a specific physical layer: layer gds METAL1 16 0 text

This command sets a bias value, in microns, by which to undersize all wires on a defined layer. It must be in the range of -1 to 1. A negative value will make the wire larger. This value is applied to each edge of the wire, decreasing the width by two times the bias value. You can select a different bias value for resistance extraction and capacitance extraction.

data: Specifies the name of the layer containing the wires to be biased.
Specifies the amount, in microns, by which to bias the wires. A positive value undersizes the width of the wire.

lines: - The following example biases each edge of the wires in the m5 layer by two tenths of a micron: layer_bias m5 0.2 The results would resemble the diagram shown in the following figure:
This command specifies the name of the OpenAccess 2.2 database generated by Cadence Innovus or Virtuoso or other applications that is to be used as input to VoltageStorm PE.
You must use the oa_view_name and top_cell commands with this command. See "oa_view_name" for information on the oa_view_name command.

data: Specifies the name of the OpenAccess database.

Specifies the path to the lib.defs file, which contains the path name of the OpenAccess database. This parameter is optional. Use it if the OpenAccess database resides in a directory other than the default directory.

lines: - The following example directs VoltageStorm PE to search for the cpu library in the ../home/cpu_pc788 directory: oa_library cpu ../home/cpu_pc788/lib.defs oa_view_name netlist top_cell NFD2QP
This command specifies the type of view to use when an OpenAccess 2.2 database is input to VoltageStorm PE.
You must use the oa_library and top_cell commands with this command. See "oa_library" for information on the oa_library command.

data: Specifies the type of view. This command has no default, but abstract is usually used.

lines: - This example specifies that the netlist view be used: oa_library cpu oa_view_name netlist top_cell NFD2QP
print iv filename <critical> [best_case | worst_case | average] <effective> < timing_window >

This command places in a specified file the results of filtering all power-grid data for the specified analysis type and the specified area, layer, or selected nodes or resistors. It can also print the worst-case data for the specified analysis types, as well as the time that the worst case occurred. It is similar to the list command, except that it places the data in a file rather than displaying it on the screen. The filter for the analysis type must be set for the print command to report data.
The output data contains the following information:
- The data value of the element or node
- The element name or node name
[WARNING] The output file can be very large for large netlists.

data: Specifies one of the analysis types shown below.
Specifies the name of the output file.

The command " print -iv filename" is for printing instance-based supply voltage data. This data contains instance-specific IR drop information suitable for use in Tempus. 
 Note: When setvar save_voltage_waveforms true environment variable is set before a print iv command, a binary file is created, not ascii instance supply voltage file. If this variable is not set before a print iv command, then the output will be an ascii instance supply voltage file. 
 The switching-window is the time interval during which non-leakage currents flow into the power and ground nets or when the gate is switching. In VCD flow the time is where a specific pin/net toggle is specified in the VCD file. PowerMeter then reads this information, computes the current waveform corresponding to the toggling and puts this waveform at the time specified in the VCD file. The switching window is the time interval of the current waveform due to the toggling. Similiarly in vectorless flow, when a pin toggles , PowerMeter computes a current waveform corresponding to the switching. The difference between vectorless flow and VCD flow is that in vectorless flow the toggling time for the pins are determined by Powermeter while in VCD flow the toggling time is given by the VCD file. 
 <critical> prints the instance voltage values at critical instances (Those with a negative slack value).This is important for timing aware decap analysis. 
 Using best_case provides an option to write the best-case supply voltage per instance over the time intervals defined by timing_window . The best-case supply voltage for an instance is the pin voltage of the connnected pin with the minimum IR -drop or ground-bounce over the timing-window time intervals (timing_window is required). 
 If none of the parameters <critical> <best_case | worst_case | average> < timing_window > is specified then the default dynamic behavior is the same as print iv filename worst_case . Since a timing window is not specified, the value may be too pessimistic for IR-drop aware delay calculation (i.e. Tempus).
Generates an ASCII report of all power gate cell instances. See discussion below.

Plots the available decoupling capacitance in the specified area. The available capacitance is calculated based on the available filler cells in the specified area and their matching decoupling cap cells.
Prints the decoupling density, which is the amount of decoupling capacitance in a given area of the design.

Prints the decoupling capacitance effectiveness measured by the ratio peak IR drop divided by the average IR drop. This option applies only to dynamic analysis and generates a warning message if only static current data is available. VoltageStorm PE calculates the average IR drop over the whole transient analysis.
Prints the decap required, which is the suggested amount of decap to add to fix IR-drop violations.
Prints the filler-cell density, which is the number of filler cells per given area.
Prints jrms current density data based on Irms current. Only supported for dynamic.

Prints instance-based supply voltage data. This data contains instance-specific IR drop information suitable for use in Tempus. 
 In order for VoltageStorm PE to print the file of instance-based supply voltage data, the print iv command must be preceded by either the analyze iv or filter iv auto command. 
 The format of this file is: 
 INSTANCESUPPLY no_inst bus_name nom_volt [P | G] 
 instance voltage cell [ power_pin [ signal_pins ]] 
 ...
